question_analysis.skill_tested: Predict the site of electrophilic aromatic substitution on a polysubstituted benzene ring by ranking substituents and applying the directing effect of the dominant activator
question_analysis.disguise: Multiple substituents with competing and conflicting directing effects obscure which group actually controls the outcome, and some preferred positions are already blocked by existing substituents
question_analysis.recognition_cue: A benzene ring carrying two or more substituents of different types — some activating, some deactivating — where you must choose a single attack site rather than simply recall one group's rule

prior_knowledge_needed: Activating groups (substituents that donate electron density into the ring, making it more reactive) are ortho-para directors
Deactivating groups (substituents that withdraw electron density from the ring, making it less reactive) are meta directors, except halogens which are deactivating but ortho-para directors

Relative activating strength hierarchy: strong activators (–OH, –NH2, –NHR) > moderate activators (–OR, –NHCOR, oxygen-attached ester) > weak activators (alkyl groups like –CH3) > weak deactivators (halogens) > moderate deactivators (–NHCOR carbonyl side, –CN, carbonyl-attached ester) > strong deactivators (–NO2, –NR3+, –CF3)
When multiple substituents are present, the most strongly activating group overrides the others and controls where the electrophile attacks

Ortho and para positions on the ring are defined relative to the directing substituent; if those positions are already occupied by other groups, the next available ortho or para position becomes the preferred site
Molecular symmetry can make two positions equivalent, collapsing them into a single expected product

solution_steps.do_this: Classify every substituent on the ring by its activating/deactivating strength and its directing type (ortho-para vs. meta)
solution_steps.why: The rule is: electrophilic aromatic substitution (EAS — attack of an electron-hungry reagent on an electron-rich aromatic ring) rate and position are governed entirely by how substituents redistribute ring electron density. You must know each group's category before you can rank them, because only the strongest activator's preferences matter in the end.

vocab.electrophilic aromatic substitution (EAS: a reaction in which an electron-seeking species replaces a hydrogen on a benzene ring while the ring's aromaticity is preserved
vocab.activating group: a substituent that pushes electron density into the ring, making it more reactive toward electrophiles and directing attack to the ortho and para positions
vocab.deactivating group: a substituent that pulls electron density away from the ring, making it less reactive and generally directing attack to the meta position

solution_steps.do_this: Identify the single most strongly activating group among all substituents; declare that group the directing controller and ignore the directing preferences of weaker or deactivating groups for position prediction
solution_steps.why: The rule is: the substituent that most increases ring electron density dominates because EAS occurs preferentially where electron density is highest. Deactivating groups reduce reactivity everywhere but cannot override an activator's positional preference; the activator simply wins the competition.

vocab.directing controller: the substituent whose positional preference determines where the incoming electrophile attaches, because it contributes the most electron density to specific ring positions
vocab.ortho-para director: a group that concentrates extra electron density at the two ortho positions (immediately adjacent) and the one para position (directly across) relative to itself on the ring

solution_steps.do_this: Map out the ortho and para positions relative to the controlling activator, then check whether any of those positions are already occupied by existing substituents
solution_steps.why: The rule is: an electrophile can only attack a position that bears a hydrogen atom — if a substituent already sits at an ortho or para site, that site is blocked. You must move to the next available ortho or para position. When all but one preferred site is blocked, the answer is unambiguous.

vocab.blocked position: a ring carbon that already carries a substituent instead of a hydrogen, making it unavailable for electrophilic attack

solution_steps.do_this: Check the symmetry of the molecule to determine whether two candidate positions are chemically identical, and if so, consolidate them into one predicted major product site
solution_steps.why: The rule is: symmetry-equivalent positions produce the same product regardless of which one is attacked, so what looks like two sites is actually one outcome. Failing to recognize symmetry leads to overcounting products and misreading the selectivity of the reaction.

vocab.symmetry-equivalent positions: two or more ring sites that are mirror images or rotational equivalents of each other, meaning attack at either gives an identical compound

solution_steps.do_this: State the final predicted substitution site(s) in terms of their relationship to the controlling activator, and note any cases where steric bulk (physical crowding near a position) might further disfavor one of two otherwise equivalent sites
solution_steps.why: The rule is: electronic effects determine which set of positions is preferred, but steric effects (physical crowding by large neighboring groups) can differentiate between two electronically similar sites. Reporting the answer in relational terms (ortho or para to group X) rather than absolute numbering keeps the reasoning transferable to any substitution pattern.

vocab.steric effect: the influence of physical size on reactivity — large groups near a reaction site physically block the approach of an incoming electrophile, reducing reaction rate at that position

question_analysis.skill_tested: Predict the preferred site of electrophilic aromatic substitution by ranking substituent directing strength and applying steric reasoning to break ties among equivalent positions
question_analysis.disguise: Each ring carries multiple substituents with potentially competing directing effects, forcing the student to rank activators/deactivators rather than simply apply one group's rule
question_analysis.recognition_cue: A benzene ring bearing two or more substituents where at least one is an oxygen-containing group (–OH, –OMe, –NO2) — the student must ask 'which group wins the directing contest, and which of the surviving positions is least hindered?'

prior_knowledge_needed: Ortho/para directors are electron-donating groups that concentrate electron density at the ortho and para positions relative to themselves
Meta directors are electron-withdrawing groups that leave the meta positions as the least electron-poor sites
Activating strength ranking: –OH and –OR are strong-to-moderate activators; halogens are weak activators; –NO2 is a deactivator
When two substituents conflict, the more strongly activating group overrides the directing influence of the weaker or deactivating group
Steric hindrance (physical bulk blocking approach of the electrophile) disfavors reaction at positions flanked by large groups, even if those positions are electronically activated

solution_steps.do_this: Identify every substituent on the ring and classify each as an activator or deactivator, then rank them by activating strength to determine which one controls the regiochemistry
solution_steps.why: The rule is: the substituent that most strongly donates electron density into the ring sets the directing pattern, because electrophiles attack wherever electron density is highest. A strong activator overpowers a weak activator or a deactivator on the same ring.

vocab.activator: a substituent that donates electron density into the aromatic ring, making it more reactive toward electrophiles
vocab.deactivator: a substituent that withdraws electron density from the aromatic ring, making it less reactive and directing incoming electrophiles to the meta position

solution_steps.do_this: Using the dominant activating group as the reference point, map out all positions that are ortho or para to it, then cross off any that are already occupied by an existing substituent
solution_steps.why: The rule is: ortho/para directors channel electrophilic attack to the two ortho positions and the one para position relative to themselves. Any position already bearing a substituent is physically unavailable for a new bond, so it must be removed from the candidate list before proceeding.

vocab.ortho position: a ring carbon immediately adjacent (one bond away) to the reference substituent
vocab.para position: the ring carbon directly across the ring (four bonds away) from the reference substituent

solution_steps.do_this: Check whether the remaining candidate position(s) is also activated by any other substituent on the ring (convergent activation), and flag any position that is flanked by bulky groups as sterically disfavored
solution_steps.why: The rule is: a position that is simultaneously activated by two different substituents receives an additive electronic benefit and is even more reactive. Conversely, steric hindrance (physical bulk) slows the approach of the incoming electrophile regardless of electronic activation, so the electronically activated but less hindered position wins.

vocab.convergent activation: the situation where two different substituents on the same ring both direct the electrophile to the same position, reinforcing each other's effect
vocab.steric hindrance: the physical blocking of a reaction site caused by large nearby groups, which raises the energy cost for the electrophile to reach that carbon

solution_steps.do_this: Among all surviving activated, unoccupied positions, select the one that is both electronically favored and least sterically crowded as the predicted site of electrophilic aromatic substitution
solution_steps.why: The rule is: reaction rate depends on both electronic activation (lowers the transition-state energy) and steric accessibility (allows the electrophile to physically reach the carbon). The position that optimizes both criteria is the kinetically preferred site.

vocab.kinetically preferred site: the position at which the reaction proceeds fastest under normal conditions, determined by the lowest-energy transition state

question_analysis.skill_tested: Predict the site of electrophilic aromatic substitution on a disubstituted benzene ring by ranking competing directing groups and applying steric reasoning to choose among symmetry-equivalent positions
question_analysis.disguise: The problem embeds familiar EAS (electrophilic aromatic substitution) logic inside a real pharmaceutical synthesis context, and introduces chlorosulfonic acid as the electrophile — a reagent students have not seen before — to obscure that the core task is simply positional prediction
question_analysis.recognition_cue: A benzene ring bearing two different substituents plus an unfamiliar electrophilic reagent: immediately flag that the new reagent is irrelevant to position prediction, then focus entirely on which substituent wins the directing contest and which open positions survive steric screening

prior_knowledge_needed: Electron-donating groups (EDGs) — substituents that push electron density into the benzene ring through lone pairs or hyperconjugation — direct incoming electrophiles to the ortho and para positions relative to themselves
Electron-withdrawing groups (EWGs) — substituents that pull electron density away from the ring through resonance or induction — direct electrophiles to the meta position relative to themselves
When two substituents compete, the stronger activator (more electron-donating group) dominates and its ortho/para positions are the ones that react
Alkoxy groups (–OR, oxygen attached directly to the ring) are moderate activators that donate lone-pair electron density into the ring via resonance

A conjugated heterocyclic ring (a nitrogen-containing ring whose pi system is continuous with the benzene ring) can withdraw electron density from benzene through resonance, making it a deactivating substituent
Steric hindrance (physical crowding near a reactive site that slows or blocks attack) disfavors the ortho position relative to a large substituent, shifting preference toward the para position
In EAS the incoming electrophile replaces a hydrogen atom at the site of attack; the identity of the electrophile affects reactivity but not the regiochemical prediction

solution_steps.do_this: Classify each substituent on the ring as an electron donor or electron withdrawor, and identify which one is the stronger activator
solution_steps.why: The rule is: in EAS on a polysubstituted ring, the substituent that donates electron density most strongly into the ring controls where substitution occurs, because the carbons that are most electron-rich attract the electrophile most effectively. The alkoxy group (an oxygen-containing group bonded directly to the ring) donates lone pairs into the ring through resonance, making it a moderate activating, ortho/para director. The nitrogen-containing heterocyclic group attached to the ring has a conjugated pi system that withdraws electron density from the ring by resonance, making it a deactivating, meta director. Because the alkoxy group is the activator and the heterocyclic group is the deactivator, the alkoxy group is the dominant director.

vocab.activating substituent: a group that donates electron density to the benzene ring, lowering the activation energy for EAS and directing attack to its ortho and para positions
vocab.deactivating substituent: a group that withdraws electron density from the benzene ring, raising the activation energy for EAS and directing attack to its meta positions
vocab.resonance donation: the sharing of lone-pair or pi electrons between a substituent and the ring through overlapping p-orbitals, increasing electron density at specific ring carbons

solution_steps.do_this: Map out every ortho and para position relative to the dominant director (the alkoxy group), then eliminate any that are already occupied by a substituent
solution_steps.why: The rule is: an ortho/para director concentrates the incoming electrophile at the two ortho carbons (immediately adjacent to the directing group) and the one para carbon (directly across the ring). Any carbon already bearing a substituent cannot accept a second group, so those positions are automatically removed from consideration. On a 1,2-disubstituted ring the two substituents together block several positions; after eliminating occupied carbons, only the open ortho and para sites of the alkoxy group remain as viable targets.

vocab.ortho position: a ring carbon directly adjacent (one bond away) to the substituent being used as the reference point
vocab.para position: the ring carbon directly opposite (three bonds away through the ring) to the reference substituent

solution_steps.do_this: Among the remaining open positions that are ortho or para to the alkoxy group, apply steric reasoning to select the single most reactive site
solution_steps.why: The rule is: steric hindrance (physical crowding) around a carbon slows the approach of an incoming electrophile; when two positions are electronically similar, the less hindered one reacts preferentially. The ortho position is flanked by the alkoxy group itself, creating close-range steric crowding. The para position is farther from both substituents and therefore less hindered. Consequently, even though both sites are electronically activated, the para carbon is the preferred site of chlorosulfonylation.

vocab.steric hindrance: the slowing or blocking of a reaction at a particular carbon because nearby atoms or groups physically crowd the space that the incoming reagent must occupy

solution_steps.do_this: Draw the product by replacing the hydrogen at the para position (relative to the alkoxy group) with the –SO2Cl group, keeping all other ring substituents and their connectivity unchanged
solution_steps.why: The rule is: in every EAS mechanism, the net result is substitution of one aromatic hydrogen for the incoming electrophile — the ring aromaticity (the stable, delocalized pi electron system) is restored by loss of a proton in the final step. Here the electrophile is the –SO2Cl unit delivered by chlorosulfonic acid. Placing –SO2Cl at the para carbon of the alkoxy group gives the major product consistent with both the electronic directing effect and the steric preference established in the previous steps.

vocab.electrophilic aromatic substitution (EAS: a reaction class in which an electrophile replaces a hydrogen on an aromatic ring, with aromaticity fully restored in the final proton-loss step
vocab.chlorosulfonyl group (–SO2Cl): a sulfur-containing functional group bearing both two oxygens and one chlorine on sulfur, introduced here as the electrophilic fragment that attaches to the ring

question_analysis.skill_tested: Determine whether a blocking group is needed by predicting the major regiochemical outcome of electrophilic aromatic substitution and comparing it to the target position
question_analysis.disguise: Four separate aromatic starting materials with different substituent combinations are presented without explicit reagents, requiring the student to judge sufficiency of direct substitution for each case
question_analysis.recognition_cue: A target aromatic compound is shown alongside a starting material — if the electrophile would land at the wrong ring position by default, a blocking group (a temporary substituent used to occupy an unwanted position) is required; if it lands at the correct position naturally, no blocking group is needed

prior_knowledge_needed: Ortho-para directors vs. meta directors: substituents that donate electron density activate the ring and steer incoming electrophiles to the ortho (adjacent) and para (opposite) positions
Activating vs. deactivating groups: electron-donating groups speed up electrophilic aromatic substitution; electron-withdrawing groups slow it down
Steric effects in EAS: bulky groups near a reactive site physically block approach of an electrophile, making para substitution favored over ortho even when both are electronically accessible
Blocking group strategy: a sulfonic acid group (–SO3H) can be installed at a position to occupy it temporarily, then removed after the desired electrophile is placed elsewhere
When two substituents are present, the stronger activator controls regiochemistry (directing effects); when both direct to the same site, that site is doubly activated

solution_steps.do_this: Identify every substituent on the starting ring and classify each as an ortho-para director or meta director, and as strongly activating, weakly activating, or deactivating
solution_steps.why: The rule is: the electronic nature and geometry of existing substituents together determine which ring positions are most reactive. Knowing the classification tells you where the electrophile will land without any intervention.

vocab.ortho-para director: a substituent that donates electron density into the ring, making the positions directly adjacent to it and directly opposite to it the most electron-rich and therefore most reactive toward electrophiles
vocab.deactivating group: a substituent that withdraws electron density from the ring, making it less reactive overall toward electrophilic attack

solution_steps.do_this: Apply steric reasoning to rank the available ortho and para positions: if the directing group is bulky, decide whether the para position will be strongly preferred over ortho positions
solution_steps.why: The rule is: large substituents create steric hindrance (physical crowding) at nearby positions, so even though ortho positions are electronically activated, the electrophile preferentially attacks the less hindered para position. This shifts the regiochemical outcome without any directing-group change.

vocab.steric hindrance: the physical blocking of a reaction site caused by the three-dimensional bulk of nearby atoms or groups, making attack at that site energetically unfavorable

solution_steps.do_this: For rings bearing two substituents, determine whether both groups direct to the same candidate positions or to conflicting ones, and identify which remaining open positions are most accessible
solution_steps.why: The rule is: when two substituents are present, the stronger activator overrides the weaker one in controlling regiochemistry; when both direct to the same position, that position is doubly activated and becomes the overwhelmingly preferred site. Recognizing agreement vs. conflict is essential before deciding whether blocking is needed.

vocab.cooperative directing: a situation where two substituents on the same ring both activate the same position, reinforcing each other's effect and making that position extremely reactive

solution_steps.do_this: Compare the predicted major product position to the position required in the target molecule, and conclude whether direct substitution gives the desired regiochemistry or not
solution_steps.why: The rule is: a blocking group is only warranted when the electrophile would naturally attack the wrong position. If the major predicted position matches the target, direct substitution suffices; if it does not match, a blocking group must temporarily occupy the unwanted site to redirect attack.

vocab.blocking group: a substituent — commonly a sulfonate group (–SO3H) introduced by sulfonation) — installed at an undesired reactive position to prevent electrophilic attack there, then removed after the desired substitution is complete

solution_steps.do_this: Render a final verdict for each case: state which position the electrophile attacks by default, whether that matches the target, and therefore whether a blocking group is or is not required
solution_steps.why: The rule is: synthesis planning requires an explicit comparison between predicted outcome and desired outcome before committing to a route. Skipping this comparison leads to selecting unnecessary steps or missing essential ones.

vocab.regiochemistry: the specific position on a molecule where a reaction occurs when more than one position is possible

solution_text_raw:
18.23.Analyze the starting material. The OH group is a strong activator and anortho-paradirector. Indeed, it is such a
strong activator that bromination conditions will likely give a tribrominated product (with bromination occurring at the
paraposition and bothorthopositions), even at room temperature. Also, even if monobromination could be carried out
cleanly, the product mixture would likely contain only some of the more sterically hindered (desired) product along
with a substantial amount of theparaisomer that would likely be the major product.Our strategy will be to block
738CHAPTER 18
some reactive positions, brominate, and then unblock.As we have seen, reactive positions can be blocked via
sulfonation, as shown here:
The first sulfonic acid group is installed quickly, and it deactivates the ring somewhat, so the second sulfonation is
expected to occur more slowly (and possibly require a higher temperature). A third sulfonation would require an even
higher temperature, so there is likely an optimum temperature at which the disulfonated product can be obtained in high
yield (and indeed, this is the case!).The regiochemistry of the second sulfonation is controlled by theortho-para-
directing effect of the OH group (although themeta-directing SO3H group also directs to the same location). This
double blocking can be carried out in one synthetic step with excess fuming sulfuric acid, but is shown stepwise above
for clarity.
Bromination at the only remaining reactive site, followed by desulfonation to remove the two blocking groups, yields
the desired product:

question_analysis.skill_tested: Design a multi-step synthesis that uses temporary blocking groups to control regiochemistry on a highly activated aromatic ring
question_analysis.disguise: The problem looks like a simple one-step bromination, but the strongly activating hydroxyl group makes selective mono-bromination at the ortho position nearly impossible without a protecting-group strategy
question_analysis.recognition_cue: Phenol as starting material + need for a specific mono-substituted product at a position that competes with a more favored site → signals that blocking groups are required before electrophilic substitution

prior_knowledge_needed: Electrophilic aromatic substitution (EAS): a reaction where an electrophile replaces a hydrogen on an aromatic ring
Activating and directing effects of the hydroxyl group: OH strongly donates electron density into the ring and directs incoming electrophiles to the ortho and para positions
Sulfonation as a reversible EAS reaction: adding SO3H to the ring with fuming sulfuric acid, then removing it by heating with dilute aqueous acid
Steric and electronic competition between ortho and para positions on a phenol ring
The concept of a blocking group: a substituent temporarily placed on a reactive site to prevent unwanted substitution there, then removed after the desired reaction

solution_steps.do_this: Assess why direct bromination of phenol will not give the desired product cleanly
solution_steps.why: The rule is: a strongly activating substituent makes every ortho and para position highly reactive, so electrophilic substitution tends to occur multiple times and preferentially at the para position (less steric hindrance). Applying this rule here means that brominating phenol directly will likely produce tribrominated product and, even if monobromination were achieved, the para isomer (less hindered) would dominate over the desired ortho isomer. Recognizing this failure mode is the first expert move.

vocab.activating group: a substituent that donates electron density to the aromatic ring, making it react faster with electrophiles
vocab.ortho/para director: a substituent that steers incoming electrophiles specifically to the positions adjacent to it (ortho) or directly across the ring (para)

solution_steps.do_this: Identify which reactive positions must be temporarily blocked and choose sulfonation as the blocking tool
solution_steps.why: The rule is: sulfonation is a reversible EAS reaction, meaning the sulfonic acid group (SO3H) can be installed under forcing conditions and removed later by hydrolysis with dilute acid and heat — it acts as a placeholder that occupies a ring position without permanently altering the molecule. Expert reasoning applies this rule by selecting the para position and the non-target ortho position for sulfonation, so the only open site left on the ring is the desired ortho position. Sulfonation is chosen specifically because no other common EAS reaction is cleanly reversible under mild aqueous conditions.

vocab.sulfonation: an EAS reaction that attaches a sulfonic acid group (–SO3H) to an aromatic ring using fuming sulfuric acid (SO3 dissolved in H2SO4)
vocab.blocking group: a substituent installed deliberately to occupy a reactive site and prevent substitution there, then removed after the desired transformation

solution_steps.do_this: Plan the sequence: install two sulfonic acid blocking groups, then brominate, then remove both blocking groups
solution_steps.why: The rule is: EAS reactions are sequential — each substituent already on the ring influences where the next one lands. Applying this sequentially means (1) the first sulfonation places SO3H at one of the positions that must be blocked (directed by OH); (2) the second sulfonation, slightly slower because the first SO3H partially deactivates the ring, places SO3H at the remaining undesired reactive site; (3) with both competing sites occupied, bromination by Br2 (a moderate electrophile sufficient for the still-activated ring) lands at the one remaining open position, which is the target ortho site; (4) heating the product with dilute aqueous acid removes both SO3H groups by reversing sulfonation, revealing the clean ortho-bromophenol product.

vocab.desulfonation: the removal of a sulfonic acid group from an aromatic ring by treating with dilute aqueous acid and heat, regenerating the C–H bond
vocab.regiochemistry: the selectivity of a reaction for one specific position on a molecule over other possible positions

solution_steps.do_this: Verify that the regiochemistry at each step is consistent with the directing effects of all substituents already on the ring
solution_steps.why: The rule is: when multiple substituents are present on a ring, their directing effects are additive when they agree and the stronger director wins when they conflict. Confirming this at each stage ensures the synthesis is internally consistent: the OH group (ortho/para director) and the first SO3H group (meta director, meaning it avoids ortho/para to itself) both point toward the same available site for the second sulfonation, reinforcing selectivity. Similarly, after both blocking groups are in place, only one ring position remains available for bromination, making the regiochemical outcome unambiguous.

vocab.meta director: a substituent that withdraws electron density from the ortho and para positions, so incoming electrophiles are steered to the meta position (two carbons away) instead
vocab.additive directing effects: when two substituents on a ring both favor the same position for incoming electrophile attack, selectivity at that position is enhanced

question_analysis.skill_tested: Select the correct electrophilic aromatic substitution (EAS) reagent set for each target functional group attached to benzene
question_analysis.disguise: The problem looks like a simple reagent-lookup exercise, but several targets require multi-step sequences or detours around carbocation rearrangement problems
question_analysis.recognition_cue: Benzene ring as starting material plus a target substituent — immediately triggers the EAS reagent toolkit, and any straight-chain alkyl group longer than ethyl triggers the rearrangement-avoidance detour

prior_knowledge_needed: Electrophilic aromatic substitution (EAS) mechanism: an electron-rich benzene ring attacks an electrophile, ultimately replacing one ring hydrogen with a new group
Lewis acid catalysts (AlCl3, FeBr3, AlBr3) activate molecular halogens or alkyl/acyl halides by generating a highly reactive electrophile
Friedel-Crafts alkylation: installing an alkyl group on benzene using an alkyl halide plus AlCl3
Friedel-Crafts acylation: installing a carbonyl (C=O) group on benzene using an acyl chloride plus AlCl3 — does NOT allow carbocation rearrangement because acylium ions are stabilized
Clemmensen reduction: converting a ketone (C=O) directly attached to a ring down to a CH2 group using zinc amalgam and HCl
Carbocation rearrangement: a primary carbocation shifts a hydrogen or alkyl group to become a more stable secondary or tertiary carbocation, giving an unwanted product
Nitration reagent: concentrated HNO3 plus concentrated H2SO4 installs a nitro group (–NO2) on the ring
Reduction of a nitro group to an amino group (–NH2) using H2/Pd or Fe/HCl, because direct amination of benzene is not a taught single-step method
Halogenation: Cl2/AlCl3 gives chlorobenzene; Br2/FeBr3 (or AlBr3) gives bromobenzene

solution_steps.do_this: Classify each target substituent into one of four categories: halogen, nitro/amino, straight-chain alkyl, or methyl/branched alkyl
solution_steps.why: The rule is: each category maps to a distinct EAS strategy or reagent set, so grouping first prevents applying the wrong strategy. Halogens use X2/Lewis acid; nitro uses mixed acid; amino requires a two-step nitration then reduction; alkyl groups require Friedel-Crafts alkylation unless rearrangement is a risk.

vocab.substituent: any atom or group of atoms that replaces a hydrogen on the benzene ring

solution_steps.do_this: For the halogen targets (chlorobenzene and bromobenzene), assign the matching diatomic halogen plus the appropriate Lewis acid catalyst
solution_steps.why: The rule is: molecular halogens (Cl2 or Br2) alone are not electrophilic enough to attack benzene; a Lewis acid (AlCl3 for chlorine, FeBr3 or AlBr3 for bromine) polarizes the halogen–halogen bond, generating the powerful electrophile needed for ring attack. Matching the Lewis acid to the halogen matters because AlCl3 is the standard for chlorination while iron-based catalysts are typical for bromination.

vocab.Lewis acid: a substance that accepts an electron pair — here it grabs onto one halogen atom and makes the other halogen atom strongly electron-deficient and reactive

solution_steps.do_this: For aniline (aminobenzene), recognize that no direct single-step amination exists and plan a two-step sequence: first nitration, then reduction of the nitro group
solution_steps.why: The rule is: amino groups (–NH2) are too nucleophilic (electron-donating, electron-pair donating) to survive the electrophilic conditions needed to attach them to benzene in one step; therefore the standard workaround installs the oxidized analog (–NO2) via HNO3/H2SO4 and then reduces it. This indirect route reliably delivers the amino group without destroying the ring.

vocab.nitro group: a –NO2 group attached to the ring, which is the oxidized, stable precursor that gets reduced to the –NH2 amino group

solution_steps.do_this: For the propyl group target, identify why direct Friedel-Crafts alkylation with n-propyl chloride fails, then plan the acylation–reduction detour
solution_steps.why: The rule is: primary carbocations (formed from n-propyl chloride under AlCl3) rearrange almost immediately to more stable secondary carbocations, so the product would be isopropylbenzene rather than propylbenzene. To avoid this, perform Friedel-Crafts acylation with propanoyl chloride (an acyl chloride — a carbonyl chloride) and AlCl3 to install a three-carbon acyl group without rearrangement, then use Clemmensen reduction (Zn(Hg)/HCl) to remove the carbonyl oxygen and give the desired straight-chain propyl group.

vocab.acylium ion: a stabilized carbocation that carries its positive charge on a carbon directly attached to a C=O group, which prevents the geometry needed for a hydride or alkyl shift rearrangement
vocab.Clemmensen reduction: a reaction using zinc amalgam in concentrated hydrochloric acid that converts a ring-attached ketone carbonyl directly to a CH2 group

solution_steps.do_this: For toluene (methylbenzene), confirm that a methyl group is safely installed by direct Friedel-Crafts alkylation with methyl chloride and AlCl3, since a methyl carbocation cannot rearrange to a more stable species
solution_steps.why: The rule is: carbocation rearrangement requires a neighboring carbon that can donate a hydride or alkyl group to give a more stable ion — a methyl carbocation has no such neighbor, so rearrangement is impossible and the single-step Friedel-Crafts alkylation is reliable here.

vocab.Friedel-Crafts alkylation: an EAS reaction in which an alkyl halide activated by a Lewis acid delivers an alkyl group to the benzene ring

question_analysis.skill_tested: Design a multi-step synthesis from benzene by choosing the correct order of electrophilic aromatic substitution reactions based on directing effects and avoiding carbocation rearrangements
question_analysis.disguise: Four separate target molecules are shown as finished structures, hiding the retrosynthetic logic and the sequencing constraints that govern which group must be installed first
question_analysis.recognition_cue: A polysubstituted benzene ring target where two or more substituents must end up in specific relative positions (ortho/para/meta) signals that you must work backwards, asking which group already on the ring would direct each incoming group to exactly the right spot

prior_knowledge_needed: Ortho/para directors vs. meta directors: activating groups (alkyl, amino, OH) send incoming electrophiles to the ortho and para positions; deactivating groups with lone pairs (halogens) send them ortho/para but weakly; strongly deactivating groups (nitro, carbonyl) send them to meta

Friedel-Crafts alkylation limitation: carbocation rearrangements mean you cannot reliably install a straight-chain alkyl group longer than two carbons directly; use acylation then reduction (Clemmensen or Wolff-Kishner) instead
Clemmensen reduction: converts a ketone carbonyl attached to a ring into a methylene (CH2) group using zinc amalgam and HCl, giving the unrearranged alkyl chain
Nitro group reduction: treatment of an aryl nitro group with H2/Pd or Fe/HCl converts it to a primary amino (NH2) group
Blocking group strategy: a sulfonic acid group can be installed temporarily to occupy a position, block it from substitution, and later be removed by hydrolysis with steam/acid
Sulfonation reversibility: adding fuming sulfuric acid installs an SO3H group; heating with dilute aqueous acid removes it — making it useful as a removable placeholder
Steric effects on EAS: bulky groups already on the ring can steer an incoming electrophile away from the sterically crowded position even when electronics alone might allow it

solution_steps.do_this: Work backwards from each target: identify every substituent, note its position relative to the others, and classify each as an ortho/para director or meta director
solution_steps.why: The rule is: the regiochemical outcome of electrophilic aromatic substitution (EAS — a reaction where an electrophile replaces a ring hydrogen) is controlled by whatever substituent is already on the ring at the moment of attack. Therefore, to get a substituent in the right place you must ensure the correct directing group is present before that substitution step. Retrosynthetic analysis (working backward from product to starting materials) reveals which group had to be installed first.

vocab.retrosynthetic analysis: a problem-solving strategy where you start with the target molecule and mentally disconnect bonds one at a time, asking what simpler precursor could give rise to it
vocab.ortho/para director: a substituent that concentrates electron density at the positions flanking it (ortho) and directly across the ring (para), making those spots most reactive toward electrophiles
vocab.meta director: a substituent that withdraws electron density from the ortho and para positions, leaving the meta positions relatively more reactive

solution_steps.do_this: For each target, determine the installation order by asking: if group A is put on first, does it direct group B to exactly the correct position? If yes, that order is viable; if not, reverse the order or plan a blocking group
solution_steps.why: The rule is: you can only exploit a group's directing effect if that group is already on the ring when the next electrophile arrives. Changing the order of steps changes which positions are activated or deactivated at each stage. For example, an alkyl group (ortho/para activator) installed first lets a subsequent nitration land para to it; a nitro group installed first would instead send bromination to the meta position, which is wrong if para is needed.

vocab.activating group: a substituent that donates electron density into the ring, making the ring react faster with electrophiles than plain benzene does
vocab.deactivating group: a substituent that withdraws electron density from the ring, making the ring react more slowly and directing incoming electrophiles to the meta positions

solution_steps.do_this: Check whether any required alkyl group is longer than two carbons or is a secondary/tertiary carbon chain; if so, replace direct Friedel-Crafts alkylation with Friedel-Crafts acylation followed by carbonyl reduction
solution_steps.why: The rule is: carbocations (positively charged carbon intermediates) rearrange to the most stable structure whenever a lower-energy pathway exists. A primary carbocation formed from a straight-chain alkyl halide will shift to a secondary or tertiary carbocation before it attacks the ring, giving the wrong product. Acylation uses an acylium ion (a resonance-stabilized electrophile that cannot rearrange) so it installs the carbon skeleton faithfully; subsequent Clemmensen reduction then removes the oxygen to give the desired alkyl substituent.

vocab.Friedel-Crafts acylation: an EAS reaction where an acid chloride and a Lewis acid catalyst generate an acylium ion that attacks the ring, placing a carbonyl-containing group on the ring
vocab.carbocation rearrangement: a structural reorganization where a positively charged carbon moves a hydrogen or alkyl group to itself from a neighboring carbon, converting a less stable carbocation into a more stable one

solution_steps.do_this: When the target requires an amino group, plan nitration first (to install a nitro group as a controllable placeholder), use the nitro group's meta-directing or the existing ring substituent's ortho/para-directing effect to set regiochemistry, then reduce the nitro group to an amino group at the end
solution_steps.why: The rule is: a free amino group (NH2) is so strongly activating that it makes the ring extremely reactive and difficult to control; introducing it late avoids over-reaction and allows precise positional control earlier in the sequence. The nitro group, by contrast, is stable to most EAS conditions and can be reduced cleanly to the amine only when you choose to do so.

vocab.nitration: an EAS reaction using a mixture of concentrated nitric and sulfuric acids that places a nitro (NO2) group on the ring
vocab.Clemmensen reduction: treatment of a ring-attached ketone with zinc amalgam in concentrated HCl, converting the C=O group into a CH2 group without disturbing the ring

solution_steps.do_this: For targets where the needed regiochemistry cannot be achieved without a blocking group, plan sulfonation to occupy a position temporarily, complete the remaining substitutions exploiting the directing effects of the permanent substituents, then remove the sulfonic acid group by heating with dilute aqueous acid
solution_steps.why: The rule is: a sulfonic acid group (SO3H) attached to an aromatic ring is reversible — it can be added and removed under controlled conditions — making it the standard blocking group in EAS synthesis. Placing it at a position you want to protect prevents an electrophile from landing there; once no longer needed, it is cleaved away, revealing a free ring hydrogen at that position. This strategy is only necessary when the desired product's substitution pattern cannot be accessed by simple sequential EAS without unwanted isomers forming.

vocab.blocking group: a substituent installed temporarily to prevent reaction at a specific ring position, then removed after the desired substitution elsewhere has been completed
vocab.sulfonation: an EAS reaction using fuming sulfuric acid (H2SO4 containing dissolved SO3) that places a sulfonic acid group on the ring; the reaction is reversible upon heating with dilute aqueous acid

question_analysis.skill_tested: Plan a multi-step synthesis by working backward (retrosynthetically) from a target molecule, correctly sequencing substituent installations based on their directing effects
question_analysis.disguise: The problem looks like a simple two-group installation, but it hides the critical ordering constraint: putting groups on in the wrong sequence gives the wrong regiochemistry (positional pattern) on the ring
question_analysis.recognition_cue: Target molecule has two substituents on a benzene ring with a specific positional relationship — one is an electron-withdrawing nitro group and one is an electron-donating amide group — plus a CF3 group already present on the starting material, signaling that directing effects must govern the installation sequence

prior_knowledge_needed: Electrophilic aromatic substitution (EAS): a reaction where an electron-hungry species replaces a hydrogen on a benzene ring, guided by existing substituents
Ortho/para directors: substituents that donate electron density into the ring and steer incoming electrophiles to positions adjacent to or directly across from them
Meta directors: substituents that withdraw electron density from the ring and force incoming electrophiles to the position between the two groups flanking each carbon
Nitration: introduction of a nitro group (–NO2) onto a benzene ring using a nitrating mixture
Reduction of nitro groups: converting –NO2 to –NH2 (an amine) using a reducing agent such as Fe/HCl or catalytic hydrogenation
Amide bond formation: reaction of an amine (–NH2) with an acyl chloride (RCOCl) in the presence of a base like pyridine to give an amide (–NHC(=O)R)
Retrosynthetic analysis: the strategy of reasoning backward from the target molecule, disconnecting bonds to reveal simpler precursor molecules

solution_steps.do_this: Identify all structural differences between the starting material and the target, then label each new group by its directing character
solution_steps.why: The rule is: before planning any sequence of steps, an expert catalogues every bond-forming event needed and classifies each resulting substituent as either an electron-donating ortho/para director or an electron-withdrawing meta director, because that classification dictates which group must already be on the ring when each new group is introduced

vocab.directing effect: the ability of a substituent already on a benzene ring to control where the next incoming group attaches, based on whether the substituent donates or withdraws electron density

solution_steps.do_this: Determine the correct installation order by reasoning backward: ask which substituent, if placed on the ring last, would be steered to the correct position by whatever group is already present
solution_steps.why: The rule is: the group that is installed last must be guided into its correct position by the group installed just before it — so an expert tests each possible ordering and selects the one where the directing effect of the earlier group is consistent with the observed regiochemistry (positional pattern) in the target; here, the amide group is an ortho/para director, so installing the nitro group last and letting the amide direct it produces the correct para relationship, whereas reversing the order would force the amide to install meta to the nitro group, giving the wrong product

vocab.regiochemistry: which specific position on the ring a new group attaches to, out of the several chemically distinct positions available
vocab.para relationship: two substituents located directly across the ring from each other, separated by two carbons on each side

solution_steps.do_this: Trace the amide group back to its precursor amine, and trace that amine back to a nitro group that was previously on the ring, establishing the full retrosynthetic sequence
solution_steps.why: The rule is: amide bonds in this context are always formed from an amine reacting with an acyl chloride, so working backward the amide reveals an aniline-type intermediate (an aryl amine); and aryl amines are most efficiently prepared by nitration followed by reduction, meaning the earliest step on this ring must be introduction of a nitro group that will later be reduced to the amine needed for amide formation

vocab.retrosynthetic disconnection: the mental act of breaking a bond in the target to reveal what simpler molecule or functional group would have been the immediate precursor
vocab.aryl amine: an amine group (–NH2) attached directly to a benzene ring

solution_steps.do_this: Assemble the forward synthesis in the correct order: (1) nitrate the trifluoromethylbenzene starting material to place a nitro group at the position that will ultimately become the amine, (2) reduce that nitro group to an amine, (3) couple the amine with the appropriate acyl chloride to form the amide, and (4) nitrate again, relying on the amide's ortho/para directing effect to install the second nitro group in the correct position
solution_steps.why: The rule is: a synthesis is only valid when each step's regiochemical outcome is controlled by the substituents present at that moment — confirming the forward sequence shows that the amide installed in step 3 acts as an ortho/para director in step 4, correctly placing the final nitro group para to the large amide substituent and delivering the target connectivity

vocab.forward synthesis: writing the reaction sequence in the direction of increasing complexity, from starting material to product, after retrosynthetic planning is complete

question_analysis.skill_tested: Identify which leaving group participates in nucleophilic aromatic substitution by determining its positional relationship to the activating nitro group
question_analysis.disguise: Two potential leaving groups (Cl and Br) are both present on the ring, tempting the student to pick arbitrarily or to default to bromide as the 'better' leaving group from aliphatic chemistry rules
question_analysis.recognition_cue: An aryl halide reacting with a strong nucleophile/base under heat, where a nitro group is also on the ring — this pattern signals nucleophilic aromatic substitution, and position of each halide relative to the nitro group becomes the deciding factor

prior_knowledge_needed: Nucleophilic aromatic substitution (SNAr) requires an electron-withdrawing group ortho or para to the leaving group to stabilize the Meisenheimer complex intermediate
The Meisenheimer complex is a negatively charged cyclohexadienyl anion formed when the nucleophile adds to the aromatic ring before the leaving group departs

Electron-withdrawing groups such as nitro (NO2) stabilize the Meisenheimer complex only when they are ortho or para to the site of nucleophilic attack, because the negative charge can then be delocalized onto the nitro group through resonance
Sodium methoxide (NaOCH3) acts as a nucleophile (methoxide ion) and the heat indicates an activated but not trivially easy substitution
In SNAr, leaving-group ability is secondary to ring activation: a leaving group at a non-activated position simply does not react, regardless of its intrinsic leaving-group quality

solution_steps.do_this: Recognize the reaction mechanism class by reading the reagent and substrate clues together
solution_steps.why: The rule is: when an aryl (ring-attached) halide is treated with a strong nucleophile and the ring bears an electron-withdrawing group, SNAr is the operative pathway. The presence of NaOCH3 (a strong nucleophile), heat, and a nitro group on the ring collectively flag SNAr rather than a free-radical or elimination pathway.

vocab.SNAr: nucleophilic aromatic substitution — a two-step mechanism where a nucleophile first adds to the electron-poor aromatic ring, forming a Meisenheimer complex, then a leaving group departs to restore aromaticity
vocab.electron-withdrawing group: an atom or group that pulls electron density away from the ring, making the ring carbon more electrophilic and able to accept a nucleophile

solution_steps.do_this: Map the positional relationship of each halide substituent to the nitro group on the ring
solution_steps.why: The rule is: SNAr can only proceed at a carbon that is ortho (1,2-relationship) or para (1,4-relationship) to an electron-withdrawing group, because only in those positions can the Meisenheimer complex negative charge be delocalized onto the nitro group via resonance. A halide that is meta (1,3-relationship) to the nitro group sits at a position where no resonance structure places the negative charge on the nitro group, so no stabilization occurs and that site is essentially unreactive.

vocab.ortho: substituents on adjacent ring carbons, a 1,2-positional relationship
vocab.para: substituents on ring carbons directly across from each other, a 1,4-positional relationship
vocab.meta: substituents separated by one ring carbon on each side, a 1,3-positional relationship
vocab.Meisenheimer complex: the anionic sigma complex intermediate in SNAr where the nucleophile is covalently bonded to the ring and aromaticity is temporarily broken

solution_steps.do_this: Use the positional analysis to eliminate the unreactive halide and identify the actual leaving group
solution_steps.why: The rule is: the leaving group in SNAr is whichever halide sits ortho or para to the activating nitro group — not necessarily the one with the highest intrinsic leaving-group ability in aliphatic chemistry. Because bromide occupies the meta position relative to the nitro group, no resonance stabilization of the Meisenheimer complex is available there, so bromide does not act as the leaving group. Chloride occupies the para position relative to the nitro group, enabling full delocalization of the Meisenheimer complex charge onto the nitro oxygen atoms, so chloride is displaced despite being a weaker leaving group than bromide in aliphatic contexts.

vocab.leaving group: the atom or group that departs with the bonding electron pair when a nucleophile attacks, restoring aromaticity in the final step of SNAr

solution_steps.do_this: Construct the product by replacing the reactive leaving group with the incoming nucleophile and confirming the rest of the ring is unchanged
solution_steps.why: The rule is: in SNAr the nucleophile substitutes only the activated position; all other substituents remain on the ring. Methoxide (CH3O−) replaces chloride at the para position relative to the nitro group, yielding an aryl methyl ether (a compound where an OCH3 group is attached directly to the benzene ring) while bromide remains attached at its meta position.

vocab.aryl methyl ether: a compound in which a methoxy group (OCH3) is directly attached to an aromatic ring carbon

question_analysis.skill_tested: Design a multi-step synthesis from benzene to a disubstituted aromatic compound by selecting and sequencing reactions that control regiochemistry (the specific positions of substituents on the ring)
question_analysis.disguise: The target molecule looks simple — just an amine and a hydroxyl group on a benzene ring — but the student must work backward to figure out which reaction installs each group, in what order, and how to guarantee they end up in the correct relative positions
question_analysis.recognition_cue: When a target aromatic compound carries both an –NH2 and an –OH group, and those groups are para to each other, recognize that SNAr (nucleophilic aromatic substitution) can install –OH only when an activating nitro group is already present ortho or para to a leaving group, so the –NH2 must originate from reduction of that nitro group — not from a direct amination

prior_knowledge_needed: Electrophilic aromatic substitution (EAS): the process by which an electrophile replaces a hydrogen on a benzene ring, producing halogenated or nitrated products
Directing effects: electron-donating groups direct incoming electrophiles to ortho and para positions; electron-withdrawing groups direct to meta

SNAr (nucleophilic aromatic substitution): a reaction where a nucleophile replaces a leaving group on an electron-poor aromatic ring — the ring must carry strong electron-withdrawing groups ortho or para to the leaving group to stabilize the intermediate anion
Nitro group reduction: treatment of an aromatic nitro group (–NO2) with a reducing agent such as iron/HCl or H2/Pd converts it to an amino group (–NH2)
Retrosynthetic analysis: the strategy of working backward from the target molecule, disconnecting bonds to identify simpler precursors at each stage

solution_steps.do_this: Apply retrosynthetic analysis to the target: identify which bond or functional group to disconnect last (closest to the target), recognizing that the –NH2 is best traced back to a –NO2 group rather than installed directly
solution_steps.why: The rule is: aromatic amines are almost always made by reducing aromatic nitro groups, not by direct amination. Direct attachment of –NH2 to a benzene ring by EAS does not work cleanly. Therefore, any –NH2 in the target must be 'unmasked' from a –NO2 precursor, and the synthesis plan must include that nitro group earlier in the sequence.

vocab.retrosynthetic analysis: a problem-solving strategy where you start at the target molecule and work backward, asking 'what immediate precursor could give this product?' at each step
vocab.unmasking: revealing a desired functional group by chemically converting a different group that was used as a stand-in during earlier, incompatible steps

solution_steps.do_this: Identify the only available pathway to install an –OH group on the ring (SNAr), then determine what structural features must already be present on the ring for SNAr to work, specifically a leaving group and a nitro group positioned ortho or para to it
solution_steps.why: The rule is: SNAr proceeds through a negatively charged intermediate called a Meisenheimer complex (a cyclohexadienyl anion stabilized by resonance into electron-withdrawing groups). That intermediate is only stable — and the reaction only fast — when a strong electron-withdrawing group such as –NO2 sits ortho or para to the leaving group, because those positions bear the most negative charge. A nitro group at meta to the leaving group cannot stabilize the intermediate and SNAr fails. This constraint dictates that the chlorine (leaving group) and nitro group must be para to each other in the precursor.

vocab.SNAr: nucleophilic aromatic substitution — a two-step addition-elimination sequence where a nucleophile adds to an electron-poor ring, forming a stabilized anionic intermediate, before a leaving group departs
vocab.Meisenheimer complex: the anionic intermediate in SNAr, a cyclohexadienyl anion (a six-membered ring that has temporarily lost its aromaticity) whose negative charge is delocalized into flanking electron-withdrawing groups
vocab.leaving group: an atom or group that departs with the bonding electrons during a reaction — on an aromatic ring in SNAr, halogens such as Cl serve this role

solution_steps.do_this: Plan the forward sequence of reactions from benzene, ordering the steps so that each group installed helps direct the next group to the correct position: (1) halogenation to attach a chlorine, (2) nitration to place –NO2 para to the chlorine using the directing power of chlorine, (3) SNAr with hydroxide to replace chlorine with –OH, (4) reduction of –NO2 to –NH2
solution_steps.why: The rule is: chlorine on a benzene ring is an ortho/para director in EAS, despite being an electron-withdrawing atom, because its lone pairs donate into the ring by resonance at ortho and para positions. This means nitration after chlorination will preferentially place –NO2 para to Cl (para is favored over ortho due to steric reasons), giving exactly the para-chloro, para-nitro arrangement needed for SNAr. Performing nitration before chlorination or after hydroxide installation would either produce the wrong regiochemistry or interfere with later steps.

vocab.ortho/para director: a substituent already on a benzene ring that channels the incoming electrophile to the positions ortho (adjacent) and para (directly across) to itself during EAS
vocab.regiochemistry: which specific position on a molecule a reaction occurs at — in aromatic chemistry, this means which carbon of the ring bears the new substituent
vocab.halogenation: an EAS reaction that attaches a halogen atom (here, Cl) to the ring, typically using Cl2 and a Lewis acid catalyst such as FeCl3

solution_steps.do_this: Verify that the overall regiochemical outcome matches the target: confirm that after all four steps the –OH and –NH2 groups are para to each other on the ring, as required by the target structure
solution_steps.why: The rule is: each step in a multi-step synthesis must be checked not just for whether the reaction occurs, but for whether it places functional groups at the exact correct positions. Here, Cl directs –NO2 para; SNAr replaces Cl with –OH at that same carbon; reduction converts –NO2 to –NH2 at its carbon — so the final product has –OH and –NH2 in a 1,4 (para) relationship, matching the target. If any step had produced an ortho nitration instead, the final product would be a different isomer and the synthesis would fail.

vocab.1,4-relationship: two substituents on a benzene ring that are para to each other, meaning they sit on opposite ends of the ring with two carbons between them on each side
vocab.isomer: a compound with the same molecular formula as another but with atoms arranged differently in space or connectivity, giving different properties

18.34Starting with benzene and using any other neces-
sary reagents of your choice, design a synthesis for anisole
(methoxybenzene).
solution_text_raw:
18.34.Wehavenotyetseenadirectmethodfor
installing a methoxy group on an aromatic ring, however,
wehaveseenawaytoinstallanOHgroup(via
chlorination,followedbyelimination-addition).A
Williamson ether synthesis can then be used to methylate
the oxygen atom, giving the desired target molecule.
CHAPTER 18747

question_analysis.skill_tested: Design a multi-step synthesis by recognizing that a methoxy group must be built indirectly from a hydroxyl group already attached to an aromatic ring
question_analysis.disguise: The problem looks like a simple one-step substitution, but no direct electrophilic aromatic substitution installs –OCH₃; the student must construct the oxygen-containing group in stages
question_analysis.recognition_cue: Target molecule has an –OCH₃ directly on a benzene ring (an aryl ether) with no other substituents — this pattern signals Williamson ether synthesis applied to a phenol intermediate, which itself requires a round-about aromatic route

prior_knowledge_needed: Electrophilic aromatic halogenation: Cl₂/FeCl₃ installs a chlorine atom on a benzene ring
Benzyne mechanism: strong base causes elimination of HX from an aryl halide to form a strained triple-bond intermediate (benzyne), which water or hydroxide then attacks to give a phenol
Williamson ether synthesis: an alkoxide ion (deprotonated alcohol) attacks a primary alkyl halide in an SN2 reaction to form an ether
Sodium hydroxide deprotonates a phenol (pKa ~10) to form sodium phenoxide, the nucleophile needed for Williamson synthesis
Methyl iodide (CH₃I) or dimethyl sulfate serve as the methyl-donating electrophile in Williamson synthesis

solution_steps.do_this: Recognize that no direct electrophilic aromatic substitution reaction installs a methoxy group, so plan to install an oxygen-containing group first, then convert it
solution_steps.why: The rule is: electrophilic aromatic substitution (EAS) requires a strong electrophile that attacks the pi system; –OCH₃ has no electrophilic form that works directly on benzene, so the methoxy group must be assembled in two stages — oxygen attachment first, then methylation

vocab.electrophilic aromatic substitution (EAS: a reaction in which an electrophile replaces a hydrogen on a benzene ring, driven by the ring's electron-rich pi cloud

solution_steps.do_this: Plan Step 1 and Step 2: convert benzene to chlorobenzene via EAS halogenation, then treat chlorobenzene with very strong base (NaNH₂ or NaOH at high temperature) to generate a benzyne intermediate and subsequently hydrolyze it to phenol
solution_steps.why: The rule is: aryl halides do not normally undergo SN2, but under forcing conditions (very strong base, high temperature) an elimination-addition pathway operates — base removes an adjacent ring hydrogen to form a highly strained triple bond (benzyne), which is then attacked by hydroxide to give phenol; this is currently the taught route to attach –OH directly to a benzene ring

vocab.benzyne: a fleeting intermediate with a formal triple bond inside a six-membered ring, so strained that it reacts rapidly with any nucleophile
vocab.elimination-addition: a two-stage mechanism where a small molecule is first removed (elimination) to form a reactive intermediate, then a nucleophile adds across that intermediate

solution_steps.do_this: Plan Step 3: deprotonate the phenol with NaOH (or NaH) to form sodium phenoxide, the oxygen-centered nucleophile needed for the next step
solution_steps.why: The rule is: Williamson ether synthesis requires an alkoxide (deprotonated alcohol) as the nucleophile; phenol (pKa ~10) is acidic enough to be fully deprotonated by NaOH, generating sodium phenoxide — a charged, strongly nucleophilic oxygen species that will not simply protonate the alkyl halide instead of attacking it

vocab.sodium phenoxide: the salt formed when a phenol loses its –OH proton to base, leaving an oxygen atom with a negative charge attached to the benzene ring

solution_steps.do_this: Plan Step 4: carry out Williamson ether synthesis by reacting sodium phenoxide with a methyl electrophile (e.g., CH₃I) to form anisole (methoxybenzene)
solution_steps.why: The rule is: in Williamson ether synthesis the alkoxide attacks the carbon of a primary alkyl halide in an SN2 reaction, displacing the halide and forming a C–O–C linkage; methyl iodide is primary and unhindered, so backside attack by the phenoxide oxygen is fast and clean, installing the –OCH₃ group and completing the target molecule

vocab.Williamson ether synthesis: a method for making ethers by SN2 reaction of an alkoxide nucleophile with a primary (or methyl) alkyl halide
vocab.anisole: the common name for methoxybenzene, a benzene ring with an –OCH₃ group directly attached

(the aromatic ring does not have a nitro group), so we expect an elimination-addition reaction.Upon treatment with
sodium hydroxide, two possible benzyne intermediates are initially formed:
Then, for each of these benzyne intermediates, the hydroxide ion can attack either side of the triple bond, which means
that the OH group is ultimately positioned at either C4, C5, or C6, giving rise to the following three products. Note that

question_analysis.skill_tested: Predict all constitutional isomers produced when a substituted arene undergoes elimination-addition (benzyne mechanism) with a strong base, by mapping every possible benzyne intermediate and every possible nucleophile-attack site
question_analysis.disguise: The problem looks like a simple nucleophilic substitution on an arene, tempting a student to invoke SNAr (sigma-complex pathway); the actual pathway is the less obvious benzyne (elimination-addition) route
question_analysis.recognition_cue: An aryl chloride treated with NaOH at high temperature, where the ring carries NO electron-withdrawing groups — the absence of ring-activating withdrawing groups rules out SNAr and points directly to the benzyne elimination-addition pathway

prior_knowledge_needed: The three criteria for SNAr (nucleophilic aromatic substitution via sigma/Meisenheimer complex): leaving group on the ring, at least one strong electron-withdrawing group ortho or para to the leaving group, and a good nucleophile
Benzyne (aryne) intermediate: a highly strained species with a formal additional pi bond in the aromatic ring formed by elimination of adjacent H and leaving group
How to number a polysubstituted benzene ring to track regiochemistry
The rule that a nucleophile can attack either carbon of the benzyne triple bond, giving two possible substitution positions per benzyne
How two different benzynes can arise from one starting material when the leaving group has non-equivalent neighbors

solution_steps.do_this: Check whether the SNAr (direct nucleophilic displacement) pathway is available for this substrate
solution_steps.why: The rule is: SNAr requires at least one strong electron-withdrawing group (such as nitro or carbonyl) positioned ortho or para to the leaving group so it can stabilize the Meisenheimer complex (the anionic addition intermediate where the ring temporarily loses aromaticity). Because the ring in this substrate carries only alkyl substituents — which are electron-donating, not withdrawing — none of the three SNAr criteria are satisfied, so the reaction cannot proceed by direct nucleophilic addition. This forces the mechanism into the elimination-addition (benzyne) pathway.

vocab.SNAr: nucleophilic aromatic substitution in which a nucleophile adds to an electron-poor aromatic ring, forming a Meisenheimer complex (a stable carbanion intermediate) before the leaving group departs
vocab.Meisenheimer complex: the anionic sigma complex (intermediate where one ring carbon is now sp3 and the ring has lost aromaticity) formed when a nucleophile adds to an electron-deficient aromatic carbon

solution_steps.do_this: Identify all distinct benzyne intermediates that can form from this starting material by determining which ring hydrogens are adjacent to the leaving group
solution_steps.why: The rule is: benzyne formation requires base to abstract a proton from a ring carbon immediately adjacent (ortho) to the leaving group, and the leaving group then departs to generate the strained triple bond. Because the chlorine-bearing carbon has two neighboring ring carbons, each with a different substitution environment, two structurally distinct benzynes can form — one in which the triple bond is positioned toward one neighbor and one where it is positioned toward the other neighbor. Each unique benzyne is a separate intermediate leading to a different set of products.

vocab.benzyne (aryne): a highly reactive, neutral intermediate in which two adjacent ring carbons are joined by what can be described as a strained additional pi bond within the aromatic ring, formed by syn-elimination of an adjacent hydrogen and the leaving group
vocab.elimination-addition: a two-stage mechanism where base first eliminates HX from an aryl halide to give benzyne, then a nucleophile adds across the strained triple bond

solution_steps.do_this: For each benzyne intermediate, determine the two possible attack sites for the hydroxide nucleophile and draw the resulting phenol product
solution_steps.why: The rule is: hydroxide can add to either carbon of the benzyne triple bond with roughly equal probability because both carbons are similarly electrophilic in a symmetric or near-symmetric triple bond. Each attack position places the hydroxyl group at a different ring carbon, so two regioisomeric phenols arise from each benzyne. When the products from both benzynes are collected and duplicates are removed, the total set of constitutional isomers (compounds with identical molecular formulas but different connectivity) is obtained. For this substrate, tallying both benzynes and both attack sites on each yields exactly three distinct C9H12O phenol products.

vocab.regioisomers: constitutional isomers (same molecular formula, different atom connectivity) that differ in the position of a substituent on the ring
vocab.phenol: an aromatic compound carrying a hydroxyl group (-OH) directly attached to the ring carbon

solution_steps.do_this: Verify that the three drawn structures are genuinely distinct by comparing the relative positions of the methyl, ethyl, and hydroxyl groups on each ring, and confirm all share the molecular formula C9H12O
solution_steps.why: The rule is: constitutional isomers must differ in connectivity, not merely in orientation; rotating or flipping a ring can make two drawn structures identical. Expert chemists assign systematic locants (numbering positions) to each proposed product and confirm that no two structures share the same substitution pattern. Confirming the molecular formula (count: benzene ring 6C + methyl 1C + ethyl 2C = 9C; 12H after accounting for ring and alkyl hydrogens; one O from OH) ensures no arithmetic errors were introduced.

vocab.constitutional isomers: molecules that share the same molecular formula but have atoms connected in different sequences or arrangements
vocab.locant: the number assigned to a ring carbon position used to unambiguously describe where substituents are attached
