question_analysis.skill_tested: Plan a multi-step synthesis from benzene by choosing the correct order of substituent installation to exploit temporary directing groups
question_analysis.disguise: The final product has two groups that are both ortho/para directors, making it seem impossible to achieve meta-relative placement through direct substitution — the trick is hidden in the two-step nature of amino group installation
question_analysis.recognition_cue: When two substituents in the target are positioned meta to each other but BOTH are ortho/para directors in their final form, ask whether either group requires a precursor that is a meta director

prior_knowledge_needed: Nitro groups (-NO2) are meta directors (electron-withdrawing, deactivating), meaning electrophilic attack occurs preferentially at the position 180° across the ring
Amino groups (-NH2) are ortho/para directors (electron-donating through lone pair resonance)
An amino group is installed via a two-step sequence: first nitration to place a nitro group, then reduction (e.g., with Fe/HCl or H2/Pd) to convert -NO2 to -NH2
Friedel-Crafts acylation installs an acyl group (R-C=O attached to ring) that acts as a meta director because it is electron-withdrawing
Acyl groups can be reduced to alkyl groups (Clemmensen or Wolff-Kishner reduction) but in this context the carbonyl's directing effect is exploited before any reduction
Bromine can be installed on a benzene ring via electrophilic aromatic bromination using Br2 and a Lewis acid catalyst (e.g., FeBr3)
The order of steps in a synthesis determines the regiochemistry (positional selectivity) of each new substituent

solution_steps.do_this: Analyze the spatial relationship between the two groups in the target molecule and classify each group's directing behavior in its final form
solution_steps.why: The rule is: the directing effect of a substituent already on the ring governs where the NEXT group lands. An expert always maps out what each final group directs before choosing an installation order, because if both final groups are ortho/para directors yet sit meta to each other, direct sequential installation is impossible and a workaround is required

vocab.regiochemistry: the positional outcome of a reaction — specifically, which carbon on the ring receives the new substituent
vocab.ortho/para director: a substituent that steers incoming electrophiles to the positions adjacent to it (ortho) or directly across from it (para) because it donates electron density into those positions
vocab.meta director: a substituent that steers incoming electrophiles to the position two carbons away because it withdraws electron density from ortho and para positions, making meta relatively electron-rich

solution_steps.do_this: Identify whether either target substituent must be synthesized through a precursor that has a different directing effect than the final group, and choose that precursor as the handle for sequencing
solution_steps.why: The rule is: a group's directing power applies at the moment it sits on the ring, not after later transformation. An amino group (-NH2) does not appear on the ring directly — it is produced by reducing a nitro group (-NO2). A nitro group is a meta director. Therefore, installing -NO2 first and then doing the next substitution BEFORE reducing converts the meta-directing window into an asset, achieving meta placement that the final amino group alone could never direct

vocab.nitro group: a -NO2 group attached to the ring; strongly electron-withdrawing, making it a meta director and deactivator
vocab.reduction: a reaction that adds hydrogen (or removes oxygen) to convert -NO2 into -NH2, changing both the chemistry and the directing ability of the substituent

solution_steps.do_this: For part (a), design the sequence: (1) nitrate benzene to place the meta-directing nitro group, (2) brominate the nitrobenzene so the bromine lands meta to the nitro group, (3) reduce the nitro group to the amino group, yielding the meta-amino-bromo product
solution_steps.why: The rule is: perform electrophilic aromatic substitution (EAS) reactions in an order where the group already on the ring directs the new group to the correct position. Nitro is on the ring during bromination, so it directs Br to the meta position. Only after correct positioning is established is the nitro group reduced to -NH2, which would have been an unhelpful ortho/para director if installed first

vocab.electrophilic aromatic substitution (EAS: a reaction class where an electrophile replaces a hydrogen on an aromatic ring, preserving aromaticity; both nitration and bromination proceed by this mechanism
vocab.nitration: EAS using a mixture of concentrated nitric and sulfuric acids to attach a -NO2 group to the ring

solution_steps.do_this: For part (b), recognize that achieving meta placement of two groups both of which are ortho/para directors in their final form requires using a Friedel-Crafts acylation to introduce a temporary meta-directing acyl group, performing the second substitution under its direction, then transforming or retaining that acyl group as needed to reach the target structure
solution_steps.why: The rule is: when a target substituent cannot itself act as a meta director at any stage, an entirely different meta-directing group must be used as a temporary scaffold. A Friedel-Crafts acyl group (an electron-withdrawing carbonyl attached to the ring) is a meta director; it can be installed, used to direct a second substituent to the meta position, and then either reduced or otherwise modified. This strategy converts the synthetic problem from 'impossible with ortho/para directors' to 'straightforward with a temporary meta director'

vocab.Friedel-Crafts acylation: EAS using an acyl chloride (R-C(=O)-Cl) and a Lewis acid catalyst (like AlCl3) to attach an acyl group to the aromatic ring
vocab.acyl group: a carbonyl-containing fragment (R-C=O-) attached to the ring; electron-withdrawing, making it a meta director
vocab.temporary directing group: a substituent installed solely to control regiochemistry of a subsequent step, which is then modified or removed after its directing job is done

solution_steps.do_this: Verify the overall synthetic logic by tracing each step's directing group and confirming that the group present on the ring at the moment of each new EAS step points to the correct carbon for the target's substitution pattern
solution_steps.why: The rule is: a synthesis is only valid if every substitution step is governed by the correct director at that exact moment. Checking step-by-step prevents errors where a student installs groups in a logical-seeming order that actually delivers the wrong regiochemistry. Confirming the logic also reveals if any protecting groups or extra steps are needed to prevent undesired reactivity (e.g., protecting -NH2 before strong electrophilic conditions)

vocab.protecting group: a temporary chemical modification that masks a reactive group (such as converting -NH2 to an amide) so it does not interfere with a subsequent reaction, then removed afterward

question_analysis.skill_tested: Identify which step in a two-step aromatic synthesis fails by applying directing-group rules and carbocation-rearrangement logic to each specific case
question_analysis.disguise: The problem presents finished reaction schemes that look plausible at first glance, hiding subtle regioselectivity errors, deactivation incompatibilities, and carbocation rearrangement pitfalls
question_analysis.recognition_cue: A two-step EAS sequence where the first step installs a substituent and the second step attempts further functionalization — triggers a check: (1) Is the ring reactive enough? (2) Will the directing group send the reagent to the right position? (3) Can the reagent undergo carbocation rearrangement?

prior_knowledge_needed: Electron-withdrawing groups (substituents that pull electron density away from the ring) deactivate the benzene ring toward electrophilic aromatic substitution (EAS) and direct incoming groups to the meta position
Electron-donating groups (substituents that push electron density into the ring) activate the ring and direct incoming groups to the ortho and para positions
Friedel-Crafts alkylation (attaching an alkyl chain to a benzene ring using an alkyl halide and a Lewis acid catalyst) fails entirely on strongly deactivated rings such as nitrobenzene

Primary alkyl carbocations (positively charged carbon with one carbon neighbor) are unstable and tend to rearrange via hydride or methyl shifts to more stable secondary or tertiary carbocations, leading to a different alkyl group being installed than intended

Friedel-Crafts acylation (attaching an acyl group — a carbonyl with an attached carbon chain — to a benzene ring) places the carbonyl group where the directing substituent dictates; a bromine substituent is an ortho/para director

Bulky substituents already on a ring create steric hindrance (physical crowding that blocks approach) at the adjacent ortho positions, forcing an incoming electrophile toward the less hindered para position

solution_steps.do_this: For case (a), evaluate whether the ring produced in step 1 is reactive enough to undergo the step-2 transformation
solution_steps.why: The rule is: a strongly deactivating group renders a benzene ring essentially inert toward Friedel-Crafts reactions. Step 1 installs a nitro group (–NO2), a powerfully electron-withdrawing substituent, producing nitrobenzene. The nitro group drains so much electron density from the ring that the ring cannot donate electrons to an incoming electrophile generated in a Friedel-Crafts alkylation. Therefore step 2 — Friedel-Crafts alkylation with an alkyl chloride and AlCl₃ — simply will not proceed.

vocab.deactivated ring: a benzene ring that reacts more slowly than benzene itself toward electrophilic aromatic substitution because a substituent has reduced the ring's electron density
vocab.Friedel-Crafts alkylation: an EAS reaction that attaches an alkyl group to a benzene ring using an alkyl halide and a Lewis-acid catalyst such as AlCl₃

solution_steps.do_this: For case (b), assess whether the reagent used in step 2 will deliver the intended alkyl group without structural change
solution_steps.why: The rule is: alkyl carbocations (positively charged carbon intermediates) will rearrange to the most stable structure available before reacting with the ring. Step 2 uses a primary alkyl chloride (propyl chloride, where the chlorine is on the end carbon). When AlCl₃ generates the carbocation, the unstable primary carbocation rapidly undergoes a hydride shift (migration of a hydrogen with its electron pair to the neighboring carbon) to give a more stable secondary carbocation. The ring therefore receives an isopropyl group instead of the desired n-propyl group, yielding a mixture of rearranged products rather than the target compound.

vocab.carbocation rearrangement: a structural reorganization in which a hydride or methyl group migrates to an adjacent positively charged carbon, converting a less stable carbocation into a more stable one
vocab.primary carbocation: a carbocation in which the positively charged carbon is bonded to only one other carbon atom; highly unstable and prone to rearrangement

solution_steps.do_this: For case (c), determine where the directing group already on the ring will send the electrophile introduced in step 2, and compare that to the desired position
solution_steps.why: The rule is: ortho/para directors (substituents that donate electron density into the ring) concentrate electrophile attack at the positions directly adjacent to or directly across from themselves on the ring. Step 1 places a bromine atom on the ring. Bromine is an ortho/para director, so step 2 — Friedel-Crafts acylation — will install the acyl group ortho or para to the bromine, with the para product predominating because the ortho positions are more sterically crowded. The target molecule requires the acyl group at the meta position, which is exactly where an ortho/para director does NOT direct. The synthesis therefore produces the wrong regioisomer (compound with the same molecular formula but substituents at different ring positions).

vocab.ortho/para director: a ring substituent that makes the ring positions directly adjacent to it (ortho) and directly across from it (para) the preferred sites for electrophilic attack
vocab.regioisomer: one of two or more compounds that have identical molecular formulas and bond types but differ in the location of a substituent or functional group

solution_steps.do_this: For case (d), assess whether steric hindrance from the step-1 substituent will allow the step-2 electrophile to reach the intended ortho position
solution_steps.why: The rule is: bulky substituents block electrophilic attack at the adjacent ortho positions through steric hindrance, redirecting the electrophile to the less congested para position. Step 1 installs a tert-butyl group (a carbon bonded to three other carbons — very large and branched). Although a tert-butyl group is an ortho/para activator, its sheer size physically prevents the incoming bromine electrophile from approaching the two ortho positions flanking it. Bromination therefore occurs predominantly at the para position, not at the desired ortho position.

vocab.steric hindrance: the physical blocking of a reaction site caused by the spatial bulk of nearby atoms or groups, which prevents a reagent from approaching closely enough to react
vocab.tert-butyl group: a carbon substituent in which the carbon attached to the ring is bonded to three additional carbon groups, making it large and highly branched

question_analysis.skill_tested: Predict the ring and position where electrophilic aromatic bromination occurs on a molecule containing two aromatic rings with competing activating and deactivating substituents
question_analysis.disguise: The molecules look complex because they contain two fused or linked aromatic rings, masking the need to compare relative ring activation and then apply directing rules within the winning ring
question_analysis.recognition_cue: Two aromatic rings in one molecule, one ring attached to a carbonyl or other deactivating group and the other attached to an electron-donating group — this pattern demands a two-stage decision: pick the more activated ring first, then find the preferred position on that ring

prior_knowledge_needed: Activating substituents (groups that donate electron density into the ring, making it more reactive toward electrophiles) versus deactivating substituents (groups that withdraw electron density, making the ring less reactive)
A carbonyl group attached directly to an aromatic ring acts as a deactivating, meta-directing substituent
An alkyl or methylene (–CH2–) group attached to a ring acts as a weak activating, ortho/para-directing substituent
A nitrogen atom attached to a ring (amine or amide nitrogen) acts as a strong activating, ortho/para-directing substituent
Ortho/para directors (activators) place the incoming electrophile at the positions directly adjacent to or directly across from the substituent
Steric hindrance (physical crowding near the substituent) disfavors the ortho positions when the substituent is large, directing the reaction predominantly to the para position

solution_steps.do_this: Classify each aromatic ring in the molecule as activated or deactivated by examining what group connects to it
solution_steps.why: The rule is: electron-donating groups raise the ring's electron density and speed up electrophilic attack, while electron-withdrawing groups lower it and slow attack. Identifying which ring is electron-richer tells you where the electrophile will preferentially strike before you consider position within a ring.

vocab.activating substituent: a group that donates electrons into the aromatic ring through resonance or induction, making the ring more reactive toward electron-seeking reagents
vocab.deactivating substituent: a group that pulls electrons away from the aromatic ring, making it less reactive toward electron-seeking reagents

solution_steps.do_this: Select the more activated ring as the site of bromination
solution_steps.why: The rule is: electrophilic aromatic substitution (a reaction where an electrophile replaces a hydrogen on an aromatic ring) occurs preferentially on whichever ring has higher electron density. For parts (a) and (b), one ring bears a direct carbonyl connection (deactivated) and the other bears a methylene or nitrogen connection (activated), so the activated ring wins. For part (c), the left ring has only one substituent that is an activator, whereas the right ring has both an activator and a deactivator partially canceling each other, making the left ring more activated overall.

vocab.electrophilic aromatic substitution: a reaction class where an electrophile attacks an electron-rich aromatic ring, ultimately replacing one ring hydrogen with the new group

solution_steps.do_this: Determine the directing effect of the substituent on the chosen ring to identify whether ortho, meta, or para positions are favored
solution_steps.why: The rule is: activating substituents are always ortho/para directors because they stabilize the carbocation intermediate (arenium ion) most effectively when the positive charge lands at positions ortho or para to them. Since every winning ring in these problems carries an activating substituent, bromination is steered to the ortho and para positions on that ring.

vocab.ortho position: the ring carbon immediately adjacent (one bond away) to the carbon bearing the substituent
vocab.para position: the ring carbon directly across the ring (three bonds away) from the carbon bearing the substituent
vocab.arenium ion: the positively charged, non-aromatic intermediate formed when an electrophile adds to an aromatic ring during electrophilic aromatic substitution

solution_steps.do_this: Evaluate steric hindrance at the ortho positions to decide between ortho and para as the dominant product site
solution_steps.why: The rule is: large substituents physically block the space near the ortho carbons, raising the energy cost for the electrophile to approach those positions. When the substituent is bulky (for example, a –CH2– linker to another ring or a nitrogen-containing group with attached atoms), steric congestion disfavors ortho attack and the reaction occurs predominantly at the less crowded para position.

vocab.steric hindrance: physical crowding caused by the size of nearby atoms or groups that prevents a reagent from easily reaching a particular site

solution_steps.do_this: Confirm the final answer by marking the para carbon of the activated ring as the predicted bromination site
solution_steps.why: The rule is: the position that combines high electron density (on the activated ring, at an ortho/para site) with minimal steric interference (para rather than ortho) is the thermodynamically and kinetically preferred site for electrophilic attack. Combining the ring-selection decision and the positional-directing decision gives one clear site for each molecule.

vocab.regioselectivity: the preference of a reaction to occur at one specific position on a molecule over other possible positions

question_text_raw:
18.56Whenpara-bromotolueneistreatedwithsodiumamide
(NaNH2), two products are obtained. Draw both products and propose
a plausible mechanism for their formation.
solution_text_raw:
18.56.The reagent is sodium amide, which is strongly nucleophilic and strongly basic. The three criteria for SNAr are
not met (the aromatic ring lacks a nitro group), and therefore, the reaction must occur via an elimination-addition
mechanism (as seen in Mechanism 18.9), shown here. The benzyne intermediate can be attacked on either side of the
triple bond, leading to the two products shown:
pairing_note: Structural diagram(s) in original not captured by text extraction

question_analysis.skill_tested: Predict both regioisomeric products from a benzyne intermediate by identifying which carbon of the strained triple bond gets attacked
question_analysis.disguise: The problem looks like a simple nucleophilic aromatic substitution (direct replacement of a leaving group on a benzene ring), but the ring lacks the electron-withdrawing groups required for that pathway, forcing a completely different two-step mechanism
question_analysis.recognition_cue: An aryl halide (halogen directly on a benzene ring) treated with a very strong base like sodium amide (NaNH2), where the ring carries only electron-donating or neutral substituents and no nitro groups — this specific combination signals the elimination-addition (benzyne) route

prior_knowledge_needed: SNAr (nucleophilic aromatic substitution by direct addition-elimination) requires at least one strong electron-withdrawing group ortho or para to the leaving group to stabilize the Meisenheimer complex (a negatively charged ring intermediate)

When SNAr conditions are not met, a strong base can instead abstract a proton adjacent to the leaving group to form a benzyne intermediate — a six-membered ring containing a highly strained formal triple bond
Benzyne (aryne) has a distorted, highly reactive pi bond lying in the plane of the ring; nucleophiles attack either carbon of this bond with roughly equal ease
Para-bromotoluene structure: a benzene ring with a methyl group and a bromine atom at opposite ends (positions 1 and 4)
Sodium amide (NaNH2) acts both as a strong base (pKa of NH3 ~ 38) that can deprotonate a C–H bond on the ring, and as a nucleophile (the amide anion, NH2⁻) that attacks the benzyne intermediate

solution_steps.do_this: Check whether the three criteria for SNAr are satisfied: leaving group on the ring, good nucleophile present, and electron-withdrawing group(s) activating the ring toward nucleophilic attack
solution_steps.why: The rule is: SNAr proceeds only when a stabilized anionic intermediate (Meisenheimer complex) can form, which requires electron density to be pulled away from the ring by withdrawing substituents. A methyl group is electron-donating, not withdrawing, so no Meisenheimer complex can be stabilized here — SNAr is ruled out and the mechanism must shift to elimination-addition

vocab.SNAr: nucleophilic aromatic substitution via an addition-elimination sequence in which the nucleophile adds to the ring first, forming a charged intermediate, before the leaving group departs
vocab.Meisenheimer complex: the negatively charged, non-aromatic ring intermediate formed when a nucleophile adds to an aryl ring during SNAr; it is only stable when electron-withdrawing groups spread out the negative charge

solution_steps.do_this: Map out the elimination step: identify which ring carbon-hydrogen bond (adjacent to the carbon bearing bromine) the amide base removes, and determine which regiochemical direction of elimination gives the benzyne intermediate
solution_steps.why: The rule is: in elimination-addition, the base abstracts a proton from a ring carbon directly neighboring the carbon that holds the leaving group, generating a carbanion (a carbon bearing a negative charge) that immediately expels the leaving group to form the strained triple bond. For para-bromotoluene, there are carbons on both sides of the bromine; the one closer to the methyl group and the one farther away are not equivalent, so two different benzyne regioisomers are conceivable — but typically one predominates based on which adjacent C–H is more accessible

vocab.elimination-addition: a two-stage mechanism where a leaving group and an adjacent hydrogen are first eliminated to form a benzyne, then a nucleophile adds across the triple bond
vocab.benzyne (aryne): a transient (very short-lived) intermediate consisting of a benzene ring with a formal triple bond between two adjacent carbons; the extra bond is severely strained because it is forced to lie in the plane of the ring

solution_steps.do_this: Draw the benzyne intermediate and then evaluate nucleophilic attack at each of the two carbons of the strained triple bond
solution_steps.why: The rule is: a nucleophile can attack either end of the benzyne triple bond because both carbons bear partial positive character; whichever carbon is attacked becomes the new C–N bond, and the other carbon receives the proton when the resulting carbanion is quenched by solvent or conjugate acid. Because the two carbons of the triple bond are in different environments relative to the methyl substituent, attack at one carbon places the amino group (–NH2) at one position, and attack at the other carbon places it at a different position — yielding two distinct aniline (aminobenzene) products that are regioisomers of each other

vocab.regioisomers: constitutional isomers (same molecular formula, atoms connected differently) that differ only in which position on the ring a substituent is attached
vocab.carbanion: a carbon atom bearing a full negative charge due to having an extra pair of electrons

solution_steps.do_this: Identify the two final products by naming the relationship of the amino group to the methyl group in each case, and confirm that bromine has been fully expelled in both
solution_steps.why: The rule is: the overall transformation converts an aryl halide into an arylamine (an amine group directly attached to a benzene ring); the two products are the two possible aminotoluenes (one with amino and methyl groups in a 1,3-relationship and one in a 1,2-relationship relative to each other), reflecting the two modes of nucleophilic addition to the symmetric benzyne intermediate. Both products lack bromine, confirming complete elimination-addition

vocab.arylamine: a compound in which an amino group (–NH2) is attached directly to a benzene ring, also called an aniline derivative
vocab.aminotoluene: a benzene ring bearing both a methyl group and an amino group; also called toluidine; the specific isomer depends on the relative positions of the two substituents

question_text_raw:
18.57Picric acid is a military explosive formed via the nitration of
phenol under conditions that install three nitro groups. Draw the struc-
ture and provide an IUPAC name for picric acid.
solution_text_raw:
18.57.Phenol has only one substituent (OH) connected to the aromatic ring. This group is a strong activator, and is
therefore anortho-paradirector. If conditions are controlled such that three nitro groups are installed, then we expect
the three nitro groups to be installed at theorthoandparapositions, as shown:
CHAPTER 18763
pairing_note: Structural diagram(s) in original not captured by text extraction

question_analysis.skill_tested: Predict the substitution pattern on a polynitrated aromatic ring by identifying the directing effects of the existing substituent, then construct the IUPAC name for the resulting compound.
question_analysis.disguise: The problem is framed historically as a military explosive, which may obscure that it is fundamentally an electrophilic aromatic substitution (EAS) regiochemistry problem requiring knowledge of activating/deactivating groups and their directing effects.
question_analysis.recognition_cue: Any time a benzene ring already carries a hydroxyl group (–OH) and you are asked to add multiple nitro groups, the expert framework that fires is: OH is a strong ortho/para director, so all incoming groups must land at those positions — fill ortho and para seats first, then account for symmetry.

prior_knowledge_needed: Electrophilic aromatic substitution (EAS): the general reaction where an electrophile replaces a hydrogen on a benzene ring, driven by the ring's electron density.

Activating vs. deactivating substituents: groups attached to the ring either donate electron density (activating) or withdraw it (deactivating), changing how reactive the ring is toward electrophiles.

Ortho/para directors: substituents that donate electron density through resonance or induction, making the ortho (adjacent) and para (across the ring) positions electron-rich and therefore the preferred sites of attack.
IUPAC nomenclature for polysubstituted benzenes: numbering the ring to give the lowest locant set, listing substituents alphabetically as prefixes, and identifying the principal characteristic group.
Nitration: the EAS reaction where a nitro group (–NO2) is introduced onto an aromatic ring using a nitrating mixture.

solution_steps.do_this: Identify the directing effect of the substituent already attached to the benzene ring in phenol.
solution_steps.why: The rule is: oxygen-containing groups attached directly to an aromatic ring donate lone-pair electron density into the ring through resonance, making the ring more electron-rich and steering incoming electrophiles specifically to the ortho (positions adjacent to the substituent) and para (position directly across the ring) sites. Because phenol's –OH group follows this rule, all three nitro groups must land at ortho or para positions — there is no other option under normal EAS conditions.

vocab.resonance donation: The process by which a lone pair on an atom attached to the ring is delocalized into the ring's pi system, increasing electron density at alternating positions.
vocab.ortho position: The two ring carbons immediately adjacent to the carbon bearing the substituent.
vocab.para position: The single ring carbon directly opposite the carbon bearing the substituent.

solution_steps.do_this: Map out all available ortho and para positions on the phenol ring and determine where three –NO2 groups can be placed without overlap.
solution_steps.why: The rule is: a monosubstituted benzene ring has exactly two ortho positions and one para position relative to the existing group, giving three open seats total. Because three nitro groups are needed and there are exactly three ortho/para seats, every one of those seats must be occupied — no ambiguity remains about which positions receive the nitro groups.

vocab.monosubstituted benzene: A benzene ring that carries exactly one substituent, leaving five hydrogens that could potentially be replaced.
vocab.substitution seat: An informal term for a ring position that is activated toward electrophilic attack by the directing group.

solution_steps.do_this: Draw the resulting structure by placing an –OH group on the ring and then attaching –NO2 groups at both ortho positions and the para position.
solution_steps.why: The rule is: once directing effects dictate which positions are activated, the structure is fully determined — the product is the molecule that has all three directing-favored positions substituted. The expert confirms the drawing is consistent by checking that no two groups share the same carbon and that the ring retains its six-carbon aromatic framework.

vocab.aromatic framework: The six-carbon ring with alternating pi electrons (often drawn as a circle inside a hexagon) that persists throughout EAS because aromaticity is restored after each substitution.
vocab.nitro group (–NO2): A functional group consisting of one nitrogen atom attached to two oxygen atoms, strongly electron-withdrawing when already on the ring but introduced here onto a ring activated by –OH.

solution_steps.do_this: Assign an IUPAC name by numbering the ring to give the principal characteristic group (–OH, named as 'phenol' base or 'hydroxy' prefix) the lowest possible locant, then assign numbers to the three –NO2 groups and compile the name with substituents listed alphabetically.
solution_steps.why: The rule is: IUPAC nomenclature requires the principal characteristic group to receive the lowest locant (position 1 for –OH when phenol is the parent), and all other substituents are cited as prefixes with locants that form the lowest possible set. Applying this rule to the three nitro groups — which land at the two ortho carbons (positions 2 and 6) and the para carbon (position 4) — produces the systematic name 2,4,6-trinitrophenol.

vocab.principal characteristic group: The highest-priority functional group in IUPAC nomenclature, which determines the parent name and receives the lowest locant.
vocab.locant: The number assigned to a specific carbon in the ring to indicate where a substituent is attached.
vocab.trinitrophenol: A phenol (hydroxybenzene) carrying three nitro groups; the 2,4,6-isomer is the compound historically called picric acid.

question_text_raw:
18.58Propose a plausible mechanism for the following transformation:
OH
OH
H2SO4
solution_text_raw:
18.58.The starting material is benzene, and the product exhibits a disubstituted aromatic ring. This will require two
successive aromatic substitution reactions.The reagent (the diol) is not a very strong nucleophile, nor is it a very
strong electrophile.However, in acidic conditions, the diol exists in equilibrium with a very powerful electrophile (a
carbocation), as shown:
As seen above, one of the OH groups can be protonated, giving an excellent leaving group, which can leave to give a
tertiary carbocation.Since the reagent is electrophilic, we expect that the reaction will proceed via electrophilic
aromatic substitution.Accordingly, the aromatic ring attacks the carbocation to give an intermediate sigma complex,
which then loses a proton to restore aromaticity:
The entire process is then repeated again, except this time, the reaction will proceed in anintramolecularfashion,
closing a ring.The OH group is protonated to give an excellent leaving group, which can leave to give a tertiary
carbocation.The aromatic ring attacks the carbocation in an intramolecular process to give an intermediate sigma
complex, which then loses a proton to restore aromaticity:
Since aqueous acidic conditions are employed (H2SO4 is generally dissolved in water), the proton source throughout
+
the mechanism has been H3O, and the base has always been H2O, to be consistent with acidic conditions.
764CHAPTER 18

question_analysis.skill_tested: Construct a stepwise electrophilic aromatic substitution mechanism involving carbocation formation from a diol under acid catalysis, performed twice — once intermolecularly and once intramolecularly — to build a fused ring system
question_analysis.disguise: The diol reagent looks like a simple alcohol, masking the fact that acid converts it into a powerful carbocation electrophile; the two-step ring-forming sequence is hidden behind a single arrow notation
question_analysis.recognition_cue: A benzene ring plus a gem-diol (two OH groups on the same carbon) reacting with a strong acid — this pattern signals carbocation generation from the diol followed by electrophilic aromatic substitution, repeated to close a ring

prior_knowledge_needed: Electrophilic aromatic substitution (EAS) mechanism: attack of an aromatic pi system on an electrophile, forming a sigma complex, then proton loss to restore aromaticity
Protonation of an alcohol by strong acid converts the OH into a water molecule — an excellent leaving group — allowing departure to generate a carbocation
Tertiary carbocations are far more stable than secondary or primary ones due to hyperconjugation and inductive donation from three attached alkyl groups
Intramolecular reactions: a reaction where the electrophile and nucleophile are tethered within the same molecule, allowing ring closure
Sigma complex (arenium ion / Wheland intermediate): the positively charged, non-aromatic intermediate formed when the aromatic ring attacks the electrophile, losing aromaticity temporarily
Acid-base consistency in mechanism: in aqueous H2SO4 the proton source is H3O+ and the base is H2O — never use strong bases or strong acids inconsistently with the reaction conditions

solution_steps.do_this: Identify how the acid converts the diol into a strong electrophile rather than treating the diol as a nucleophile
solution_steps.why: The rule is: an OH group attached to carbon becomes an excellent leaving group only after it is protonated, because protonation converts it from hydroxide (poor leaving group) into neutral water (excellent leaving group). Consequence: H2SO4 protonates one of the two OH groups on the same carbon of the diol, and that water molecule departs, generating a tertiary carbocation (a carbon bearing a full positive charge stabilized by three attached alkyl groups). This carbocation is now a powerful electrophile capable of attacking the aromatic ring.

vocab.diol: a molecule carrying two OH (hydroxyl) groups, here both on the same carbon
vocab.tertiary carbocation: a positively charged carbon that has three carbon-containing groups attached to it, making it relatively stable
vocab.electrophile: an electron-hungry species that seeks out areas of high electron density, such as an aromatic pi system

solution_steps.do_this: Trace the first electrophilic aromatic substitution: intermolecular attack of the benzene ring on the tertiary carbocation, then proton loss to restore aromaticity
solution_steps.why: The rule is: an aromatic ring acts as a nucleophile (electron donor) toward any sufficiently strong electrophile, using its pi electrons to form a new C–C bond. Consequence: two electrons from benzene's pi cloud attack the carbocation, forming a sigma complex (arenium ion) — a positively charged, cyclohexadienyl intermediate that has lost aromaticity. Because aromaticity is highly stabilizing, the system immediately expels a proton (using water as the base under acidic conditions) to regenerate the aromatic ring. The product is a monosubstituted benzene now carrying the diol-derived group.

vocab.sigma complex: the non-aromatic, positively charged ring intermediate formed when the aromatic carbon bonds to the incoming electrophile, also called a Wheland intermediate or arenium ion
vocab.aromaticity: the special extra stability of a cyclic, fully conjugated pi system following Hückel's rule (4n+2 pi electrons)

solution_steps.do_this: Recognize that the product of the first EAS still carries a second OH group, and plan an intramolecular second EAS to close the second ring
solution_steps.why: The rule is: if an electrophilic site and a nucleophilic site are tethered within the same molecule and the geometry allows ring formation, intramolecular reaction is strongly favored over a competing intermolecular pathway. Consequence: the remaining OH group on the carbon already attached to the ring gets protonated by H3O+, generating a second tertiary carbocation — this time within the same molecule. The nearby aromatic ring then attacks this carbocation in an intramolecular fashion, forming the sigma complex that bridges the two portions of the molecule.

vocab.intramolecular: occurring between two reactive sites within the same molecule rather than between two separate molecules
vocab.ring closure: the step where an intramolecular bond forms to create a new cyclic structure

solution_steps.do_this: Complete the second EAS by having water (the base present under acidic conditions) remove a proton from the sigma complex to restore aromaticity and yield the bicyclic product
solution_steps.why: The rule is: after sigma complex formation, the driving force for proton loss is the large thermodynamic stabilization gained by restoring aromaticity. Consequence: H2O (the only base available in dilute aqueous H2SO4) abstracts the proton from the sp3 carbon of the sigma complex, re-establishing the aromatic pi system. The result is a fully aromatic bicyclic compound — two fused rings — and H3O+ is regenerated, consistent with acidic conditions throughout.

vocab.bicyclic: a molecule containing two fused or bridged ring systems sharing at least two carbons
vocab.proton abstraction: the removal of H+ from a species by a base, here water acting as the base

solution_steps.do_this: Audit the entire mechanism for internal consistency: verify that every proton transfer uses H3O+ as the acid and H2O as the base, and confirm that aromaticity is restored after each EAS cycle
solution_steps.why: The rule is: every species written in a mechanism must be chemically reasonable under the stated reaction conditions — using strong bases in strongly acidic solutions, or vice versa, is a mechanistic error. Consequence: checking that H3O+ protonates each OH and that H2O deprotonates each sigma complex ensures the mechanism is self-consistent, avoids introducing impossible intermediates, and confirms that both rings in the final product are fully aromatic.

vocab.mechanistic consistency: the requirement that all intermediates and reagents in a proposed mechanism are stable and present under the actual reaction conditions

aluminum trichloride under conditions that favor dialkylation. Draw the
major product expected from this reaction.
solution_text_raw:
18.59.These reagents will achieve the installation of an
isopropyl group on the aromatic ring.The problem
statementindicatesthatconditionswillfavor
dialkylation, which means that two isopropyl groups are
installed.After installation of the first isopropyl group,
thesecondisopropylisexpectedtobeinstalled
predominantly in theparaposition (because alkyl groups
areortho-paradirectors, and theorthopositions are
sterically hindered by their proximity to the isopropyl
group):

question_analysis.skill_tested: Predict the regiochemistry of a double Friedel-Crafts alkylation by applying directing effects and steric considerations sequentially
question_analysis.disguise: The problem forces you to think in two separate electrophilic aromatic substitution steps rather than one, and to weigh both electronic directing effects and steric crowding when placing the second group
question_analysis.recognition_cue: An alkyl halide plus AlCl3 acting on benzene under 'dialkylation conditions' — this signals two sequential Friedel-Crafts alkylations, requiring you to treat the monoalkylated ring as the starting material for the second substitution

prior_knowledge_needed: Friedel-Crafts alkylation: AlCl3 acts as a Lewis acid to generate a carbocation electrophile from an alkyl halide, which then attacks the aromatic ring
Electrophilic aromatic substitution (EAS): a reaction where an electrophile replaces one hydrogen on an aromatic ring, driven by restoration of aromaticity
Ortho/para directors: substituents that donate electron density into the ring and direct incoming electrophiles to the positions ortho (adjacent) and para (across the ring) to themselves
Alkyl groups are activating ortho/para directors because they donate electron density to the ring through hyperconjugation and induction
Steric hindrance: bulky groups physically block nearby positions on the ring, making ortho attack less favorable when the existing substituent is large

solution_steps.do_this: Identify what the electrophile is and confirm the first substitution product
solution_steps.why: The rule is: AlCl3 abstracts the chloride from the alkyl halide to generate a carbocation electrophile. Isopropyl chloride yields a secondary carbocation (a positively charged carbon with two carbon groups attached), which attacks benzene. Since benzene is unsubstituted and symmetric, there is only one possible monoalkylated product: isopropylbenzene (also called cumene), where one isopropyl group is attached to the ring.

vocab.carbocation: a carbon atom bearing a positive charge and only three bonds, making it electron-deficient and highly reactive toward electron-rich aromatic rings
vocab.isopropyl group: a three-carbon fragment shaped like a V, where the central carbon is attached to the ring and two methyl groups branch from it

solution_steps.do_this: Determine the directing effect of the first isopropyl group on the second substitution
solution_steps.why: The rule is: alkyl groups are ortho/para directors because their sigma bonds interact with the pi system of the ring (hyperconjugation), pushing electron density toward the ortho and para positions. This makes those positions more nucleophilic (electron-rich) and therefore more reactive toward the incoming electrophile. The second isopropyl group will therefore land at an ortho or para position relative to the first.

vocab.ortho position: the ring carbon immediately adjacent (next door) to the carbon bearing the substituent
vocab.para position: the ring carbon directly across the ring from the carbon bearing the substituent
vocab.hyperconjugation: overlap between the electrons in a C-H or C-C sigma bond and the adjacent pi system, which donates electron density into the ring

solution_steps.do_this: Apply steric reasoning to choose between ortho and para placement for the second group
solution_steps.why: The rule is: when a substituent is bulky, the ortho positions are sterically hindered (physically crowded) because an incoming group would have to squeeze next to the large existing substituent. An isopropyl group is branched and takes up significant space around its attachment point. Therefore, although ortho is electronically allowed, the steric penalty makes ortho attack much less favorable. Para attack places the second isopropyl group as far as possible from the first, avoiding this crowding entirely.

vocab.steric hindrance: the physical obstruction caused by large groups in space that raises the energy of a transition state and slows or prevents a reaction at that site

solution_steps.do_this: Assemble the final structure by placing two isopropyl groups in the para relationship on the benzene ring
solution_steps.why: The rule is: the major product of a reaction is the one formed through the lowest-energy pathway. Combining the electronic preference (ortho/para direction) with the steric preference (para over ortho for bulky groups) converges on 1,4-diisopropylbenzene — a benzene ring with one isopropyl group attached at position 1 and a second isopropyl group attached at position 4 directly across the ring. This is the major dialkylation product.

vocab.1,4-disubstituted benzene: a benzene ring where two substituents are placed on carbons that are directly opposite each other, also described as the para arrangement

question_analysis.skill_tested: Draw all valid resonance contributors for the arenium ion (carbocation intermediate) formed during electrophilic aromatic substitution at a specified ring position
question_analysis.disguise: The problem specifies a named substituent (methyl group on toluene) and a named position (para), requiring the student to mentally place the attacking electrophile and then distribute charge — without being told how many structures exist
question_analysis.recognition_cue: Any electrophilic aromatic substitution question asking to draw the sigma complex (arenium ion) at a specific ring position is a signal to apply the three-resonance-structure rule for the cyclohexadienyl cation, then check whether the methyl substituent's position relative to the positive charge allows extra stabilization

prior_knowledge_needed: Electrophilic aromatic substitution (EAS) mechanism: an electrophile bonds to one carbon of the aromatic ring, breaking aromaticity and producing a positively charged, non-aromatic intermediate called the sigma complex or arenium ion (also called a Wheland intermediate)

Resonance structures: alternative Lewis dot representations of the same molecule that differ only in the placement of electrons, connected by double-headed arrows, used together to describe the true electron distribution
A cyclohexadienyl cation (the arenium ion skeleton) always distributes its positive charge across three ring carbons via three resonance contributors
Toluene has a methyl group attached to a benzene ring; the methyl group is an electron-donating, ortho/para-directing substituent
Ring position numbering: if the methyl-bearing carbon is C-1, then the para position is C-4, directly across the ring

solution_steps.do_this: Locate where the electrophile (Cl+) bonds to the ring by placing it at the carbon that is para to the methyl group, and redraw the ring as a non-aromatic cyclohexadienyl cation skeleton at that attachment point
solution_steps.why: The rule is: the sigma complex forms at whichever carbon the electrophile attacks, and that carbon becomes sp3 (tetrahedral, four single bonds), instantly breaking the aromatic delocalization and generating a positive charge somewhere on the remaining pi system. Identifying that attacked carbon first is the anchor for all subsequent resonance drawing.

vocab.sigma complex: the non-aromatic, positively charged ring intermediate in EAS where a new single (sigma) bond has formed between the electrophile and one ring carbon
vocab.sp3 carbon: a carbon using four single bonds pointing toward the corners of a tetrahedron, no longer part of the pi system

solution_steps.do_this: Systematically move the pi electrons around the remaining double bonds to generate three resonance contributors, tracking exactly which ring carbons carry the positive charge in each structure
solution_steps.why: The rule is: in a cyclohexadienyl cation the positive charge is delocalized (spread out) over three non-adjacent carbons via conjugation (alternating single and double bonds that allow pi electrons to shift). Each resonance structure shows the charge on one of those three positions. Skipping any contributor gives an incomplete picture of the intermediate's stability.

vocab.delocalization: the spreading of electrons across multiple atoms through overlapping p orbitals rather than being fixed between two atoms
vocab.conjugation: a system of alternating single and double bonds that allows pi electrons to shift continuously across the connected atoms

solution_steps.do_this: Examine which of the three carbons bearing the positive charge in the resonance structures is directly adjacent to (ortho to) the methyl-bearing carbon, and note any special stabilization
solution_steps.why: The rule is: an electron-donating group (EDG) stabilizes positive charge on an adjacent carbon through hyperconjugation and inductive donation, making that resonance contributor lower in energy. When chlorine attacks the para position, one of the three carbons with the positive charge ends up ortho to the methyl group, meaning the methyl group can donate electron density toward it — this extra stabilization is why para attack is particularly favorable.

vocab.electron-donating group (EDG): a substituent that pushes electron density into the ring, stabilizing nearby positive charge
vocab.hyperconjugation: stabilization of a carbocation by overlap of a C-H (or C-C) sigma bond with the adjacent empty p orbital, effectively donating electron density

solution_steps.do_this: Verify the complete set of resonance structures by confirming each one obeys valence rules (no carbon exceeds four bonds, positive charge is shown with a plus sign on the correct carbon) and that the structures are connected by proper double-headed resonance arrows
solution_steps.why: The rule is: every resonance structure must be a valid Lewis structure — correct atom connectivity, correct bond counts, and explicit charge symbols — otherwise it does not represent a real contributor to the hybrid. Checking this step prevents common errors like placing the charge on the sp3 carbon (which already has four single bonds and cannot hold a positive charge through an empty p orbital in that geometry).

vocab.resonance hybrid: the single true structure of a molecule that is the weighted average of all valid resonance contributors, more stable than any one contributor alone
vocab.double-headed arrow: the curved arrow symbol (↔) placed between resonance structures to show they are alternative descriptions of the same species, not separate molecules

question_analysis.skill_tested: Predict the number and identity of monobromination products by identifying whether a ring substituent activates or deactivates the ring and whether it directs to ortho/para or meta positions
question_analysis.disguise: The problem looks like a simple substitution question but actually tests whether the student can connect substituent electronic effects to both reactivity (how many products form) and regiochemistry (where on the ring the new group lands)
question_analysis.recognition_cue: A benzene ring carrying a single substituent presented alongside a question about bromination products — this pattern demands immediate classification of that substituent as activating/deactivating and ortho-para/meta directing before any product can be drawn

prior_knowledge_needed: Activating groups (substituents that donate electron density into the ring, making it more reactive toward electrophiles) vs deactivating groups (substituents that withdraw electron density, making the ring less reactive)

Ortho-para directors (groups that stabilize the carbocation intermediate when the electrophile attacks the ortho or para position) vs meta directors (groups that destabilize ortho/para intermediates, so meta attack is relatively favored)
Steric effects: bulky groups near the ring make ortho attack physically crowded, so the para product often predominates even when both ortho and para are electronically accessible

A methyl group donates electrons through hyperconjugation and is an ortho-para director; an ester group is a weak deactivator overall but its lone pairs on the oxygen attached to the ring still direct ortho-para
Friedel-Crafts alkylation requires an activated (electron-rich) ring, so strongly deactivated rings do not undergo this reaction

solution_steps.do_this: Classify each substituent as activating or deactivating to decide which ring reacts fastest and whether Friedel-Crafts conditions are even possible
solution_steps.why: The rule is: an electrophilic aromatic substitution reaction proceeds faster when the ring carries more electron density, because the rate-determining step is the formation of a carbocation intermediate (arenium ion) — more electron-rich rings stabilize that intermediate better. A methyl group is activating, so toluene reacts fastest. A deactivating group prevents Friedel-Crafts reactions entirely because those reactions require a highly nucleophilic ring.

vocab.electrophilic aromatic substitution: a reaction in which an electron-hungry species attacks an electron-rich benzene ring, temporarily breaking aromaticity and then restoring it by losing a proton
vocab.arenium ion: the positively charged, non-aromatic intermediate formed when the electrophile bonds to one carbon of the ring

solution_steps.do_this: For each reactive ring, identify the directing effect of its substituent to predict which ring positions are available for attack
solution_steps.why: The rule is: the existing substituent controls regiochemistry (the position on the ring where reaction occurs) by stabilizing or destabilizing the arenium ion at each possible site. Ortho-para directors donate electron density into the ortho and para positions of the arenium ion, lowering its energy at those sites. Meta directors withdraw electron density, making ortho and para attack especially unfavorable, so meta becomes the least-bad option.

vocab.regiochemistry: which specific position among several possible positions on the ring is actually substituted
vocab.ortho position: the ring carbon immediately adjacent to the carbon that already carries the substituent
vocab.para position: the ring carbon directly across the ring from the substituent-bearing carbon

solution_steps.do_this: Count how many distinct monobromination products each directing pattern produces, then apply steric reasoning to predict which product predominates when both ortho and para are allowed
solution_steps.why: The rule is: symmetry of the molecule reduces the number of unique products — for a para-directing or ortho-para-directing group, the ring has two equivalent ortho sites and one para site, so up to two structurally distinct monosubstituted products are possible. When a bulky group already occupies one position, attack at the nearby ortho sites is sterically hindered (physically blocked by the size of the group), so the para product forms in greater amount even though both are electronically accessible.

vocab.steric hindrance: the physical crowding that occurs when large groups are close together in space, raising the energy of the transition state for reactions at nearby positions
vocab.predominate: to be formed in the larger proportion of the product mixture

solution_steps.do_this: Draw the explicit structures of each predicted product and label which is the major product, connecting every structural feature back to the directing and steric analysis just completed
solution_steps.why: The rule is: a complete answer to a regiochemistry problem must show both the connectivity of the new bond and the spatial relationship to the existing substituent, because two products with bromine in different positions are constitutionally distinct molecules (constitutional isomers) with different physical and chemical properties. Labeling the major product demonstrates mastery of steric versus electronic competition.

vocab.constitutional isomers: molecules sharing the same molecular formula but differing in which atoms are bonded to which — ortho-bromotoluene and para-bromotoluene are constitutional isomers of each other

question_text_raw:
18.62CompoundAand compoundBare both aromatic esters with
the molecular formula C8H8O2. When treated with bromine in the
presence of iron tribromide, compoundAis converted into only one
product, while compoundBis converted into two different monobro-
mination products. Identify the structure of compoundAand com-
poundB.
solution_text_raw:
18.62.The molecular formula indicates five degrees of
unsaturation, which accounts for the aromatic ring (four
degrees) and the ester group (one degree). The aromatic
ring accounts for six of the eight carbon atoms, so the
ester group can only have two carbon atoms.The

question_analysis.skill_tested: Determine the structure of an aromatic ester by counting degrees of unsaturation and predicting the number of electrophilic aromatic substitution products based on ring symmetry and substituent directing effects
question_analysis.disguise: The problem appears to be about identifying unknowns from a reaction outcome, but the real task is reasoning backward from the number of bromination products to the symmetry of the aromatic ring, which reveals the substituent arrangement
question_analysis.recognition_cue: When a problem states that one aromatic compound gives exactly ONE monosubstitution product while a structural isomer gives TWO, the single-product compound must have a ring so symmetric that all open positions are equivalent — this points to a para-disubstituted benzene ring

prior_knowledge_needed: Degrees of unsaturation (also called index of hydrogen deficiency): each double bond or ring contributes one degree; formula = (2C + 2 + N - H - X) / 2
A benzene ring contributes four degrees of unsaturation (three double bonds plus one ring)
An ester group (–C(=O)–O–) contributes one degree of unsaturation from its carbonyl double bond
Electrophilic aromatic substitution (EAS): a reaction where an electrophile, here Br+, replaces a hydrogen on a benzene ring
Ortho/para directors vs. meta directors: electron-donating groups steer incoming electrophiles to positions ortho and para to themselves; electron-withdrawing groups steer to meta
Ring symmetry and equivalent positions: if two ring positions are mirror images of each other (homotopic), they give the same substitution product, reducing the count of distinct products
Structural isomers of a disubstituted benzene: ortho (1,2-), meta (1,3-), and para (1,4-) arrangements each have different numbers of unique open positions

solution_steps.do_this: Calculate degrees of unsaturation for C8H8O2 and assign them to functional groups present
solution_steps.why: The rule is: degrees of unsaturation constrain which combinations of rings, double bonds, and triple bonds are possible, so tallying them first eliminates impossible structures. Applying the formula gives (2×8 + 2 − 8) / 2 = 5 degrees. A benzene ring accounts for 4, and an ester carbonyl accounts for 1, exhausting all 5. This means the molecule is a benzene ring with a single ester group attached and nothing else unsaturated.

vocab.degrees of unsaturation: a count of how many rings or pi bonds (double/triple bonds) a molecule contains, calculated from its molecular formula

solution_steps.do_this: Determine how the eight carbons must be distributed between the benzene ring and the ester fragment
solution_steps.why: The rule is: the benzene ring always contains exactly six carbons, so the remaining carbons belong entirely to the substituents. With 8 total carbons minus 6 for benzene, exactly 2 carbons remain for the ester group. An ester (–C(=O)–O–) connecting two carbons can only be arranged as either a methyl ester on a methyl-substituted ring (CH3–C6H4–COOCH3 is impossible here since that needs 8 carbons total but has two substituents) or as phenyl acetate (a benzene ring with an –O–C(=O)–CH3 group attached, using 6 + 1 + 1 = 8 carbons) or methyl benzoate (a benzene ring with a –C(=O)–OCH3 group, also 6 + 1 + 1 = 8 carbons). These are the two candidate parent skeletons.

vocab.ester group: a functional group with the connectivity –C(=O)–O–, formed from a carboxylic acid and an alcohol

solution_steps.do_this: For each candidate skeleton, enumerate how many structurally distinct open ring positions exist for electrophilic bromination
solution_steps.why: The rule is: the number of distinct monosubstitution products equals the number of sets of equivalent ring positions. Methyl benzoate has one substituent (–COOCH3) on the ring; the ring has three sets of positions — two ortho, two meta, and one para — yielding up to three distinct products. Phenyl acetate (Ph–O–CO–CH3) also has one substituent on the ring, so it similarly has three sets. Neither of these alone explains a single-product outcome. Therefore the two-carbon ester fragment must be split across two separate positions on the ring, creating a disubstituted benzene. The only disubstituted arrangement using two one-carbon fragments that gives only ONE EAS product is the para isomer (1,4-substitution), because all four remaining ring hydrogens are equivalent by symmetry. A meta isomer would give three distinct products, and an ortho isomer would give two.

vocab.monosubstitution product: the compound formed when exactly one hydrogen on the benzene ring is replaced by the incoming group
vocab.equivalent positions: ring sites that are interchangeable by the molecule's symmetry, so substitution at any of them gives the same product

solution_steps.do_this: Assign compound A as the para-disubstituted isomer and compound B as the ortho- or meta-disubstituted isomer, then verify with directing effects
solution_steps.why: The rule is: a para-disubstituted benzene ring has all four open positions equivalent, so EAS always yields exactly one product regardless of which substituent directs. This matches compound A's behavior. For compound B giving two products, either the ortho isomer (which has two pairs of equivalent positions, yielding two products) or the meta isomer fits. Given that the ester oxygen in phenyl acetate is electron-donating (ortho/para director) and the carbonyl carbon in a benzoate ester is electron-withdrawing (meta director), the methyl group and ester oxygen in a cresyl ester arrangement would further constrain regioselectivity. The simplest consistent assignment is: Compound A = methyl para-hydroxybenzoate rearranged as para-substituted phenyl acetate (4-methylphenyl formate is not right) — specifically Compound A is para-methoxybenzaldehyde's ester analog. Concretely: Compound A is methyl 4-methylbenzoate is wrong carbons. Returning to the carbon count: the two one-carbon units are –OCH3 and –C(=O)– attached directly to the ring, meaning Compound A is methyl para-aminobenzoate analog without nitrogen. The correct structures are: Compound A = para-methoxymethyl benzoate? No. With 6 ring carbons + CO + OCH3: that is methyl benzoate (one substituent, 3 product sets) not one product. The correct reading is the two carbons are a –CH3 and a –COOH-derived ester giving a methylbenzoate or a cresyl formate. For C8H8O2 with a disubstituted ring: –CH3 (1C) + –OCHO (1C) = methyl and formate, or –CH3 (1C) + –COOCH3 needs 2C leaving only 0 for the other, impossible. So the split must be one substituent carrying both carbons as one group (–COOCH3 = methyl ester, 2C) on a monosubstituted ring, OR two substituents each with 1C (–CH3 and –OCHO). The disubstituted route with –CH3 and –OCHO: para gives one product (Compound A), ortho gives two products (Compound B). Compound A = para-cresyl formate is not right either since formate is H–C(=O)–O–. Actually –OCHO on ring means phenol ester: ring–O–CHO. So Compound A = 4-methylphenyl formate (para-cresol formate ester) giving one EAS product, and Compound B = 2-methylphenyl formate (ortho-cresol formate ester) giving two EAS products. Both have formula C8H8O2 and are aromatic esters. This perfectly satisfies all constraints.

vocab.ortho/para director: a substituent that, through electron donation, makes the ring positions ortho and para to itself more reactive toward electrophiles
vocab.para-disubstituted benzene: a benzene ring with two substituents located directly across from each other at positions 1 and 4, creating a highly symmetric ring

solution_steps.do_this: Confirm the final assignments by checking symmetry and product counts explicitly for each structure
solution_steps.why: The rule is: verification of symmetry is mandatory before accepting a structural assignment, because incorrect symmetry reasoning is the most common error in this type of problem. 4-Methylphenyl formate (para arrangement): the ring has –CH3 at C1 and –OCHO at C4; the four open positions are C2, C3, C5, C6 — positions C2 and C6 are equivalent (both ortho to CH3 and meta to OCHO), and C3 and C5 are equivalent (both meta to CH3 and ortho to OCHO), yielding exactly two sets. Wait — that gives TWO products, not one. Therefore the para isomer is actually Compound B, and we must reconsider. The isomer giving ONE product must be the one where all open positions are equivalent — this happens only when BOTH substituents are identical or when the molecule has a mirror plane making all open positions equivalent. For a 1,4-disubstituted ring with two DIFFERENT groups, there are two pairs of equivalent positions → two products. For Compound A to give one product, it must be a monosubstituted benzene where the single substituent is symmetric and the ring positions collapse to one unique set — impossible for a standard monosubstituted ring (which always has ortho, meta, para sets). The resolution: Compound A must be methyl benzoate (one –COOCH3 group) if the iron/bromine conditions and the strong meta-directing ester group make only the meta product form in practice — but that would still be one major product at meta. Actually in strict regiochemical terms, a monosubstituted ring gives up to 3 products, but a strongly meta-directing group with two equivalent meta positions gives predominantly one product. A strongly ortho/para directing group can give up to two products (ortho + para). So Compound A (one product) = methyl benzoate where the –COOCH3 group is a strong meta director, and both meta positions are equivalent, yielding exclusively the meta-brominated product. Compound B (two products) = phenyl acetate (Ph–O–CO–CH3) where the –O– attached to the ring is a strong ortho/para director, generating both ortho-bromophenyl acetate and para-bromophenyl acetate as two distinct products.

vocab.meta director: a substituent that withdraws electron density from the ring and thereby makes the meta positions (two positions away) the most reactive sites for electrophilic attack
vocab.phenyl acetate: an ester in which the oxygen of a phenol is connected to an acetyl group (CH3C=O), written as C6H5–O–CO–CH3

question_analysis.skill_tested: Design a multi-step aromatic synthesis by determining the correct order of electrophilic substitutions based on the directing effects and activation/deactivation of existing ring substituents
question_analysis.disguise: The problem presents four different target molecules, each requiring the student to reverse-engineer a synthetic sequence rather than simply predict a single reaction product
question_analysis.recognition_cue: A target aromatic ring has two or more substituents whose relative positions (ortho/meta/para to each other) must be traced back to the directing logic of whatever group was on the ring FIRST — the order of steps is the puzzle

prior_knowledge_needed: Ortho-para directors (electron-donating groups like -OCH3 and alkyl groups) activate the ring and steer incoming electrophiles to the ortho and para positions
Meta directors (electron-withdrawing groups like -NO2 and -COOH) deactivate the ring and steer incoming electrophiles to the meta position
Steric hindrance near a bulky substituent disfavors ortho attack, so para products often predominate over ortho even when both positions are electronically equivalent
Benzylic oxidation: a strong oxidizing agent (e.g., KMnO4) converts an alkyl side-chain attached directly to a benzene ring into a carboxylic acid (-COOH)
A blocking group (e.g., a sulfonyl group) can temporarily occupy the para position to force an electrophile to the ortho position instead, and can be removed later
Bromination of an activated ring (one with an electron-donating substituent) proceeds without a Lewis acid catalyst; deactivated rings require a Lewis acid

solution_steps.do_this: Identify every substituent in the target molecule and classify each one as an ortho-para director or a meta director
solution_steps.why: The rule is: the directing effect of a group already on the ring completely controls where the NEXT group lands. Knowing the directing class of each substituent in the product tells you what the ring must have looked like at each earlier stage of the synthesis — this is the master constraint that fixes step order.

vocab.ortho-para director: a substituent that donates electron density into the ring (e.g., -OCH3, alkyl), activating it and channeling incoming electrophiles to the positions two and four atoms away from itself
vocab.meta director: a substituent that withdraws electron density from the ring (e.g., -NO2, -COOH), deactivating it and channeling incoming electrophiles to the position three atoms away

solution_steps.do_this: Check whether the relative positions of the two substituents in the target are consistent with the directing effect of the substituent that would be installed first; if not, swap the order or introduce a blocking group
solution_steps.why: The rule is: a reagent adds where the ring directs it, not where you wish it to go. For parts (a) and (b), the -OCH3 group is already present and is an ortho-para director, so whichever new group must end up para is installed first while the ring is still unencumbered. For parts (c) and (d), the isopropyl group (ortho-para director) must be used to place the nitro group correctly BEFORE oxidizing it to -COOH (which would become a meta director and place the nitro group in the wrong spot).

vocab.electrophilic aromatic substitution (EAS: a reaction in which an electron-rich aromatic ring attacks an electrophile, replacing one ring hydrogen with a new substituent
vocab.benzylic oxidation: conversion of a carbon attached directly to an aromatic ring into a carboxylic acid using a strong oxidant such as KMnO4

solution_steps.do_this: For the isopropylbenzene targets (parts c and d), decide whether the nitro group and the carboxylic acid are meta to each other or ortho/para to each other in the product, because this determines whether oxidation or nitration must come first
solution_steps.why: The rule is: you must exploit the directing effect that gives you the correct regiochemistry (positional selectivity — control over which ring position reacts), so you must keep the ortho-para-directing alkyl group intact until the nitro group is in the right place. In part (d) the two groups are meta to each other, so oxidizing first then nitrating works perfectly — the new -COOH is a meta director and places -NO2 exactly where needed. In part (c) the two groups are ortho to each other, so nitrating first (using the isopropyl group's ortho-para direction) is required; a blocking group at the para position steers the nitro group to the ortho position specifically.

vocab.regiochemistry: which specific position on a molecule reacts when more than one position is possible
vocab.blocking group: a substituent temporarily attached to a ring position to prevent reaction there, then removed after the desired substitution elsewhere is complete

solution_steps.do_this: Account for steric effects when both ortho and para positions are available: predict that the para product will predominate unless the para position is blocked, and design the synthesis accordingly
solution_steps.why: The rule is: steric hindrance (the physical crowding caused by nearby atoms) disfavors attack at a position adjacent to a large group, so electrophiles preferentially land at the less hindered para position. In part (a), bromination of anisole gives predominantly the para-bromo product, which is exactly the regiochemistry needed — so bromination is the correct first step. In part (b), the desired bromine position is ortho to -OCH3 in the final product, so nitration (which places -NO2 para) must come first, leaving the ortho positions available for subsequent bromination directed ortho to -OCH3.

vocab.steric hindrance: the spatial crowding around a reactive site caused by nearby bulky atoms or groups, which slows or blocks attack at that position

solution_steps.do_this: Write out the final synthesis sequence for each target as an ordered list of reagents, verifying at each step that the group already on the ring directs the incoming electrophile to the correct position
solution_steps.why: The rule is: each intermediate must have substituents whose combined directing effects are consistent with the next step's regiochemical outcome. A final verification step catches errors where a reasonable-sounding order would actually place a substituent in the wrong position.

vocab.intermediate: a compound formed during a synthesis that is not the final target but is transformed further in subsequent steps
vocab.nitration: introduction of a nitro group (-NO2) onto an aromatic ring using a mixture of concentrated nitric and sulfuric acids
vocab.bromination: introduction of a bromine atom onto an aromatic ring using Br2, with or without a Lewis acid catalyst depending on ring activation
