question_analysis.skill_tested: Predict the site of electrophilic aromatic substitution by identifying the strongest directing group and mapping its ortho/para or meta preference onto available ring positions
question_analysis.disguise: Multiple substituents are already on the ring, so the student must rank competing directors and account for steric hindrance and occupied positions rather than simply applying one rule
question_analysis.recognition_cue: A benzene ring with two or more existing substituents plus an electrophilic reagent (Cl2/AlCl3, HNO3/H2SO4, fuming H2SO4) — the need to adjudicate between conflicting directing groups signals a director-hierarchy problem

prior_knowledge_needed: Electrophilic aromatic substitution (EAS) mechanism: an electrophile attacks the pi system of benzene, forming a carbocation intermediate, then a proton is lost to restore aromaticity

Activating vs. deactivating substituents: groups that donate electron density into the ring stabilize the carbocation intermediate and speed reaction; groups that withdraw electron density destabilize it and slow reaction

Ortho/para directors vs. meta directors: electron-donating groups and halogens direct incoming electrophiles to ortho and para positions; strongly electron-withdrawing groups (nitro, carbonyl, ester) direct to the meta position
Director hierarchy rule: when multiple substituents compete, the most strongly activating group overrides the others and controls regioselectivity
Steric hindrance: large groups or substituents already occupying adjacent (ortho) positions block attack there, funneling reaction to the para position or the less hindered ortho position
Sulfonation with fuming sulfuric acid installs a sulfonic acid group (–SO3H) via the same EAS logic as other electrophilic substitutions

solution_steps.do_this: Identify every substituent on the ring and classify each one as an activator or deactivator, and as an ortho/para director or meta director
solution_steps.why: The rule is: the electronic character of a substituent determines which positions of the ring have elevated electron density; only by cataloguing all substituents can you know which electronic environments are competing. Halogens are weak deactivators but ortho/para directors because lone pairs donate into the ring via resonance despite sigma withdrawal. Ester and nitro groups are meta directors because their carbonyl/nitrogen withdraws electron density from ortho and para positions. Alkoxy (–OCH3) and hydroxyl (–OH) groups are strong activators and ortho/para directors.

vocab.activator: a substituent that increases electron density in the benzene ring, making EAS faster
vocab.deactivator: a substituent that decreases electron density in the benzene ring, making EAS slower
vocab.ortho/para director: a substituent that concentrates electron density at the positions two and four bonds away from itself on the ring
vocab.meta director: a substituent that leaves the positions three bonds away from itself as the least electron-poor, directing attack there

solution_steps.do_this: Apply the director hierarchy: locate the most strongly activating group (or, if no activators exist, the least deactivating ortho/para director) and treat its directional preference as controlling
solution_steps.why: The rule is: the substituent that most stabilizes the carbocation intermediate (arenium ion) exerts dominant control over regioselectivity. A strongly activating group such as –OH or –OCH3 overwhelms a weakly deactivating halogen or a meta-directing ester. If no activator is present, the ortho/para-directing halogen overrides meta-directing ester groups because it at least channels electron density to ortho/para positions rather than away from them.

vocab.arenium ion: the positively charged, non-aromatic carbocation intermediate formed when an electrophile bonds to one carbon of the benzene ring
vocab.regioselectivity: the preference for reaction to occur at one specific position on the ring rather than another

solution_steps.do_this: Map the ortho and para positions of the controlling director onto the ring and cross off any that are already occupied by existing substituents
solution_steps.why: The rule is: a carbon that already bears a substituent cannot accept a second one in EAS; substitution can only occur at unoccupied ring carbons. After eliminating occupied sites, the remaining open ortho or para position(s) become the predicted site(s) of substitution.

vocab.ortho position: the ring carbon immediately adjacent (next door) to the substituent, two bonds away
vocab.para position: the ring carbon directly across the ring from the substituent, four bonds away along the shorter path

solution_steps.do_this: Evaluate steric hindrance at each remaining candidate position: if an open ortho site is flanked by bulky groups, deprioritize it in favor of the para position or a less crowded ortho site
solution_steps.why: The rule is: the transition state for EAS requires the electrophile to approach the ring face near the target carbon; large neighboring groups raise the energy of that approach. Therefore, even an electronically favored ortho position may be kinetically inaccessible if adjacent substituents create steric congestion, and the para position becomes the major product.

vocab.steric hindrance: physical bulk of nearby atoms that blocks or slows a reagent from reaching a particular site

solution_steps.do_this: Draw the product by attaching the new electrophile (Cl, NO2, SO3H, or Br) to the identified position and verify consistency with both the dominant director and any secondary directors
solution_steps.why: The rule is: the correct product must satisfy the directional preferences of all substituents simultaneously whenever possible. Checking that the chosen position is ortho/para to the dominant activator AND not meta to a secondary director (or at worst meta to only the deactivators) confirms the assignment. If the dominant and secondary directors point to the same position, confidence in that product is highest.

vocab.electrophile: an electron-seeking species (here Cl+, NO2+, SO3, or Br+) that attacks the electron-rich aromatic ring

question_analysis.skill_tested: Identify the correct acyl chloride and aromatic starting material for a Friedel-Crafts acylation by retrosynthetically disconnecting the carbonyl-to-ring bond and then verifying that the remaining substituents on the ring are compatible with the reaction conditions.
question_analysis.disguise: The problem presents a finished product and asks you to work backward, which hides the need to (1) choose which C–C bond to disconnect retrosynthetically and (2) check whether the resulting aromatic fragment is reactive enough to undergo Friedel-Crafts conditions.
question_analysis.recognition_cue: A ketone where the carbonyl carbon is directly attached to an aromatic ring, with other substituents already present on that ring — this pattern signals: disconnect the acyl–arene bond and test each fragment for Friedel-Crafts feasibility.

prior_knowledge_needed: Friedel-Crafts acylation mechanism: a Lewis acid activates an acyl chloride to form an acylium electrophile that attacks an electron-rich aromatic ring
Friedel-Crafts acylation fails on strongly deactivated aromatic rings (e.g., rings bearing nitro groups), because the ring is too electron-poor to attack the electrophile
Retrosynthetic disconnection: mentally breaking a bond in the product to reveal what starting materials would re-form it in the forward reaction
Directing effects: electron-donating groups (methoxy, alkyl) are ortho/para directors and activate the ring; electron-withdrawing groups (nitro) are meta directors and deactivate the ring
Steric bias: even when two positions are electronically equivalent, the less hindered position reacts faster, so para substitution usually dominates over ortho when a large group is present

solution_steps.do_this: Locate every bond between the carbonyl carbon and an aromatic ring in the target molecule, then list each as a candidate disconnection point.
solution_steps.why: The rule is: a Friedel-Crafts acylation always forms exactly one new sigma bond between an acylium electrophile and an aromatic carbon. Therefore, each such bond in the product represents a possible retrosynthetic cut, and you must evaluate all of them before committing to one pathway.

vocab.retrosynthetic disconnection: mentally breaking a bond in the target molecule to reveal which simpler starting materials could produce it in the forward direction
vocab.acylium electrophile: a positively charged carbon species of the form R-C≡O⁺ formed when a Lewis acid removes chloride from an acyl chloride

solution_steps.do_this: For each candidate disconnection, identify the aromatic fragment that would serve as the substrate and assess whether it is activated or deactivated toward electrophilic aromatic substitution.
solution_steps.why: The rule is: Friedel-Crafts acylation requires the aromatic ring to be at least moderately electron-rich. A ring carrying a strongly electron-withdrawing group (such as a nitro group) is so deactivated that the reaction will not proceed, eliminating that disconnection as a viable synthetic route.

vocab.electrophilic aromatic substitution: a reaction class where an electron-rich aromatic ring attacks an electrophile, replacing a ring hydrogen with the new group
vocab.deactivated ring: an aromatic ring made electron-poor by attached electron-withdrawing groups, making it unreactive toward electrophiles

solution_steps.do_this: Select the disconnection whose aromatic fragment is activated (bears electron-donating groups), then write the corresponding acyl chloride by re-attaching a chloride to the carbonyl carbon of the disconnected acyl portion.
solution_steps.why: The rule is: only the feasible retrosynthetic pathway — the one producing a reactive aromatic substrate — maps onto a real forward reaction. Once that pathway is chosen, the acyl chloride is simply the acyl fragment with a Cl replacing the bond that was cut.

vocab.acyl chloride: a compound of the form R-C(=O)-Cl, used as the electrophilic acylating agent in Friedel-Crafts acylation
vocab.electron-donating group: a substituent (like methoxy or alkyl) that pushes electron density into the aromatic ring, activating it toward electrophilic attack

solution_steps.do_this: Use the directing effects and steric bias of the substituents already on the chosen aromatic fragment to confirm that electrophilic attack will occur at the correct position to give the desired regiochemistry in the product.
solution_steps.why: The rule is: the position where electrophilic substitution occurs is governed by the substituents already on the ring — activating groups direct to ortho and para positions, and among those, the less hindered para position usually dominates. Confirming this match ensures the chosen starting materials will actually produce the correct product, not a regioisomeric by-product.

vocab.regiochemistry: which specific position on the ring the new group attaches to
vocab.ortho-para director: a substituent that channels incoming electrophiles to the positions immediately adjacent to it (ortho) or directly across the ring (para)

question_analysis.skill_tested: Design a multi-step synthesis from benzene by sequencing electrophilic aromatic substitution reactions in the correct order based on directing effects, reactivity constraints, and steric considerations
question_analysis.disguise: The problem presents a finished target molecule with multiple substituents, forcing the student to work backward from the product and figure out which group to install first, second, and last — a non-obvious ordering problem
question_analysis.recognition_cue: A polysubstituted benzene ring where two or more substituents are meta to each other, or where one substituent is an ortho-para director and another is a meta director — this pattern demands retrosynthetic analysis to find the correct installation sequence

prior_knowledge_needed: Ortho-para directors (substituents that push electron density into the ring and direct incoming electrophiles to ortho and para positions) vs. meta directors (electron-withdrawing substituents that direct incoming electrophiles to the meta position)

Friedel-Crafts acylation and alkylation: Lewis-acid-catalyzed reactions that attach acyl or alkyl groups to a benzene ring, and why strongly deactivated rings (e.g., nitrobenzene) cannot undergo these reactions

Oxidation of benzylic positions: a side-chain carbon attached directly to an aromatic ring can be oxidized to a carboxylic acid using a strong oxidant like KMnO4, so an alkyl group can serve as a COOH precursor

Steric hindrance control of regiochemistry: a bulky substituent (e.g., isopropyl) blocks ortho positions so that the next incoming electrophile is forced to the para position, giving better regiochemical control than a small group like methyl

SNAr (nucleophilic aromatic substitution): a reaction in which a nucleophile displaces a leaving group on an electron-poor aromatic ring — requires strong electron-withdrawing groups ortho or para to the leaving group to activate the ring toward nucleophilic attack
Retrosynthetic analysis: working backward from a target molecule by identifying which bond was formed last and what simpler precursor that implies

solution_steps.do_this: Identify every substituent on the target ring and classify each one as an ortho-para director, a meta director, or a group that must be generated indirectly (e.g., COOH from oxidation of an alkyl group)
solution_steps.why: The rule is: the directing group already on the ring at the time of each new substitution step controls where the next group lands. Knowing which groups are directors and which are not tells you which group must be present on the ring during each substitution step, and which group can only be installed after everything else is in place.

vocab.ortho-para director: a substituent that donates electron density into the benzene ring, making the ortho and para positions more reactive toward electrophiles
vocab.meta director: a substituent that withdraws electron density from the benzene ring, making the meta positions relatively less deactivated and therefore the site of attack

solution_steps.do_this: Work retrosynthetically — starting from the final target, ask which substituent was installed last and what the precursor ring looked like before that final step, then repeat for each earlier step
solution_steps.why: The rule is: a meta-directing group deactivates the ring so strongly that Friedel-Crafts reactions cannot occur afterward. Therefore, any Friedel-Crafts acylation or alkylation step must precede installation of any nitro group or other strong deactivator. Identifying this constraint first fixes the overall ordering of steps.

vocab.retrosynthetic analysis: a problem-solving strategy in which you mentally disconnect the target molecule into simpler precursors, working backward step by step until you reach available starting materials
vocab.benzylic oxidation: converting a carbon attached directly to an aromatic ring into a carboxylic acid group using a strong oxidant, so an alkyl substituent can serve as a masked COOH group

solution_steps.do_this: Evaluate steric considerations when choosing alkyl group size — decide whether a small group (methyl) or a bulky group (isopropyl) is needed to achieve acceptable regiochemical selectivity in subsequent substitution steps
solution_steps.why: The rule is: small substituents like methyl provide little steric differentiation between ortho and para positions, giving mixtures of regioisomers. A bulky group like isopropyl physically blocks the nearby ortho positions and forces the next electrophile almost exclusively to the para position. Choosing the right group size is therefore a strategic decision, not cosmetic.

vocab.regiochemical selectivity: the preference for a reaction to occur at one specific position on a molecule rather than at other possible positions
vocab.steric hindrance: the physical blockage of a reactive site by nearby large groups, which slows or prevents reaction at that position

solution_steps.do_this: For any target that requires an oxygen nucleophile (alkoxy group) on the ring, decide whether to use a direct electrophilic route or an SNAr route, based on whether strong electron-withdrawing groups are present ortho or para to the leaving group
solution_steps.why: The rule is: SNAr requires the ring to be strongly electron-poor at the carbon bearing the leaving group, which is achieved by having nitro groups ortho or para to it. If those activating groups for SNAr are present, a chlorine atom can be displaced by an alkoxide nucleophile. If they are absent, direct electrophilic methods or conversion of a phenol are needed instead.

vocab.SNAr (nucleophilic aromatic substitution: a two-step mechanism in which a nucleophile adds to an electron-poor aromatic ring bearing a leaving group, forming a negatively charged intermediate called a Meisenheimer complex, followed by expulsion of the leaving group
vocab.Meisenheimer complex: the negatively charged intermediate formed when a nucleophile adds to an aromatic ring during SNAr, stabilized by adjacent electron-withdrawing groups

solution_steps.do_this: Assemble the complete forward synthesis by listing each reagent set in the confirmed order, then verify by mentally tracing the directing effects at each stage to confirm that the major product at every step matches the connectivity in the target molecule
solution_steps.why: The rule is: each intermediate in a synthesis has its own directing profile, and that profile — not the original starting material — controls where the next group goes. Checking each intermediate independently catches ordering errors that retrosynthetic logic alone might miss, such as a step where all remaining open positions are equivalent or where an unexpected group is activated.

vocab.directing profile: the combined set of regiochemical instructions that all substituents currently on a ring give to an incoming electrophile, which may reinforce each other or conflict depending on their positions

question_analysis.skill_tested: Predict the single major trialkylation product of benzene by tracking how each successive methyl group installation is directed by steric and electronic effects of existing substituents
question_analysis.disguise: The problem looks like a simple 'add three methyls' exercise, but the real challenge is recognizing that after each alkylation step the directing effects narrow the outcome to one dominant regiochemical (positional) result rather than a mixture
question_analysis.recognition_cue: When you see benzene + alkyl halide + Lewis acid catalyst + 'conditions favoring trialkylation,' the expert flag is: each round of alkylation is governed by the combined directing effects of ALL methyl groups already on the ring, and steric bulk eliminates symmetry-equivalent positions from competition

prior_knowledge_needed: Friedel-Crafts alkylation mechanism: a Lewis acid such as AlCl3 activates an alkyl halide to form a carbocation-like electrophile, which then attacks the pi system of benzene

Electrophilic aromatic substitution (EAS) regioselectivity: alkyl groups are ortho/para directors because they donate electron density to those positions, making them more reactive toward electrophiles
Definition of ortho, meta, para positions relative to a substituent on a benzene ring
Steric effects in EAS: bulky groups already on the ring physically block nearby positions, biasing substitution toward less hindered sites even when electronics would permit multiple locations

How to combine directing effects from two substituents: when two alkyl groups already exist, every open position is evaluated for whether it is ortho or para to each group, and positions that are ortho/para to BOTH groups are most activated

solution_steps.do_this: Identify what reagent system is present and what single structural change it performs each time it reacts with the ring
solution_steps.why: The rule is: AlCl3 activates an alkyl halide to generate an electrophilic (electron-seeking) carbon species; that species installs exactly one alkyl group per reaction event. Recognizing this one-group-per-event principle sets up the stepwise tracking that follows.

vocab.Friedel-Crafts alkylation: a reaction in which an alkyl group is attached to a benzene ring using an alkyl halide and a Lewis acid catalyst such as aluminum trichloride
vocab.Lewis acid: a substance that accepts a pair of electrons; AlCl3 acts this way by grabbing the chloride from the alkyl chloride and generating a highly electrophilic carbon

solution_steps.do_this: Track the first and second alkylation steps to determine which disubstituted (two-methyl) intermediate accumulates before the third methyl is added
solution_steps.why: The rule is: ortho/para directors steer the next incoming electrophile to positions ortho and para to themselves. After the first methyl arrives, toluene (methylbenzene) forms; the second methyl is directed ortho or para to that first methyl. Ortho attack gives 1,2-dimethylbenzene and para attack gives 1,4-dimethylbenzene; the meta (1,3-) isomer is electronically disfavored and essentially not produced. Both ortho and para xylenes therefore accumulate as the mixture entering the third step.

vocab.ortho/para director: a substituent attached to a benzene ring that donates electron density preferentially to the positions two and four bonds away, making those sites faster to react with electrophiles
vocab.xylene: a dimethylbenzene; the three isomers are named by the relationship of the two methyl groups: 1,2- (ortho), 1,3- (meta), and 1,4- (para)

solution_steps.do_this: Apply combined directing and steric analysis to the two xylene intermediates separately to show that BOTH converge on the same trimethylbenzene
solution_steps.why: The rule is: when two substituents are already attached, every open position on the ring is assessed against BOTH directors simultaneously, and steric crowding further filters the options. For 1,2-dimethylbenzene (ortho-xylene), positions 3 and 4 are open; position 3 is ortho to one methyl and meta to the other, while position 4 is para to one methyl and ortho to the other — making position 4 electronically favored; additionally, position 3 sits between two bulky methyls (sterically congested), so position 4 wins. For 1,4-dimethylbenzene (para-xylene), all four open positions are equivalent by symmetry, and each is ortho to one methyl and meta to the other — yet the ortho relationship still provides activation, and all attacks lead to the identical product. Both pathways install the third methyl at the position that becomes carbon 4 of the final product, yielding 1,2,4-trimethylbenzene.

vocab.steric congestion: physical crowding near a reaction site caused by large nearby groups, which slows or prevents a reagent from approaching that site
vocab.regiochemistry: the specific location on a molecule where a reaction occurs when multiple sites are possible

solution_steps.do_this: Name the single major product using IUPAC rules and confirm its structure
solution_steps.why: The rule is: IUPAC names for polysubstituted benzenes assign the lowest possible set of locant numbers to substituents; three methyl groups attached at ring carbons 1, 2, and 4 (with the numbering chosen to minimize the locant set) gives 1,2,4-trimethylbenzene. This compound is the one major product because both xylene intermediates funnel into it, meaning steric and electronic factors reinforce rather than oppose each other across both pathways.

vocab.locant: a number in an IUPAC name that tells you which carbon of the ring bears a particular substituent
vocab.1,2,4-trimethylbenzene: a benzene ring with three methyl groups attached at adjacent carbon 1, the next carbon 2, and then carbon 4; also known by the trivial name pseudocumene

question_analysis.skill_tested: Predict the major product of electrophilic aromatic bromination by resolving competing directing effects and applying steric reasoning to choose between two viable positions
question_analysis.disguise: The problem looks like a simple substitution question, but the real challenge is recognizing that two substituents both direct to the same positions, then using sterics to break the tie between the two remaining open sites
question_analysis.recognition_cue: A disubstituted benzene ring where one substituent is an alkyl group (electron-donating, ortho/para director) and the other is a carbonyl-containing group (electron-withdrawing, meta director) — whenever both groups point to overlapping positions, steric bulk at the crowded site determines which product predominates

prior_knowledge_needed: Electrophilic aromatic substitution (EAS): a reaction where an electrophile replaces a hydrogen atom on an aromatic ring, driven by the ring's pi electron density
Activating vs. deactivating substituents: groups already on the ring that either increase (activators) or decrease (deactivators) the ring's electron density toward an incoming electrophile

Ortho/para directors vs. meta directors: activating groups steer the incoming electrophile to the ortho (adjacent) and para (across the ring) positions; deactivating groups — except halogens — steer it to the meta position
When two substituents are present, the more strongly activating group controls the regiochemistry (positional outcome)
Steric hindrance: bulky groups near a reaction site physically block approach of the incoming electrophile, making that position less reactive regardless of electronic preference

solution_steps.do_this: Identify the two substituents on the benzene ring and classify each as an activator or deactivator
solution_steps.why: The rule is: every substituent attached to a benzene ring either donates or withdraws electron density, and this classification directly predicts which ring positions are enriched enough to attract the electrophilic bromine species. An alkyl group (a carbon chain with no electron-withdrawing features) is a weak activator through hyperconjugation (stabilization of positive charge by adjacent C–H bonds). A carbonyl group (a carbon double-bonded to oxygen) is a moderate deactivator because oxygen pulls electron density away from the ring through resonance (delocalization of electrons across connected p-orbitals).

vocab.activator: a substituent that increases electron density on the aromatic ring, making it more reactive toward electrophiles
vocab.deactivator: a substituent that decreases electron density on the aromatic ring, making it less reactive toward electrophiles
vocab.hyperconjugation: stabilization arising from overlap between a filled C–H bonding orbital and an adjacent empty or partially empty orbital, here donating slight electron density into the ring

solution_steps.do_this: Determine which substituent controls regiochemistry and map out the positions it directs the electrophile toward
solution_steps.why: The rule is: when two substituents give conflicting or overlapping direction, the more strongly activating group overrides the weaker one in setting regioselectivity (control over which position on the ring reacts). The alkyl group is the activator here, so it controls. As an ortho/para director, it points the incoming electrophile to the two ortho positions (directly adjacent, one on each side) and the one para position (directly across the ring). However, one ortho position is already occupied by the other substituent, leaving only one ortho site and the para site as viable targets.

vocab.regiochemistry: which specific position on the molecule undergoes reaction when more than one position is possible
vocab.ortho position: a ring carbon directly adjacent (one bond away) to the substituent
vocab.para position: the ring carbon directly across the ring from the substituent, separated by two carbons on each side

solution_steps.do_this: Check whether the directing effects of both substituents are cooperative (point to the same sites) or antagonistic (point to different sites)
solution_steps.why: The rule is: when two substituents on the same ring both favor the same positions, those positions receive a double reinforcement of reactivity, making them strongly preferred. The carbonyl group is a meta director, meaning it directs to the position two carbons away from itself. Because of the relative placement of the two substituents on the ring, the meta positions relative to the carbonyl group coincide with the ortho and para positions relative to the alkyl group. Both groups therefore cooperate, confirming that only the two positions already identified (one ortho to alkyl, one para to alkyl) are electronically viable.

vocab.meta position: the ring carbon two bonds away from the substituent, neither directly adjacent nor directly across
vocab.cooperative directing: a situation where two substituents on the same ring independently favor the same set of positions, reinforcing each other's influence

solution_steps.do_this: Apply steric reasoning to choose between the remaining ortho site and the para site
solution_steps.why: The rule is: when two positions are electronically equivalent (both activated to the same degree), the electrophile preferentially attacks the less sterically hindered (less physically blocked) site. The ortho position is flanked on one side by the alkyl substituent and on the other by the carbonyl substituent — it is sandwiched between two groups. The para position has neither substituent directly beside it and is therefore much more open. Steric crowding at the ortho site raises the energy of the transition state (the highest-energy point along the reaction pathway) for attack there, so bromination occurs predominantly at the para position relative to the alkyl group.

vocab.steric hindrance: physical blocking of a reaction site by bulky groups attached nearby, which raises the activation energy for reaction at that site
vocab.transition state: the highest-energy, unstable arrangement of atoms that must be passed through as reactants convert to products

solution_steps.do_this: State the structure of the major product by naming the position where bromine is installed
solution_steps.why: The rule is: the major product of EAS is the one formed through the lowest-energy transition state, which corresponds to attack at the site that is both electronically activated and sterically accessible. Combining the electronic analysis (para to the alkyl group is activated) with the steric analysis (para position is unhindered), the bromine atom is installed para to the alkyl group and meta to the carbonyl group. This trisubstituted benzene ring is the predominant product.

vocab.trisubstituted benzene: a benzene ring bearing three substituents (here: the original alkyl group, the original carbonyl group, and the newly added bromine atom)

question_text_raw:
18.72Whenortho-bromonitrobenzene is treated with NaOH at ele-
vated temperature, only one product is formed.
(a) Draw the product.
(b) Identify the intermediate involved in the mechanism.
(c) Would the reaction occur if the starting compound were
meta-bromonitrobenzene?
(d) Would the reaction occur if the starting compound were
para-bromonitrobenzene?
solution_text_raw:
18.72.
(a)The reagent (hydroxide) is a strong nucleophile, and the three criteria for SNAr are met (there is a nitro group and a
leaving group that areorthoto each other), so we expect an SNAr mechanism.In an SNAr reaction, the nucleophile
(hydroxide) replaces the leaving group (bromide) and maintains the same location:
Under these basic conditions, the product (ortho-nitrophenol) is deprotonated upon its formation (because the resulting
resonance stabilized phenoxide anion is more stable than hydroxide).In order to return the proton and regenerate
ortho-nitrophenol, we must introduce a proton source into the reaction flask after the reaction is complete (aqueous
acidic workup).The problem statement did not mention an acidic workup, so the product is the following salt (the
conjugate base ofortho-nitrophenol):
(b)In an SNAr mechanism, the nucleophile (hydroxide) attacks the ring at the position bearing the leaving group,
giving the following resonance-stabilized intermediate (called a Meisenheimer complex):
(c)No reaction is expected to take place.In order for an SNAr reaction to occur, the leaving group must beorthoor
parato the nitro group.If the leaving group ismetato the nitro group, then the nitro group cannot function as a
reservoir for electron density to stabilize the intermediate Meisenheimer complex via resonance delocalization..The
Meisenheimer complex is therefore too high in energy to form at an appreciable rate.
(d)Yes, this reaction is expected to proceed.This example meets all three criteria for an SNAr mechanism.The
aromatic starting material exhibits a nitro group and a leaving group (bromide) that areparato each other.

question_analysis.skill_tested: Predict whether SNAr substitution will occur by checking the positional relationship between an electron-withdrawing group and a leaving group on an aromatic ring
question_analysis.disguise: The problem looks like a simple substitution question, but it actually tests whether the student knows that SNAr requires stabilization of a charged intermediate through specific ring positions, not just the presence of any activating group
question_analysis.recognition_cue: Aromatic ring bearing both a halide leaving group (Br) and a strong electron-withdrawing group (NO2) treated with a nucleophile (NaOH) under forcing conditions — this pattern signals SNAr, not SN1/SN2 or elimination

prior_knowledge_needed: SNAr (nucleophilic aromatic substitution) requires: an electron-withdrawing group on the ring, a leaving group on the ring, and a nucleophile
The Meisenheimer complex is the negatively charged, cyclohexadienyl anion intermediate formed when the nucleophile attacks the aromatic ring carbon bearing the leaving group
Resonance delocalization of the negative charge in the Meisenheimer complex into the electron-withdrawing group only occurs when that group is ortho or para to the site of nucleophilic attack
Phenols (ArOH) are more acidic than water, so under basic conditions the phenol product is immediately deprotonated to give a phenoxide salt
Acidic workup (adding aqueous acid after reaction) is needed to protonate the phenoxide and isolate the free phenol

solution_steps.do_this: Recognize the reaction mechanism class by cataloging the three structural requirements present in the starting material
solution_steps.why: The rule is: SNAr proceeds only when all three conditions coexist — a nucleophile, a halide or other leaving group directly attached to the aromatic ring, and an electron-withdrawing group capable of delocalizing the negative charge that builds up in the intermediate. Seeing NaOH (nucleophile), Br (leaving group on ring), and NO2 (strong withdrawing group) together triggers the SNAr framework rather than any other substitution pathway

vocab.SNAr: nucleophilic aromatic substitution — a reaction where a nucleophile displaces a leaving group directly attached to an aromatic ring by first adding to the ring to form a charged intermediate, then expelling the leaving group
vocab.electron-withdrawing group: an atom or cluster of atoms attached to the ring that pulls electron density away from the ring, stabilizing a negative charge placed on the ring

solution_steps.do_this: Draw and evaluate the Meisenheimer complex to determine whether the negative charge can be delocalized onto the nitro group, using the ortho/para positional rule
solution_steps.why: The rule is: negative charge in the Meisenheimer complex can only reach an attached substituent through resonance when that substituent sits ortho or para to the carbon being attacked. When the NO2 group is ortho or para to the Br, resonance structures place the negative charge directly on a nitrogen-oxygen bond of the nitro group, dramatically lowering the energy of the intermediate and allowing the reaction to proceed at a reasonable rate. When the NO2 group is meta, no resonance structure places the charge on the nitro group, so the intermediate is destabilized and the reaction does not occur at an appreciable rate

vocab.Meisenheimer complex: the anionic, non-aromatic cyclohexadienyl intermediate formed during SNAr when the nucleophile bonds to the ring carbon, temporarily disrupting aromaticity and carrying a full negative charge
vocab.resonance delocalization: the spreading of a charge or electron pair across multiple atoms through alternating single and double bonds, lowering the overall energy of the species

solution_steps.do_this: Apply the ortho/para rule to each positional isomer (ortho, meta, para) and predict reactivity for each case
solution_steps.why: The rule is: only ortho and para arrangements between the leaving group and the electron-withdrawing group satisfy the resonance-stabilization requirement for the Meisenheimer complex. Therefore, ortho-bromonitrobenzene reacts (NO2 is ortho to Br — condition met), meta-bromonitrobenzene does not react (NO2 is meta to Br — charge cannot delocalize onto NO2), and para-bromonitrobenzene reacts (NO2 is para to Br — condition met)

vocab.positional isomers: compounds that share the same molecular formula and substituents but differ in where those substituents are placed relative to each other on the ring (ortho = adjacent positions, meta = one position apart, para = directly across the ring)

solution_steps.do_this: Identify the actual isolated product by accounting for the basic reaction conditions after substitution occurs
solution_steps.why: The rule is: any acid stronger than the solvent will be deprotonated by a base present in solution. The phenol product (ArOH) formed after the Br leaves has a pKa around 7–10, making it a stronger acid than water, so the excess hydroxide immediately removes the OH proton. Without an acidic workup step, the isolated product is the phenoxide salt (ArO⁻ Na⁺), not the free phenol. This distinction matters for correctly drawing the product

vocab.phenoxide: the conjugate base of a phenol, in which the oxygen attached to the aromatic ring carries a negative charge; it is stabilized by resonance with the ring
vocab.acidic workup: addition of dilute aqueous acid to the reaction mixture at the end of a reaction to protonate any anionic species and recover neutral products

question_text_raw:
18.73•Which of the following is the least reactive toward Friedel–
Crafts conditions?
CH3NO2
(a)(b)
ClOCH3
(c)(d)
ACS- Style Problems (Multiple Choice)879
solution_text_raw:
18.73.Thecorrectansweris(b).Friedel-Crafts
reactions cannot be performed with aromatic rings that
are strongly or moderately deactivated.Options (a) and
(d) are both activated aromatic rings, and option (c) has a
halogen, which is a weak deactivator.All three of these
compounds will undergo Friedel-Crafts reactions.Only
nitrobenzene is strongly deactivated and therefore, it will
not undergo Friedel-Crafts reactions.

question_analysis.skill_tested: Identify which aromatic compound is too deactivated to undergo Friedel-Crafts alkylation or acylation by ranking substituent effects on ring electron density
question_analysis.disguise: The problem presents four different aromatic compounds with different substituents and asks for the least reactive, which requires comparing activating vs. deactivating substituents rather than predicting a single reaction outcome
question_analysis.recognition_cue: When a multiple-choice question asks which aromatic compound will NOT react under Friedel-Crafts conditions, immediately flag any strongly electron-withdrawing groups (especially nitro groups) as the likely answer, since Friedel-Crafts requires a nucleophilic ring

prior_knowledge_needed: Friedel-Crafts reactions require the aromatic ring to act as a nucleophile (electron donor), so rings that are electron-poor react very slowly or not at all
Substituents on benzene rings are classified as activating (increase ring electron density) or deactivating (decrease ring electron density) based on resonance and inductive effects
The nitro group (-NO2) is a strong deactivator because it pulls electron density away from the ring through both inductive withdrawal and resonance withdrawal, making the ring strongly electron-poor

Halogens (like -Cl) are weak deactivators: they withdraw electrons inductively but donate a lone pair back through resonance, resulting in only mild deactivation that still permits Friedel-Crafts reactions
Alkoxy groups (-OCH3) and alkyl groups (-CH3) are activating substituents that increase ring electron density, making those rings more reactive toward Friedel-Crafts conditions

solution_steps.do_this: Recognize the core requirement: determine which substituent kills the ring's ability to act as an electron donor
solution_steps.why: The rule is: Friedel-Crafts reactions always require the benzene ring to function as a nucleophile (electron-rich attacker); any group that makes the ring strongly electron-poor will shut the reaction down entirely, not just slow it

vocab.nucleophile: an electron-rich species that seeks out and attacks electron-poor centers — in electrophilic aromatic substitution, the pi electrons of the ring play this role
vocab.electrophilic aromatic substitution: a reaction class where an electron-poor species attacks an electron-rich aromatic ring, replacing one hydrogen with the new group

solution_steps.do_this: Classify each substituent in the four answer choices as activating, weakly deactivating, or strongly deactivating
solution_steps.why: The rule is: the electronic effect of a substituent determines whether the ring has enough electron density to attack the electrophile generated under Friedel-Crafts conditions; strong deactivators cross the threshold into 'unreactive' territory, while mild deactivators merely slow the reaction

vocab.activating group: a substituent that donates electron density into the ring, raising the ring's overall electron density and making it more reactive toward electrophiles
vocab.deactivating group: a substituent that withdraws electron density from the ring, lowering the ring's electron density and making it less reactive — or completely unreactive — toward electrophiles

solution_steps.do_this: Identify the nitro group (-NO2) as a strong deactivator and recognize why it uniquely disqualifies that compound
solution_steps.why: The rule is: groups that withdraw electrons through both the sigma framework (inductive withdrawal) and through pi-bond overlap with the ring (resonance withdrawal) at the same time create the maximum possible electron deficiency on the ring; the nitro group does both simultaneously, pushing the ring so far into electron poverty that it cannot attract or react with the Lewis-acid-generated electrophile in Friedel-Crafts conditions

vocab.inductive withdrawal: removal of electron density through the chain of sigma bonds due to an electronegative atom pulling electrons toward itself
vocab.resonance withdrawal: removal of electron density directly from the ring's pi system because the substituent's attached atoms can accept a pair of electrons into their own empty orbital or pi system

solution_steps.do_this: Eliminate the other three options by confirming they retain enough electron density to react
solution_steps.why: The rule is: a substituent that either adds electron density (activating) or only mildly removes it (weak deactivator like a halogen) still leaves the ring reactive enough for Friedel-Crafts; the methyl group and methoxy group are activating, and the chlorine substituent is only weakly deactivating via net inductive withdrawal — none of these drop the ring below the reactivity threshold

vocab.halogen as weak deactivator: halogens pull electrons inductively (making the ring slightly poorer) but simultaneously donate a lone pair back into the ring through resonance, partially compensating — the net effect is mild deactivation, not enough to prevent Friedel-Crafts

solution_steps.do_this: Select nitrobenzene (the compound with -NO2 attached to the ring) as the least reactive answer
solution_steps.why: The rule is: among all common substituents, only strongly electron-withdrawing groups like nitro, carbonyl-containing groups, and cyano groups deactivate the ring to the point where Friedel-Crafts conditions fail; nitrobenzene is the only option carrying a strongly deactivating group, so it is the compound that will not undergo Friedel-Crafts reactions

vocab.strongly deactivated ring: an aromatic ring whose electron density has been reduced so severely by attached electron-withdrawing groups that it can no longer serve as a nucleophile in electrophilic substitution reactions

former is moderately deactivated, while the latter is

question_analysis.skill_tested: Predict the major bromination site on a molecule containing two different aromatic rings with opposing electronic effects
question_analysis.disguise: The molecule looks symmetric at first glance because it has two benzene rings, but the two rings have fundamentally different substituents that make them electronically distinct, requiring the student to analyze each ring separately before choosing a site
question_analysis.recognition_cue: Two benzene rings connected by a bridging unit where one ring bears a carbonyl-type group and the other bears an oxygen atom — this pattern signals that one ring is deactivated and the other is activated, so bromination will be selective

prior_knowledge_needed: Electron-donating groups (EDGs) — substituents that push electron density into a ring — activate the ring toward electrophilic aromatic substitution (EAS) and direct new groups to the ortho and para positions
Electron-withdrawing groups (EWGs) — substituents that pull electron density away from a ring — deactivate the ring toward EAS and direct new groups to the meta position
An oxygen atom attached directly to a benzene ring acts as an EDG via lone-pair donation, making that ring activated
A carbonyl group (C=O) attached directly to a benzene ring acts as an EWG via resonance withdrawal, making that ring deactivated
When two reactive sites compete, the electrophile attacks the more electron-rich (activated) ring preferentially
Steric effects favor the para position over the ortho positions when the ortho sites are hindered by a bulky or nearby substituent

solution_steps.do_this: Identify the electronic character of each benzene ring separately by examining what group is directly attached to it
solution_steps.why: The rule is: the reactivity of a benzene ring in EAS (electrophilic aromatic substitution — attack of a positive electrophile on the pi-electron cloud of the ring) is controlled entirely by what is attached to it. One ring has a carbonyl-containing group attached — this group withdraws electron density by resonance, deactivating that ring. The other ring has an oxygen atom attached — oxygen donates a lone pair into the ring by resonance, activating it. These two rings are not equivalent.

vocab.electrophilic aromatic substitution (EAS: a reaction class in which an electron-hungry reagent (here, Br+) replaces a hydrogen on a benzene ring by attacking the ring's pi electrons
vocab.deactivated ring: a benzene ring made less reactive toward EAS because an attached group pulls electron density away from the ring
vocab.activated ring: a benzene ring made more reactive toward EAS because an attached group pushes extra electron density into the ring

solution_steps.do_this: Decide which ring will be brominated by comparing the reactivities of the two rings
solution_steps.why: The rule is: in a molecule with two aromatic rings of different reactivity, the electrophile will react at the more electron-rich ring because there is greater pi-electron density available to stabilize the intermediate arenium ion (the positively charged, non-aromatic intermediate formed when the electrophile bonds to the ring). The oxygen-bearing ring is activated, so it reacts far faster than the carbonyl-bearing ring. Bromination therefore occurs on the oxygen-bearing ring, not the carbonyl-bearing ring.

vocab.arenium ion: the positively charged, temporarily non-aromatic intermediate (also called a sigma complex or Wheland intermediate) formed when an electrophile attacks one carbon of a benzene ring and bonds to it

solution_steps.do_this: Determine the regiochemistry (which specific position on the activated ring receives the bromine) using directing effects and steric reasoning
solution_steps.why: The rule is: an oxygen atom attached to a benzene ring is an ortho/para director — it stabilizes positive charge at the ortho (adjacent) and para (directly opposite) positions of the ring through lone-pair resonance, so the electrophile is guided to those positions. Among ortho and para positions, the para position is typically favored when the ortho positions are sterically hindered (crowded by nearby atoms), because a bulky electrophile like Br experiences steric strain approaching the ortho sites. Therefore the major product has bromine at the para position of the oxygen-bearing ring.

vocab.regiochemistry: which specific position on a molecule is attacked or modified in a reaction
vocab.ortho/para director: an attached group that channels an incoming electrophile to the positions one step away from it (ortho) or directly across the ring (para) by stabilizing positive charge at those sites
vocab.steric hindrance: physical crowding by nearby atoms that slows or blocks a reaction at a particular site

solution_steps.do_this: Match the predicted product — bromine at the para position of the activated, oxygen-bearing ring — to the correct answer choice among the options given
solution_steps.why: The rule is: the correct answer must simultaneously satisfy three criteria: (1) bromination occurs on the more reactive ring, (2) the substitution position is consistent with the ring's directing group, and (3) the less reactive ring is untouched. Only the answer showing Br para to the oxygen on the oxygen-bearing ring while the carbonyl-bearing ring remains intact satisfies all three criteria. This corresponds to option (c).

vocab.para position: the carbon on the benzene ring located directly opposite (1,4-relationship) to the group already attached to the ring

question_analysis.skill_tested: Predict which multi-step electrophilic aromatic substitution sequence achieves a specific regiochemical (positional) outcome on a benzene ring
question_analysis.disguise: The problem presents four plausible two-step sequences that all install the same two substituents, hiding the fact that the ORDER of steps and the DIRECTING EFFECTS of each group installed first are what determine success or failure
question_analysis.recognition_cue: A benzene ring transformation showing two substituents in a meta relationship — whenever a meta product is required, look for a step that first installs a meta-directing group (electron-withdrawing) before the second substitution

prior_knowledge_needed: Electrophilic aromatic substitution (EAS) — a reaction where an electrophile replaces a hydrogen on a benzene ring
Directing effects — the rule that substituents already on a ring steer incoming electrophiles to ortho/para positions (if electron-donating) or to meta positions (if electron-withdrawing)
Friedel-Crafts alkylation — EAS reaction that attaches an alkyl group using an alkyl halide and a Lewis acid catalyst such as AlCl3
Friedel-Crafts acylation — EAS reaction that attaches an acyl (C=O) group using an acyl chloride and AlCl3, giving a ketone product

Clemmensen reduction — conversion of a ketone attached to a benzene ring into a methylene group (CH2) using zinc amalgam and HCl, effectively converting an acyl group into the corresponding alkyl group without disrupting the ring
Meta directors — electron-withdrawing groups such as carbonyl (C=O) that direct the next incoming electrophile to the meta position (two carbons away)
Ortho/para directors — electron-donating groups such as alkyl groups that direct the next incoming electrophile to positions adjacent or opposite

solution_steps.do_this: Identify the regiochemical relationship between the two substituents in the target molecule
solution_steps.why: The rule is: before choosing any synthetic route, you must know which positions on the ring must be occupied. A meta (1,3) relationship between two substituents cannot be achieved by installing an ortho/para-directing group first, because that group would steer the second substituent away from meta. Recognizing that the target is meta-disubstituted immediately tells you a meta-director must be present on the ring before the second EAS step.

vocab.meta relationship: substituents located 1,3 on a six-membered benzene ring, separated by one carbon between them
vocab.regiochemical outcome: which specific position on the ring is substituted

solution_steps.do_this: Evaluate whether a direct alkylation-then-bromination sequence (like option a) achieves the meta product
solution_steps.why: The rule is: alkyl groups (electron-donating by hyperconjugation and induction) always direct incoming electrophiles to ortho and para positions, never to meta. Therefore installing an ethyl group first and then brominating would place bromine ortho or para to ethyl, producing a para (or ortho) product — not the required meta arrangement. Option (a) fails on regiochemistry despite installing both correct substituents.

vocab.ortho/para director: a substituent that donates electron density into the ring, making the positions directly adjacent (ortho) and directly opposite (para) more reactive toward electrophiles
vocab.hyperconjugation: stabilization of a positive charge or electron deficiency by overlap of adjacent C-H sigma bonds with the pi system

solution_steps.do_this: Recognize that installing an acyl group first creates a meta director, allowing correct placement of bromine
solution_steps.why: The rule is: a carbonyl group (C=O) withdraws electron density from the ring through resonance and induction, making the meta position the least electron-poor and therefore the preferred site for the next electrophile. By performing Friedel-Crafts acylation first, the ring carries a ketone that is a meta director; subsequent bromination then delivers bromine to the meta position — exactly the geometry required by the target.

vocab.Friedel-Crafts acylation: reaction of benzene with an acyl chloride in the presence of AlCl3 to attach a carbonyl-containing group to the ring
vocab.meta director: an electron-withdrawing substituent that depletes ring electron density at ortho and para positions, leaving meta as the preferred site for the next electrophilic attack

solution_steps.do_this: Apply Clemmensen reduction to convert the acyl group into an alkyl group after the correct regiochemistry has been established
solution_steps.why: The rule is: once the meta-directing carbonyl has served its purpose of positioning the second substituent correctly, it can be reduced to a simple methylene (CH2) group using zinc amalgam (Zn(Hg)) and HCl, yielding the desired alkyl chain. This two-step sequence — acylation then reduction — is the standard indirect method for achieving meta-alkylation, circumventing the regiochemical failure of direct alkylation.

vocab.Clemmensen reduction: a reaction using zinc amalgam and concentrated HCl to convert a ketone (C=O) directly bonded to an aromatic ring into the corresponding alkyl group (CH2), without affecting the ring itself
vocab.indirect alkylation: installing an acyl group and then reducing it to an alkyl group in order to achieve regiochemical control that direct alkylation cannot provide

solution_steps.do_this: Confirm that option (d) — acylation first, then bromination to install Br at meta, then Clemmensen reduction to convert ketone to ethyl — matches the target, and select it
solution_steps.why: The rule is: a valid synthetic route must satisfy both functional-group identity and regiochemical requirements. Option (d) uses Friedel-Crafts acylation to place an acyl group on the ring, exploits the meta-directing power of that carbonyl to place bromine at the correct meta position, and finally uses Clemmensen reduction to deliver the alkyl group — producing a 1,3 (meta) arrangement of ethyl and bromo substituents matching the target exactly.

vocab.Lewis acid catalyst: a substance such as AlCl3 or AlBr3 that accepts an electron pair, used in EAS to generate a highly reactive electrophile from an otherwise unreactive reagent

question_analysis.skill_tested: Classify a hydroxyl substituent as electron-donating or electron-withdrawing and predict the regiochemical outcome (ortho/para vs. meta) it produces in electrophilic aromatic substitution
question_analysis.disguise: The question presents four plausible-sounding combinations of donor/withdrawor and directing category, making it easy to confuse the OH group with carbonyl-containing groups that are withdrawing and meta-directing
question_analysis.recognition_cue: Seeing –OH (or –OR, –NH2) attached directly to a benzene ring is the surface signal that lone-pair resonance donation into the ring is possible, which immediately links to the ortho/para director framework

prior_knowledge_needed: Resonance donation: lone pairs on an atom directly attached to a pi system can be delocalized into that system, increasing electron density at specific positions
Electrophilic aromatic substitution (EAS): a reaction in which an electrophile replaces a hydrogen on an aromatic ring, and the ring's electron density controls where attack occurs
Ortho/para directors increase ring electron density and direct electrophiles to positions 2, 4, and 6 relative to the substituent
Meta directors decrease overall ring electron density (or stabilize positive charge away from ortho/para positions) and direct electrophiles to position 3 relative to the substituent
Distinction between inductive withdrawal (through sigma bonds, electron-pulling) and resonance donation (through pi system, electron-pushing)

solution_steps.do_this: Determine whether the oxygen of the –OH group donates or withdraws electron density from the aromatic ring via resonance
solution_steps.why: The rule is: any atom with a lone pair directly bonded to a pi system can push those electrons into the system through resonance, raising the ring's electron density. Because the oxygen in –OH carries two lone pairs and sits directly on the benzene ring, it donates electron density into the ring by resonance, making the ring more electron-rich overall. The inductive withdrawal by oxygen is weaker and is outweighed by the resonance donation effect.

vocab.resonance donation: the delocalization of a lone pair from a directly attached atom into the aromatic pi system, increasing electron density within the ring
vocab.inductive withdrawal: the electron-pulling effect transmitted through sigma bonds because of an electronegative atom, which slightly reduces electron density but does not override resonance donation for oxygen

solution_steps.do_this: Draw the resonance structures that show where the extra electron density is deposited on the ring
solution_steps.why: The rule is: when a lone pair from the substituent is pushed into the ring, the resulting resonance contributors place negative charge (extra electron density) at the ortho and para positions relative to the substituent. This means those two positions become nucleophilic hot spots that attract an incoming electrophile, defining the –OH group as an ortho/para director.

vocab.ortho position: the two ring carbons immediately adjacent to the carbon bearing the substituent (positions 2 and 6)
vocab.para position: the ring carbon directly across the ring from the substituent (position 4)
vocab.resonance contributor: one of several valid Lewis structures that together describe the true electron distribution in the molecule

solution_steps.do_this: Classify the –OH group as an activating or deactivating group and link that classification to the correct answer choice
solution_steps.why: The rule is: substituents that increase ring electron density relative to unsubstituted benzene are activating groups, and activating groups are always ortho/para directors (they stabilize the positive-charge intermediate at those positions). Since –OH donates electrons into the ring, it is an activating group and an ortho/para director. Scanning the answer choices, only option (b) pairs electron-donating character with ortho/para direction, making it the correct selection. Options (a), (c), and (d) are incorrect because they either assign the wrong electronic character or the wrong directing outcome.

vocab.activating group: a substituent that donates electron density to the aromatic ring, lowering the activation energy for EAS and directing electrophiles to the ortho and para positions
vocab.deactivating group: a substituent that withdraws electron density from the aromatic ring, raising the activation energy for EAS; most deactivating groups are meta directors

question_analysis.skill_tested: Identify when SNAr conditions are met and draw the substitution product replacing the leaving group with the incoming nucleophile
question_analysis.disguise: The problem presents four multiple-choice structures including intermediates from the reaction mechanism alongside the actual product, testing whether the student can distinguish the final product from sigma-complex intermediates
question_analysis.recognition_cue: A benzene ring bearing both a strong leaving group (chlorine) and a nitro group (a powerful electron-withdrawing group) in a para or ortho relationship, treated with a strong nucleophile like hydroxide — this pattern screams SNAr substitution

prior_knowledge_needed: SNAr (nucleophilic aromatic substitution) requires three criteria: an electron-withdrawing group on the ring, a leaving group on the ring, and the leaving group must be ortho or para to the electron-withdrawing group

The SNAr mechanism proceeds through a Meisenheimer complex (a negatively charged sigma complex, meaning the ring temporarily loses aromaticity and gains a carbanion-like intermediate) before the leaving group departs
Hydroxide (OH-) delivered by NaOH acts as the nucleophile that attacks the ring carbon bearing the leaving group
The final product is fully aromatic again, with OH replacing Cl — the proton on oxygen is removed during workup, and H3O+ simply protonates the alkoxide if needed
Distinguishing a reaction intermediate (Meisenheimer complex) from the final product is critical — the intermediate still has both Cl and OH attached to the same carbon and the ring is not aromatic

solution_steps.do_this: Verify that all three SNAr criteria are satisfied by the starting material
solution_steps.why: The rule is: nucleophilic aromatic substitution only proceeds when the aromatic ring is activated toward nucleophilic attack. This activation requires (1) a leaving group directly attached to the ring, (2) a strong electron-withdrawing group (EWG) also on the ring, and (3) the EWG must be ortho or para to the leaving group so it can stabilize the negative charge that builds up on the ring in the intermediate. If any criterion is missing, SNAr cannot occur. Here, chlorine is the leaving group, the nitro group (an EWG) is para to it, and hydroxide is the nucleophile — all three boxes are checked.

vocab.SNAr: nucleophilic aromatic substitution — a mechanism where a nucleophile attacks an electron-poor aromatic ring and displaces a leaving group
vocab.electron-withdrawing group (EWG): a substituent that pulls electron density away from the ring, making it more susceptible to nucleophilic attack
vocab.para relationship: the two substituents are on opposite ends of the ring, separated by two carbons on each side

solution_steps.do_this: Trace the two-step SNAr mechanism to identify what the intermediate looks like versus the final product
solution_steps.why: The rule is: in SNAr, the nucleophile adds first to form a Meisenheimer complex (the sigma complex intermediate where the ring is no longer aromatic and the attacked carbon temporarily bonds to both the incoming nucleophile and the original leaving group), and only then does the leaving group depart to restore aromaticity. This means the intermediate has both OH and Cl attached to the same sp3 carbon on a non-aromatic ring. The final product, by contrast, is fully aromatic with OH in place of Cl and no Cl remaining.

vocab.Meisenheimer complex: the anionic sigma complex intermediate in SNAr where the ring carbon under attack is temporarily sp3 and bonded to both the nucleophile and the leaving group, with the negative charge delocalized into the EWG
vocab.sp3 carbon: a carbon using four single bonds, tetrahedral geometry, not part of the aromatic pi system

solution_steps.do_this: Eliminate answer choices that correspond to the intermediate rather than the product, and confirm the correct product structure
solution_steps.why: The rule is: the question asks for the major product, not an intermediate. Any answer choice that still shows both Cl and OH on the same carbon of the ring is a Meisenheimer complex — an intermediate that has not yet lost the leaving group — and must be rejected. The correct answer shows a fully aromatic ring with the hydroxyl group (OH) where chlorine used to be, and the nitro group still para to it, with no chlorine remaining. The H3O+ workup step simply ensures any alkoxide (O-) is protonated to give the neutral phenol (an aromatic compound with OH attached directly to the ring).

vocab.phenol: an aromatic compound with a hydroxyl group (OH) attached directly to the benzene ring
vocab.alkoxide: the deprotonated, negatively charged form of an alcohol or phenol (O- instead of OH)

question_analysis.skill_tested: Identify which proposed carbocation intermediate is impossible in a Friedel-Crafts acylation mechanism by applying the rule that a carbon bearing the acyl group cannot simultaneously carry a positive charge
question_analysis.disguise: The question presents four drawn intermediates and asks which one is NOT possible, requiring the student to trace the mechanistic sequence and spot a structural contradiction rather than simply name the reaction
question_analysis.recognition_cue: An acid chloride plus AlCl3 reacting with an aromatic ring signals Friedel-Crafts acylation; when answer choices show sigma-complex (arenium ion) intermediates, check whether the positive charge is ever placed on the carbon that is already bonded to the carbonyl group — that carbon cannot bear a second positive charge

prior_knowledge_needed: Friedel-Crafts acylation mechanism: Lewis acid activates acid chloride to form an acylium ion, the aromatic ring attacks it to give a sigma complex (arenium ion), then a proton is lost to restore aromaticity
Acylium ion structure: a carbocation stabilized by resonance with the adjacent oxygen, written as R-C≡O+

Sigma complex (arenium ion): the non-aromatic, positively charged cyclohexadienyl cation formed when an electrophile bonds to one carbon of the benzene ring, placing the positive charge at three specific ring positions via resonance

Carbonyl carbon rule: a carbon that is already double-bonded to oxygen in the acyl group bears partial positive character but cannot simultaneously be the sp3 carbon bearing a full positive charge in the arenium ion — these are two different atoms

Electrophilic aromatic substitution (EAS) regiochemistry with a methoxy substituent: the -OMe group donates electrons into the ring by resonance, directing the incoming electrophile to the ortho and para positions

solution_steps.do_this: Reconstruct the correct mechanistic sequence for Friedel-Crafts acylation step by step, identifying what each legitimate intermediate looks like
solution_steps.why: The rule is: every intermediate drawn for a mechanism must be consistent with the electron-pushing sequence. By first establishing what IS correct — acylium ion formation, ring attack at ortho or para, sigma complex with positive charge delocalized only onto carbons not bonded to the electrophile — the student creates a reference to compare against the answer choices

vocab.Friedel-Crafts acylation: an electrophilic aromatic substitution reaction in which an acyl group (R-C=O) is attached to a benzene ring using an acid chloride and a Lewis acid catalyst
vocab.acylium ion: a highly electrophilic carbocation of the form R-C≡O+, formed when AlCl3 pulls the chloride away from an acid chloride
vocab.Lewis acid: a substance that accepts an electron pair; AlCl3 acts as one here by grabbing the chloride from the acid chloride

solution_steps.do_this: Apply the sigma-complex positive-charge rule to determine which ring carbons CAN and CANNOT carry the positive charge in the arenium ion intermediate
solution_steps.why: The rule is: when an electrophile attacks a ring carbon and forms a new sigma bond (single bond) to that carbon, that carbon becomes sp3 and bears no charge; the positive charge is then delocalized only across the remaining three sp2 carbons in the ring that are not directly bonded to the electrophile. Therefore, the carbon where the acyl group attached cannot also show a positive charge in the same intermediate — it is already using all four of its bonds

vocab.sigma complex: the non-aromatic intermediate in EAS where one ring carbon has formed a new bond to the electrophile, disrupting the aromatic pi system and leaving a delocalized positive charge on the ring
vocab.sp3 carbon: a carbon using four single bonds pointing toward the corners of a tetrahedron, with no available p orbital to carry a positive charge by resonance

solution_steps.do_this: Scan each answer choice and flag the one whose positive charge is drawn on the same carbon that is already bonded to the incoming acyl electrophile
solution_steps.why: The rule is: a single carbon cannot simultaneously form a new bond to the electrophile AND carry the delocalized positive charge — those are mutually exclusive outcomes of the resonance structures. Any answer choice that places a positive charge on the ipso carbon (the carbon directly bonded to the acyl group) violates this rule and represents an impossible intermediate

vocab.ipso carbon: the ring carbon that is directly bonded to the incoming electrophile during EAS; it is the sp3 center and is never the site of positive charge in a valid sigma complex

solution_steps.do_this: Confirm the selection by checking whether the methoxy substituent on the ring is consistent with para attack being the productive pathway leading to the observed product
solution_steps.why: The rule is: electron-donating groups such as -OMe (methoxy) activate the ring toward EAS and direct the electrophile to ortho and para positions by stabilizing the sigma complex through resonance when attack occurs at those positions. The observed product has para substitution, so legitimate intermediates should reflect attack at ortho or para; any intermediate inconsistent with this regiochemistry provides a secondary check

vocab.electron-donating group: a substituent that pushes electron density into the aromatic ring, making it more reactive toward electrophiles and steering substitution to ortho and para positions
vocab.regiochemistry: the positional selectivity of a reaction — which specific carbon on the ring gets substituted

18.80The nitroso group is known as a strong deactivator, yet an
ortho/para-director, in electrophilic aromatic substitution reactions. For
example, nitration of nitrosobenzene affords mostly thepara-substi-

tuted product.

Nitrosobenzene

substituent.
(b) Rationalize the directing effects by considering resonance
structures of thepara-substituted sigma complex.

18.80.
(a)The nitroso group is a strong deactivator due to both resonance and induction.Considering induction, both the
nitrogen and oxygen atoms withdraw electrons.More importantly, the pi-bond conjugated with the benzene ring
withdraws electron density via resonance, moving electron density toward the highly electronegative oxygen as shown
below.
(b)Given the lone pair of electrons on the nitrogen atom, the sigma complex may be further stabilized through extra
resonance when the electrophile adds to theorthoorparaposition.Thepara-substituted sigma-complex is shown
below for a nitration reaction, with the participation of the nitrogen lone pair highlighted.
CHAPTER 18775
(c)RingAis substituted with a nitrogen atom bearing a lone pair of electrons, and is therefore capable of donating
electron density to the benzene ring via resonance.Thus, it is an activator and anortho-paradirector.The nitrogen
atom that is a substituent on ringBcontains a positive charge along with abond to oxygen that strongly withdraws
electrons from the benzene ring through both induction and resonance.This nitrogen atom onBis structurally
analogous to a nitro group (positive charge on N) and would be expected to be a strong deactivator andmetadirector.
Since ringAis weakly activated and ringBis strongly deactivated, electrophilic aromatic substitution occurs on ringA
in either theorthoorparaposition. Given that there is significant steric hindrance at theorthoposition because of the
large substituent, it makes sense that the major product occurs viaparasubstitution on ringA.
