question_analysis.skill_tested: Reconcile an apparently contradictory substituent behavior by separately analyzing resonance withdrawal and lone-pair donation in the sigma-complex intermediate
question_analysis.disguise: The problem looks like a simple memorization check, but it demands mechanistic reasoning about why the same nitrogen can simultaneously deactivate the ring overall yet still direct ortho/para through lone-pair donation in the transition state
question_analysis.recognition_cue: Substituent has a π-bond directly conjugated into the ring AND a lone pair on the atom attached to the ring — flag this as a potential deactivating ortho/para director requiring split resonance analysis

prior_knowledge_needed: Electrophilic aromatic substitution (EAS) mechanism: an electrophile attacks the π-system of benzene to form a carbocation intermediate called the sigma complex or arenium ion, then a proton is lost to restore aromaticity
Resonance withdrawal vs. lone-pair donation: atoms bearing π-bonds conjugated into the ring pull electron density away; atoms bearing lone pairs can push electron density into the ring
Inductive effect: electronegative atoms drain electron density through σ-bonds toward themselves, destabilizing cationic intermediates
Sigma-complex stability determines regioselectivity: whichever position of attack gives the most resonance-stabilized intermediate is the preferred position

Ortho/para directing logic: a substituent directs ortho/para when it can donate electron density into the ring at the ortho and para positions of the sigma complex, placing extra resonance structures directly on the carbon bearing the substituent

solution_steps.do_this: Identify both the resonance-withdrawal pathway and the inductive-withdrawal pathway of the nitroso group to explain overall ring deactivation
solution_steps.why: The rule is: any substituent that reduces the electron density of the aromatic ring raises the activation energy for electrophilic attack and therefore deactivates the ring. The nitroso group (–N=O attached directly to the ring) exerts withdrawal in two ways. First, by induction (electron drain through σ-bonds): both nitrogen and oxygen are electronegative and pull σ-electron density away from the ring carbons. Second, and more powerfully, by resonance (electron drain through overlapping π-systems): the N=O π-bond is conjugated with the ring π-system, so resonance structures can be drawn that shift electron density from the ring toward the electronegative oxygen, leaving the ring electron-poor. Because the ring is electron-poor, it is less attractive to an incoming electrophile, making EAS slower — that is, the group is a strong deactivator.

vocab.nitroso group: a functional group consisting of a nitrogen atom double-bonded to oxygen (–N=O) attached directly to the ring carbon
vocab.inductive effect: the withdrawal or donation of electron density through σ-bonds due to electronegativity differences between atoms
vocab.resonance withdrawal: the shift of π-electron density away from the ring and toward a more electronegative atom via overlapping p-orbitals, represented by resonance structures showing a positive charge developing on the ring
vocab.deactivator: a substituent that makes the aromatic ring react more slowly in EAS by reducing ring electron density

solution_steps.do_this: Locate the lone pair on nitrogen and determine whether it can participate in sigma-complex stabilization when attack occurs at the ortho or para position
solution_steps.why: The rule is: a substituent directs to the position whose sigma-complex intermediate gains an additional resonance contributor involving the substituent. Although nitrogen in the nitroso group is involved in a double bond to oxygen (which withdraws electrons), it still retains a lone pair. When the incoming electrophile bonds to the para carbon of nitrosobenzene, the resulting sigma complex (arenium ion) places the positive charge on the ring carbons. Because the para attack puts the positive-charge-bearing carbon directly adjacent to — and in conjugation with — the nitrogen, the nitrogen lone pair can overlap with the empty p-orbital at that carbon, providing one extra resonance structure that spreads the positive charge onto nitrogen. This extra stabilization lowers the energy of the para sigma complex.

vocab.sigma complex: the carbocation intermediate (also called an arenium ion) formed when an electrophile bonds to one carbon of the aromatic ring, temporarily breaking aromaticity and leaving a positive charge delocalized over three ring carbons
vocab.lone pair: a pair of electrons on an atom that is not involved in bonding and is available to donate into an adjacent empty orbital or π-system
vocab.resonance contributor: one of several valid Lewis structures for the same species that differ only in electron placement, collectively describing the true electron distribution

solution_steps.do_this: Compare the sigma complexes from ortho/para attack versus meta attack to confirm the directing preference
solution_steps.why: The rule is: the preferred regiochemistry in EAS is the one that leads to the lower-energy sigma complex. For meta attack on nitrosobenzene, the three resonance contributors of the sigma complex never place the positive charge directly on the carbon bonded to nitrogen; therefore the nitrogen lone pair cannot provide extra stabilization. For ortho and para attack, at least one resonance contributor does place the positive charge on the carbon attached to nitrogen, allowing lone-pair donation into that position. This means the ortho and para sigma complexes are each more stable than the meta sigma complex, so the electrophile preferentially adds ortho or para — the nitroso group is an ortho/para director despite being a net deactivator.

vocab.regioselectivity: the preference for reaction at one specific position on a molecule over other possible positions
vocab.meta director: a substituent whose sigma complexes for ortho and para attack are less stable than for meta attack, steering the electrophile to the meta position
vocab.ortho/para director: a substituent that stabilizes the sigma complexes for ortho and para attack (usually by donating electrons at those positions), directing the electrophile to those sites

solution_steps.do_this: Reconcile the apparent contradiction — strong deactivation combined with ortho/para direction — into a unified mechanistic picture
solution_steps.why: The rule is: deactivation and directing effects are independent properties governed by different aspects of the substituent's electronic influence. Deactivation measures the overall electron density of the ring (nitroso lowers it via resonance and induction, slowing all EAS). Directing effect measures which position gives the most stable sigma complex (lone pair on N selectively stabilizes ortho/para intermediates). A substituent can simultaneously drain electrons from the ring overall — making it globally less reactive — while still selectively channeling the small fraction of reactions that do occur toward ortho and para positions. The nitroso group is the classic example of this combination: powerful π- and σ-withdrawal causes strong deactivation, yet the residual nitrogen lone pair provides just enough extra stabilization at ortho/para sigma complexes to steer selectivity.

vocab.activating vs. deactivating: activating substituents increase overall EAS rate by donating electron density; deactivating substituents decrease it by withdrawing electron density — these properties are about rate, not position preference
vocab.directing effect: the substituent's influence on which ring position the electrophile attacks, determined by which sigma complex is most stable, independent of how fast the overall reaction proceeds

question_analysis.skill_tested: Deduce the structure of an unknown carbonyl compound from spectral data, then predict regioselectivity of electrophilic aromatic substitution on the reduced product
question_analysis.disguise: The problem appears to be just a structure-identification exercise, but the real challenge is chaining spectral interpretation → Clemmensen reduction → EAS directing effects → steric vs. electronic competition to explain which of two monobromo products dominates
question_analysis.recognition_cue: IR near 1680 cm⁻¹ (conjugated carbonyl) + aromatic NMR signals integrating for 5H + upfield signal integrating for 3H + C8H8O formula + Zn(Hg)/HCl reduction followed by Br2/AlBr3 = classic Clemmensen then EAS regioselectivity chain

prior_knowledge_needed: Degrees of unsaturation (index of hydrogen deficiency) calculation from molecular formula
IR carbonyl stretching frequencies: unconjugated ketone ~1715 cm⁻¹ vs. conjugated ketone ~1680 cm⁻¹
¹H NMR chemical shift ranges: aromatic protons 7–8 ppm, methyl protons ~2 ppm
NMR integration ratios translate directly to relative proton counts
Monosubstituted benzene ring accounts for 5 aromatic protons and 4 degrees of unsaturation
Clemmensen reduction (Zn(Hg), HCl, heat) converts a carbonyl group (C=O) into a methylene (CH2) group
Electrophilic aromatic substitution (EAS): alkyl groups are ortho/para directors and activators
Steric hindrance at the ortho position can make para attack the major pathway even when ortho is electronically equally favored

solution_steps.do_this: Calculate degrees of unsaturation from the molecular formula and assign each degree to a structural feature hinted at by the IR and NMR data
solution_steps.why: The rule is: degrees of unsaturation (DoU) = (2C + 2 + N − H − X) / 2, and every double bond, ring, or triple bond must be accounted for before a structure can be proposed. For C8H8O: DoU = (2×8 + 2 − 8) / 2 = 5. One DoU is assigned to the IR absorption near 1680 cm⁻¹, which is characteristic of a conjugated C=O bond (a carbonyl whose π system overlaps with an adjacent π system, lowering the stretching frequency below the ~1715 cm⁻¹ typical of an isolated ketone). The remaining four DoU are explained by the cluster of NMR signals at 7.5–8 ppm, which is the chemical shift range where aromatic protons (protons attached to a benzene ring) appear — a benzene ring itself contributes four degrees of unsaturation (three double bonds + one ring).

vocab.degrees of unsaturation: a count of how many rings and/or π bonds are present in a molecule, calculated from its molecular formula
vocab.conjugated carbonyl: a C=O double bond directly adjacent to another π bond (such as a benzene ring), causing the two systems to interact and lower the IR stretching frequency

solution_steps.do_this: Use the NMR integration values to identify the two fragments that, combined with the carbonyl, exhaust all atoms in the formula
solution_steps.why: The rule is: NMR integration is proportional to the number of protons producing that signal, so the ratio of integrations gives the ratio of proton counts. The aromatic region integration of 5 indicates a monosubstituted benzene ring (a benzene ring bearing exactly one substituent, leaving five ring C–H bonds intact). The single upfield signal integrating for 3 points to a methyl group (–CH3, three equivalent protons). Checking atom count: phenyl (C6H5) + carbonyl (C=O) + methyl (CH3) = C8H8O exactly, confirming the compound is acetophenone (phenyl methyl ketone, a molecule with a benzene ring directly attached to a C=O that also bears a methyl group). The carbonyl is conjugated with the benzene ring, consistent with the 1680 cm⁻¹ IR signal.

vocab.monosubstituted benzene ring: a benzene ring in which exactly one hydrogen has been replaced by another group, leaving five aromatic C–H bonds and producing a characteristic five-proton NMR cluster
vocab.upfield signal: an NMR resonance appearing at lower ppm values, indicating protons in a more electron-rich, shielded environment such as a methyl group

solution_steps.do_this: Apply the Clemmensen reduction logic to determine the structure of compound B
solution_steps.why: The rule is: Clemmensen reduction (zinc amalgam, Zn(Hg), in concentrated hydrochloric acid with heat) converts a ketone or aldehyde C=O group into a C–H2 group without affecting the aromatic ring. Applying this to acetophenone replaces the carbonyl carbon's oxygen with two hydrogens, converting –C(=O)–CH3 into –CH2–CH3. The product is therefore ethylbenzene (a benzene ring with an ethyl group, –CH2CH3, attached), which is compound B.

vocab.Clemmensen reduction: a reaction that uses zinc amalgam and hydrochloric acid under heat to reduce a carbonyl group (C=O) all the way to a methylene (CH2) without touching aromatic rings
vocab.ethylbenzene: a benzene ring bearing an ethyl (–CH2CH3) substituent

solution_steps.do_this: Identify both possible monobromo products of EAS on ethylbenzene by mapping out the ortho and para positions relative to the ethyl group
solution_steps.why: The rule is: in electrophilic aromatic substitution (EAS), the first substituent already on the ring controls where the incoming electrophile attaches — alkyl groups (electron donors by hyperconjugation and induction) activate the ring and direct incoming electrophiles to the ortho positions (carbons directly adjacent to the substituent) and the para position (the carbon directly across the ring). For ethylbenzene, ortho attack gives 1-bromo-2-ethylbenzene and the symmetrically equivalent 1-bromo-6-ethylbenzene (both are the same compound, ortho-bromoethylbenzene), while para attack gives 1-bromo-4-ethylbenzene (para-bromoethylbenzene). These are the two distinct monobromo products.

vocab.electrophilic aromatic substitution (EAS: a reaction in which an electrophile replaces one hydrogen on a benzene ring, with the ring's π electrons attacking the electrophile
vocab.ortho position: a ring carbon immediately adjacent (next-door) to the carbon bearing the substituent
vocab.para position: the ring carbon directly opposite the substituent across the ring

solution_steps.do_this: Predict which monobromo product is the major product by weighing steric hindrance against electronic directing preference
solution_steps.why: The rule is: when ortho and para positions are electronically equivalent (both activated by the same group), steric hindrance (the physical bulk that blocks an incoming reagent from reaching a crowded site) tips the selectivity toward the less hindered position. The ethyl group is a moderate-sized substituent; bromine approaching the ortho carbon must squeeze past the ethyl group, experiencing significant steric repulsion. The para carbon has no such steric obstacle. Therefore, para attack is faster and para-bromoethylbenzene (1-bromo-4-ethylbenzene) is the major product, while ortho-bromoethylbenzene is the minor product.

vocab.steric hindrance: the physical blocking of a reaction site by nearby bulky groups, slowing down reactions at that site
vocab.major product: the compound formed by the faster (lower activation energy) reaction pathway, present in the greater amount after the reaction

question_analysis.skill_tested: Design a multi-step synthesis from benzene by identifying a learnable surrogate substituent, installing it with correct regiochemistry, then transforming it into the target group using reactions from prior chapters
question_analysis.disguise: The problem looks like a straightforward EAS (electrophilic aromatic substitution) synthesis, but the real challenge is recognizing that the target substituents cannot be installed directly and require a detour through a functional-group transformation plus careful ordering of bromination vs. the other substitution step
question_analysis.recognition_cue: Target molecule has Br plus a non-standard substituent attached to the ring via a carbon atom — this carbon-to-ring bond signals the need for a Friedel-Crafts strategy, and the presence of Br forces you to think about ortho/para vs. meta directing before choosing the reaction order

prior_knowledge_needed: EAS directing effects: activating groups (electron donors) direct incoming electrophiles to ortho and para positions; deactivating groups (electron withdrawers, especially carbonyls) direct to meta positions
Friedel-Crafts acylation installs an acyl group (a carbonyl directly attached to the ring) and leaves a ketone handle for further elaboration
Friedel-Crafts alkylation installs a plain alkyl group with no additional functional handle
Grignard reaction: an organomagnesium reagent (R-MgBr) adds to a carbonyl carbon to give an alcohol after aqueous workup
Alcohol dehydration (E1 or acid-catalyzed) converts a secondary or tertiary alcohol into an alkene
Halogenation of benzene (Br2/FeBr3) is an EAS step; halogens are ortho/para directors but deactivators, so their position relative to a second substituent determines regiochemical outcome

Regiochemistry planning: when two substituents must end up para to each other, install the meta-directing group first so the second group is directed to the correct position, OR install the ortho/para director first if the target relationship matches that

solution_steps.do_this: Identify what kind of carbon-to-ring bond the target substituent requires, and decide which learnable reaction class can form that bond
solution_steps.why: The rule is: any substituent attached to an aromatic ring through a new C–C bond can only be introduced via Friedel-Crafts alkylation or Friedel-Crafts acylation. Alkylation gives a plain alkyl group (no extra functional handle); acylation gives a ketone (a carbonyl group) directly on the ring, which is more useful because it provides a chemical handle for further elaboration. Choosing acylation therefore opens a pathway to convert the ring-carbonyl into the desired final substituent.

vocab.Friedel-Crafts acylation: an EAS reaction in which an acyl chloride (R-C(=O)-Cl) and a Lewis acid catalyst attach an acyl group — a carbon with a double bond to oxygen — directly to the benzene ring, creating an aryl ketone
vocab.functional handle: a reactive group deliberately installed on a molecule so that it can be chemically transformed into a different group in a later step

solution_steps.do_this: Plan the transformation of the ketone (installed by acylation) into the actual target substituent using reactions from earlier chapters
solution_steps.why: The rule is: a ketone can be converted to an alcohol by nucleophilic addition of an organometallic reagent (Grignard reaction), and that alcohol can then be dehydrated to an alkene. Chaining these two steps — Grignard addition then acid-catalyzed dehydration — converts the acylation product into the substituent required by the target molecule without ever needing a direct, unknown ring-functionalization method.

vocab.Grignard reaction: addition of an organomagnesium halide (R-MgBr) to a carbonyl (C=O); the carbon of R attacks the carbonyl carbon, and after aqueous workup the oxygen becomes an -OH group (alcohol)
vocab.dehydration: removal of water from an alcohol (using a strong acid and heat) to form a carbon-carbon double bond (alkene)

solution_steps.do_this: Determine the correct regiochemical relationship between Br and the other substituent, then use directing effects to fix the order in which the two groups are installed on the ring
solution_steps.why: The rule is: the directing group already on the ring controls where the next incoming electrophile attaches. A carbonyl (acyl) group is a meta director (electron-withdrawing deactivator), while Br is an ortho/para director (weak deactivator). If the target has the two substituents in a para relationship, bromination must occur before acylation — installing Br first lets the acyl group enter the meta position relative to the acetyl, which corresponds to the para position relative to Br. Reversing the order would place Br meta to the carbonyl, giving the wrong regiochemistry.

vocab.meta director: a substituent that withdraws electron density from the ortho and para positions of a benzene ring, steering the next electrophile predominantly to the meta position (the carbon two carbons away)
vocab.ortho/para director: a substituent that donates electron density into the ortho and para positions, steering the next electrophile to those positions
vocab.regiochemistry: the positional relationship (ortho = 1,2; meta = 1,3; para = 1,4) between two substituents on a benzene ring

solution_steps.do_this: Assemble the complete synthetic sequence in the correct order and verify each step's regiochemical outcome matches the target
solution_steps.why: The rule is: a synthesis is only valid if every individual step produces the correct constitutional isomer (regiochemistry) and the sequence of protecting, installing, and transforming groups is internally consistent. Walking through each arrow — bromination, then acylation, then Grignard addition, then dehydration — and checking that directing effects at each stage point to the correct ring position confirms the route is feasible and gives the intended product.

vocab.constitutional isomer: a molecule with the same molecular formula as another but with atoms connected in a different order, meaning a 1,3-disubstituted benzene and a 1,4-disubstituted benzene are different compounds even though both have two substituents

(a) What products are expected, assuming that conditions are chosen

(b) Explain why the product mixture is optically inactive.

question_analysis.skill_tested: Predict the stereochemical outcome of a Friedel-Crafts alkylation by identifying that a planar carbocation intermediate destroys the original chirality and produces a racemic mixture
question_analysis.disguise: The problem appears to be a straightforward alkylation question, but the real challenge is connecting the Lewis-acid-generated carbocation intermediate to the loss of stereochemical information and the resulting optical inactivity
question_analysis.recognition_cue: An optically active alkyl halide (one with a chiral center at the carbon bearing the halogen) reacts with benzene under a Lewis acid catalyst — this signals that a carbocation intermediate will form, and you must ask what geometry that carbocation has and what that means for stereochemistry

prior_knowledge_needed: Friedel-Crafts alkylation mechanism: a Lewis acid such as AlCl3 abstracts the halide from an alkyl halide to generate a carbocation, which then acts as the electrophile attacking the aromatic ring
Carbocation geometry: a carbocation (a carbon atom bearing only three bonds and a positive charge) is sp2-hybridized and therefore flat (planar), with an empty p orbital perpendicular to that plane

Stereochemistry at a chiral center: a chiral center (a carbon attached to four different groups) can exist as two non-superimposable mirror images called enantiomers, and a sample containing equal amounts of both enantiomers is called a racemic mixture and shows no net rotation of polarized light

Optical activity: a substance is optically active when it rotates plane-polarized light; a racemic mixture is optically inactive because the rotations from the two enantiomers cancel each other exactly
Monoalkylation conditions: using a limiting amount of the alkyl halide relative to benzene steers the reaction toward placing only one alkyl group on the ring

solution_steps.do_this: Determine what electrophile is actually formed when AlCl3 acts on (R)-2-chlorobutane
solution_steps.why: The rule is: a Lewis acid (an electron-pair acceptor) always pulls the halide away from an alkyl halide, converting the carbon that held the halide into a carbocation. Applying this rule here, AlCl3 removes the chloride from the chiral carbon of (R)-2-chlorobutane, generating a secondary carbocation at that carbon. Because the original molecule was a single enantiomer (R configuration), we must now ask whether the carbocation retains that configurational information.

vocab.Lewis acid: a species that accepts an electron pair; AlCl3 acts this way by grabbing the chloride ion
vocab.carbocation: a carbon atom carrying a positive charge because it has only three bonds instead of four
vocab.enantiomer: one of two non-superimposable mirror-image forms of a chiral molecule

solution_steps.do_this: Assess the geometry of the carbocation and decide whether it retains any chiral character
solution_steps.why: The rule is: a carbocation is always sp2-hybridized, meaning its three bonds lie in one flat plane with an empty p orbital pointing above and below that plane. Once the chiral carbon of (R)-2-chlorobutane loses its chloride and becomes a carbocation, it is no longer attached to four different groups in a tetrahedral arrangement — it is flat. A flat carbon center cannot be chiral, so all stereochemical information from the original (R) starting material is erased at this point.

vocab.sp2 hybridization: a bonding arrangement in which one carbon orbital is left unhybridized, forcing the other three bonds into a flat triangular geometry
vocab.chiral center: a carbon attached to four distinct groups, making it non-superimposable on its mirror image

solution_steps.do_this: Predict which side of the planar carbocation the benzene ring will attack, and identify the resulting products
solution_steps.why: The rule is: an incoming nucleophile (here, the pi electrons of the benzene ring) always approaches a planar electrophile from either face with equal probability when neither face is blocked or favored. Because the carbocation is flat and symmetric with respect to approach from above versus below the plane, attack from one face gives the R product and attack from the other face gives the S product in exactly equal amounts. Under monoalkylation conditions, the two products are (R)-2-phenylbutane and (S)-2-phenylbutane.

vocab.nucleophile: a species that donates electrons to form a new bond; the benzene ring acts as one here
vocab.racemic mixture: a 50:50 blend of two enantiomers that produces zero net rotation of polarized light

solution_steps.do_this: Explain why the product mixture shows no optical activity, linking geometry to the macroscopic observation
solution_steps.why: The rule is: optical activity (net rotation of plane-polarized light) is always canceled when equal quantities of two enantiomers are present, because each enantiomer rotates light by the same magnitude but in opposite directions. Because the planar carbocation is attacked from both faces with equal likelihood, the two enantiomeric products form in a 1:1 ratio. A 1:1 mixture of (R)- and (S)-2-phenylbutane is a racemic mixture, which by definition is optically inactive — the rotations cancel to zero regardless of the optical activity of the starting material.

vocab.optical activity: the ability of a chiral substance to rotate the plane of polarized light by a measurable angle
vocab.plane-polarized light: light whose electric-field oscillations are confined to a single plane, used to detect and measure chirality

question_text_raw:
18.84The H NMR spectrum of phenol exhibits three signals in the
aromatic region of the spectrum. These signals appear at 6.7, 6.8, and
7.2 ppm. Use your understanding of shielding and deshielding effects
(Chapter 15) to determine which signal corresponds with themetapro-
tons. Explain your reasoning.
solution_text_raw:
18.84.
The OH group activates the ring toward electrophilic aromatic substitution because the OH group donates electron
density via resonance.
This effect gives electron density primarily to theorthoandparapositions, as seen in the resonance structures above.
These positions are shielded, and the protons at theorthoandparapositions are expected to produce signals farther
upfield (lower ppm) than protons at themetaposition.Therefore, themetaprotons correspond with the signal at 7.2
ppm.
778CHAPTER 18

question_analysis.skill_tested: Use resonance electron-donation patterns of a substituent to predict the relative NMR chemical shifts of aromatic protons at ortho, meta, and para positions
question_analysis.disguise: The problem looks like a pure NMR puzzle, but it actually requires knowing which ring positions receive electron density from an OH group through resonance before any NMR reasoning can begin
question_analysis.recognition_cue: Three distinct aromatic signals for a monosubstituted benzene ring bearing an oxygen-containing group — this pattern signals that the substituent donates or withdraws electron density unequally around the ring, making position-by-position shielding analysis necessary

prior_knowledge_needed: Resonance structures of phenol showing lone-pair donation from oxygen into the ring, specifically placing negative charge at the ortho and para carbons

The shielding/deshielding principle in proton NMR: greater electron density around a proton increases shielding and moves its signal upfield (toward lower ppm values), while reduced electron density deshields the proton and moves its signal downfield (toward higher ppm values)
Identification of ortho (adjacent to the substituent), meta (one carbon removed from ortho), and para (directly across the ring) positions on a monosubstituted benzene ring
Activating groups donate electron density primarily to ortho and para positions, leaving the meta position comparatively electron-poor

solution_steps.do_this: Draw the resonance contributors of phenol to locate where the oxygen lone pair deposits negative charge on the ring
solution_steps.why: The rule is: lone-pair donors attached to an aromatic ring push electron density through the pi system specifically to the ortho and para carbons. This means those two sets of positions gain electron density while the meta carbons do not receive a direct resonance contribution — the meta positions are simply bypassed by this delocalization pathway

vocab.resonance contributor: one of several valid Lewis structures for the same molecule that together describe how electrons are delocalized across the structure
vocab.lone-pair donor: an atom with non-bonding electrons that can overlap with the adjacent pi system and push electron density into it

solution_steps.do_this: Translate the resonance electron-density map into a shielding map: rank ortho, meta, and para positions from most shielded to least shielded
solution_steps.why: The rule is: electron density shields a nucleus by partially opposing the external magnetic field, requiring a higher applied field to reach resonance, which registers as a lower chemical-shift value (upfield, lower ppm). Because ortho and para carbons carry extra negative charge from the oxygen resonance, their attached protons are more shielded. The meta carbons receive no extra electron density from resonance, so their attached protons are comparatively deshielded and will resonate at higher ppm

vocab.shielding: the degree to which surrounding electrons reduce the effective magnetic field felt by a nucleus, shifting its NMR signal toward lower ppm
vocab.deshielding: reduction of electron density around a nucleus, causing it to feel a stronger effective field and resonate at higher ppm
vocab.upfield: the lower-ppm region of an NMR spectrum, associated with greater shielding

solution_steps.do_this: Match the shielding rank to the three observed chemical shifts — 6.7, 6.8, and 7.2 ppm — and assign the meta protons to the highest-ppm signal
solution_steps.why: The rule is: the least-shielded protons in a set appear at the highest chemical-shift value. Since meta protons are the least electron-rich positions on the phenol ring, they correspond to the most downfield signal. Among the three values given, 7.2 ppm is the highest, so the meta protons produce the signal at 7.2 ppm; the signals at 6.7 and 6.8 ppm belong to the more shielded ortho and para protons

vocab.chemical shift (ppm): the position of an NMR signal on a scale referenced to a standard, reflecting how shielded or deshielded the proton is
vocab.downfield: the higher-ppm region of an NMR spectrum, associated with greater deshielding

question_analysis.skill_tested: Predict the structure of a polynitrated aromatic compound by combining directing-group logic with NMR signal counting
question_analysis.disguise: The problem hides the structure behind NMR data — the student must work backward from signal count and integration to determine how many nitro groups were added and where they landed
question_analysis.recognition_cue: A methylbenzene treated with excess nitrating mixture at elevated temperature giving exactly two 1H NMR signals — one upfield integration-3 signal and one downfield integration-2 signal — signals that the ring is nearly fully substituted with only two aromatic H atoms remaining

prior_knowledge_needed: Electrophilic aromatic substitution (EAS): the mechanism by which an electrophile replaces a ring hydrogen on a benzene ring
Nitration: EAS using a nitronium ion (NO2+) generated from concentrated sulfuric acid and nitric acid to install a nitro group (-NO2) on the ring
Ortho-para directors: substituents that steer incoming electrophiles to the positions directly adjacent to (ortho) or directly across from (para) themselves on the ring
Ring deactivation: each electron-withdrawing nitro group makes the ring progressively less reactive, requiring higher temperature for each successive nitration
1H NMR integration: the relative area under each signal is proportional to the number of equivalent hydrogen atoms producing that signal
Chemical shift regions: aromatic protons appear downfield (~6.5–8.5 ppm) while alkyl protons appear upfield (~0.5–4.5 ppm)

solution_steps.do_this: Decode the two NMR signals to count the total number of hydrogen atoms and identify their environments
solution_steps.why: The rule is: every chemically distinct set of hydrogen atoms produces exactly one NMR signal, and integration reveals how many equivalent H atoms are in that set. Consequence: an upfield signal with integration 3 must be the methyl group (-CH3) attached to the ring, and a downfield signal with integration 2 must be the only two aromatic H atoms still on the ring.

vocab.chemical shift: the position of an NMR signal on the frequency axis, reflecting how much electron density shields the hydrogen nucleus — low shielding gives a downfield (high ppm) signal
vocab.integration: the measured area under an NMR peak, which is directly proportional to the count of hydrogen atoms responsible for that peak

solution_steps.do_this: Determine how many substituents were added to the ring by calculating how many ring positions are now occupied
solution_steps.why: The rule is: a monosubstituted benzene ring starts with five aromatic H atoms. Consequence: if only two aromatic H atoms remain after the reaction, exactly three of those five positions must have received a new substituent — in this case, three nitro groups were installed.

vocab.monosubstituted benzene: a benzene ring that already carries one substituent (here, the methyl group), leaving five ring positions bearing hydrogen atoms before any further reaction

solution_steps.do_this: Apply the directing effect of the methyl group to assign the positions of all three nitro groups
solution_steps.why: The rule is: an alkyl group such as methyl is an ortho-para director — it donates electron density into the ring through hyperconjugation and induction, stabilizing the arenium ion (the positively charged ring intermediate in EAS) most when the electrophile attacks ortho or para to it. Consequence: successive nitrations fill the two ortho positions (C-2 and C-6) and the one para position (C-4), placing all three nitro groups symmetrically around the ring and leaving the two remaining H atoms at C-3 and C-5, which are equivalent by the mirror symmetry of the molecule — consistent with a single downfield signal.

vocab.ortho-para director: a substituent that activates the ring positions immediately adjacent to it (ortho, numbered ±1) and directly opposite it (para, numbered +3) toward electrophilic attack
vocab.arenium ion: the positively charged, non-aromatic intermediate formed when an electrophile bonds to one carbon of the benzene ring, also called a sigma complex or Wheland intermediate

solution_steps.do_this: Explain why high temperature is required and confirm the final structure as 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene
solution_steps.why: The rule is: each electron-withdrawing group installed on the ring removes electron density, making each subsequent EAS step progressively slower and requiring more activation energy. Consequence: the first nitration occurs readily, but the second and especially the third require elevated temperature to overcome the strong deactivation imposed by the already-installed nitro groups. The product — methyl at C-1, nitro groups at C-2, C-4, and C-6 — is named 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene (TNT) by IUPAC conventions, and its two remaining aromatic protons at C-3 and C-5 are equivalent, matching the single downfield NMR signal.

vocab.ring deactivation: the reduction in electron density of a benzene ring caused by electron-withdrawing substituents, which raises the energy of the arenium ion intermediate and slows EAS
vocab.2,4,6-trinitrotoluene (TNT): the IUPAC-named product 1-methyl-2,4,6-trinitrobenzene, a symmetric trinitrated methylbenzene in which the methyl group sits at C-1 and nitro groups occupy C-2, C-4, and C-6

question_analysis.skill_tested: Predict the major nitration product of an unfamiliar aromatic substituent by classifying it as an electron-donor or electron-withdrawer using resonance analysis
question_analysis.disguise: The substituents (a biphenyl-type phenyl group and a carbonyl-bearing group) were never explicitly categorized in the chapter, forcing the student to derive directing effects from first principles rather than recall a memorized list
question_analysis.recognition_cue: An aromatic ring carrying a substituent you have not seen before plus HNO3/H2SO4 — this pattern demands you pause, draw resonance structures of the substituent attached to the ring, and ask: does the positive charge in the sigma complex get stabilized (ortho/para director) or destabilized (meta director)?

prior_knowledge_needed: Electrophilic aromatic substitution (EAS): the mechanism by which an electrophile, here NO2+, replaces a hydrogen on a benzene ring via a positively charged intermediate called a sigma complex or arenium ion
The sigma complex (arenium ion): the high-energy, positively charged intermediate formed when the electrophile bonds to one carbon of the ring, temporarily breaking aromaticity
Resonance stabilization of the sigma complex: the more resonance structures that spread out the positive charge, the lower the energy of the intermediate and the faster that position reacts

Electron-donating groups (EDGs): substituents that push electron density into the ring through resonance or induction, stabilizing the sigma complex at ortho and para positions, making them ortho/para directors

Electron-withdrawing groups (EWGs): substituents that pull electron density away from the ring through resonance or induction, destabilizing the sigma complex at ortho and para positions but less so at meta, making them meta directors
Steric hindrance: bulky groups physically block an approaching electrophile, reducing reaction at the adjacent (ortho) position even if electronics favor it
Resonance structures of conjugated systems: the ability to draw curved-arrow resonance forms to trace where electron density flows between a substituent and the attached ring

solution_steps.do_this: Identify what kind of substituent is attached to the ring in each compound by examining whether it can donate or withdraw electron density through resonance
solution_steps.why: The rule is: the directing effect of any substituent is determined entirely by how it interacts with the positive charge in the sigma complex intermediate. Before predicting the product, you must classify the substituent — donor or withdrawer — using resonance structures, even for groups not in a memorized table

vocab.substituent: any atom or group of atoms attached to the main benzene ring in place of a hydrogen
vocab.resonance: the delocalization of electrons across multiple atoms, represented by drawing multiple valid Lewis structures that differ only in electron placement

solution_steps.do_this: For part (a), draw resonance structures showing what happens when the positive charge of the sigma complex lands at the ortho or para position relative to the phenyl-type substituent, and ask whether the substituent's pi electrons can overlap with and stabilize that charge
solution_steps.why: The rule is: any substituent bearing a lone pair or a pi system directly attached to the ring can delocalize the positive charge of the sigma complex over additional atoms, lowering the energy of ortho and para attack. A second aromatic ring attached directly to the first shares its pi electrons by conjugation, spreading the positive charge across both rings at ortho/para positions — this is strong stabilization, making the group an ortho/para director. Then apply steric reasoning: if the ortho position is crowded, the para product dominates

vocab.conjugation: the overlap of adjacent p orbitals across alternating single and double bonds, allowing electrons to delocalize over the entire connected pi system
vocab.ortho/para director: a substituent that steers incoming electrophiles predominantly to the positions two carbons away (ortho) or directly across the ring (para) from itself

solution_steps.do_this: For part (b), draw resonance structures of the carbonyl-bearing substituent attached to the ring and trace where positive charge appears on the ring in those resonance forms
solution_steps.why: The rule is: any substituent containing a carbonyl group (C=O) conjugated with the ring can draw electron density away from the ring by placing a partial positive charge directly on the ring carbons in certain resonance structures. When you draw the resonance contributors, you will find positive charge appearing at ortho and para ring carbons in the ground state — meaning those positions are already electron-poor before any electrophile arrives. Electrophiles avoid electron-poor positions, so attack is funneled to the meta position, which carries less positive character in the resonance structures

vocab.carbonyl group: a carbon atom double-bonded to an oxygen atom (C=O), which is strongly electron-withdrawing because oxygen pulls the shared electrons toward itself
vocab.meta director: a substituent that steers incoming electrophiles predominantly to the position three carbons away from itself on the ring, because ortho and para positions are rendered electron-poor by that substituent

solution_steps.do_this: Combine the electronic classification with any steric considerations to select the single major product and draw the nitro group (-NO2) at the predicted position
solution_steps.why: The rule is: the major product in EAS is the one formed through the lowest-energy sigma complex. For an ortho/para director with a bulky group, the para product is strongly preferred because ortho attack is sterically impeded even though both are electronically activated. For a meta director, the meta product is preferred because it is the least electronically destabilized position. Stating a clear regiochemical outcome — the specific ring position bearing the new nitro group — is the required answer

vocab.regiochemistry: the description of which specific position on a molecule undergoes reaction when more than one position is possible
vocab.nitro group: the -NO2 group introduced onto the ring during nitration, derived from the electrophile NO2+ (nitronium ion) generated by mixing HNO3 and H2SO4

question_analysis.skill_tested: Construct a multi-step electrophilic aromatic substitution (EAS) mechanism that accounts for how an activated aromatic compound and a small carbonyl compound combine repeatedly to build a polymer network
question_analysis.disguise: The problem looks like a polymer synthesis question, masking that its core is repeated EAS logic applied in sequence, with an extra acid-catalyzed dehydration step linking each monomer addition
question_analysis.recognition_cue: Phenol (hydroxyl group directly attached to a benzene ring, making it highly electron-rich) reacting with formaldehyde under acidic conditions — the combination of an activated aromatic nucleophile plus a small aldehyde plus acid catalyst is the signature pattern for an acid-catalyzed EAS polymerization mechanism

prior_knowledge_needed: Electrophilic aromatic substitution (EAS) — the general process in which an electron-rich aromatic ring attacks an electrophile, forms a sigma complex intermediate, then loses a proton to regain aromaticity
Phenol activation — the hydroxyl group attached directly to benzene donates electron density into the ring via resonance, making the ortho and para positions especially nucleophilic
Protonation of carbonyl compounds under acid catalysis — adding H+ to the oxygen of an aldehyde or ketone converts it into a better electrophile
Sigma complex (arenium ion) — the non-aromatic, positively charged intermediate formed when the ring carbon bonds to an electrophile during EAS, which then loses a proton to restore aromaticity

Acid-catalyzed dehydration of a benzylic alcohol — a hydroxyl group at a carbon adjacent to the aromatic ring can be protonated and leave as water, generating a resonance-stabilized benzylic carbocation (a positively charged carbon next to the ring, stabilized by overlap with ring pi electrons)
Polymer chain growth logic — the product of one EAS cycle contains a new electrophilic site that can undergo another EAS cycle with a second phenol, allowing the chain to grow indefinitely

solution_steps.do_this: Identify the first electrophile by working out what acid does to formaldehyde
solution_steps.why: The rule is: acid catalysts always increase electrophilicity of carbonyl compounds by protonating the carbonyl oxygen, placing positive charge on carbon. Applying this rule to formaldehyde means H+ converts it into a resonance-stabilized oxocarbenium species — a carbon that carries partial positive charge — making it reactive enough to be attacked by the aromatic ring of phenol.

vocab.carbonyl compound: any molecule containing a carbon double-bonded to oxygen (C=O), such as formaldehyde
vocab.oxocarbenium ion: the cationic species formed when a carbonyl oxygen is protonated, giving a carbon bearing positive charge stabilized by the adjacent oxygen lone pairs

solution_steps.do_this: Trace the first complete EAS cycle: nucleophilic attack by phenol's ring on the activated formaldehyde carbon, then proton loss to restore aromaticity
solution_steps.why: The rule is: in EAS, the pi electrons of a nucleophilic aromatic ring always attack the electrophilic carbon in two distinct stages — first forming a sigma complex that breaks aromaticity, then losing a proton (taken by any available base, here water) to re-establish the aromatic system. Because phenol's ring is activated at ortho and para positions, attack occurs preferentially at one of those sites. The product is a hydroxymethyl-substituted phenol — a phenol molecule that now carries a –CH2OH group attached at the ring.

vocab.sigma complex: the non-aromatic intermediate in EAS where the ring carbon has formed a new bond to the electrophile and the carbon is now sp3-hybridized, carrying a positive charge delocalized around the ring
vocab.hydroxymethyl group: a –CH2OH unit, meaning a carbon bearing two hydrogens and a hydroxyl group, attached to the aromatic ring

solution_steps.do_this: Recognize that the –CH2OH group on the product is itself a masked electrophile, and determine how acid converts it into an active one
solution_steps.why: The rule is: any alcohol adjacent to a carbocation-stabilizing group can be converted to a good electrophile under acidic conditions by protonating its oxygen and expelling water as a leaving group. Here the benzylic position (the carbon of –CH2OH is directly attached to the aromatic ring) means the resulting carbocation is resonance-stabilized by the ring pi system. This benzylic carbocation is now a powerful electrophile ready to react with a second phenol molecule.

vocab.benzylic carbocation: a positively charged carbon directly bonded to an aromatic ring, stabilized because the ring's pi electrons overlap with the empty p orbital on the cationic carbon
vocab.leaving group: an atom or group that departs with the bonding electrons during a substitution or elimination reaction; water is an excellent leaving group once its oxygen is protonated

solution_steps.do_this: Carry out the second EAS cycle using the benzylic carbocation as the electrophile attacking a fresh phenol ring, and identify the structural outcome
solution_steps.why: The rule is: EAS logic repeats identically whenever a new electrophile and an activated aromatic nucleophile encounter each other. The benzylic carbocation attacks the activated ortho or para carbon of a second phenol, forming a new sigma complex, which then loses a proton to restore aromaticity. The product is now a diarylmethane (two phenol rings bridged by a single –CH2– unit), with each ring still bearing an OH group and potentially open positions for further reaction.

vocab.diarylmethane: a structural unit in which two aromatic rings are connected through a single methylene (–CH2–) bridge

solution_steps.do_this: Generalize the repeating cycle to explain how the three-dimensional polymer network of Bakelite forms
solution_steps.why: The rule is: if each monomer unit in a condensation polymerization carries more than one reactive site, the growing chain can branch and cross-link, producing a three-dimensional network rather than a linear chain. Phenol has three available positions (two ortho and one para) and formaldehyde supplies the methylene bridges after each dehydration. Each time a new methylene bridge forms, the process consumes one formaldehyde, links two phenol rings, and releases one water molecule. Repetition of protonation-of-OH, loss-of-water, benzylic-carbocation-EAS across many phenol molecules ultimately creates the highly cross-linked, rigid macromolecular structure that gives Bakelite its durability.

vocab.cross-linking: covalent bonds that connect separate polymer chains or branches to each other, creating a network structure rather than isolated linear strands
vocab.condensation polymerization: polymer formation in which each bond-forming step also expels a small molecule — here water — as a byproduct

question_text_raw:
18.89Propose a plausible mechanism for the following transfor-
mation:
HOHO
H2SO4+
solution_text_raw:
18.89.Atert-butyl group is removed from the ring and replaced with a proton, in what appears to be the reverse of an
electrophilic aromatic substitution reaction.As such, we expect the reaction to proceed via an intermediate sigma
complex. First, the aromatic ring is protonated to give a sigma complex. Water then functions as a base and removes a
780CHAPTER 18
proton in an elimination-type reaction, where the leaving group is an aromatic ring. This step restores aromaticity, and
produces isobutylene as a byproduct:

question_analysis.skill_tested: Propose a stepwise mechanism for the acid-catalyzed removal of a tert-butyl group from an aromatic ring (proto-dealkylation), identifying the sigma complex intermediate and the driving forces at each step.
question_analysis.disguise: The transformation looks like a simple 'dealkylation,' hiding the fact that it is electrophilic aromatic substitution run in reverse — the aromatic ring itself acts as the leaving group in the elimination step.
question_analysis.recognition_cue: A tert-butyl group attached to a benzene ring disappears under acidic (H2SO4) aqueous conditions and is replaced by a hydrogen — the reverse of Friedel-Crafts alkylation pattern.

prior_knowledge_needed: Electrophilic aromatic substitution (EAS) mechanism: protonation of the ring gives a carbocation-like arenium ion called a sigma complex, then a base removes a proton to restore aromaticity.
Microscopic reversibility: any elementary mechanism can run forward or backward under appropriate conditions; the reverse of EAS is called proto-dealkylation.
Carbocation stability: tertiary carbocations (carbon bearing three carbon substituents) are stabilized by hyperconjugation and are far more stable than secondary or primary ones.
Isobutylene (2-methylpropene) formation: loss of a proton from a tert-butyl carbocation gives the stable, volatile alkene that escapes the reaction mixture, providing a thermodynamic driving force.
Role of strong acid (H2SO4): protonates intermediates and activates the ring toward sigma-complex formation.

solution_steps.do_this: Recognize the overall transformation as the microscopic reverse of Friedel-Crafts alkylation and commit to a sigma-complex pathway.
solution_steps.why: The rule is: any elementary mechanism is reversible, so EAS can run in reverse when conditions favor it. Here, a tert-butyl substituent is replaced by H, which is exactly the reverse of the EAS alkylation sequence — so the same arenium-ion intermediate must appear, but the roles of 'incoming electrophile' and 'leaving group' are swapped.

vocab.proto-dealkylation: the acid-catalyzed removal of an alkyl group from an aromatic ring and its replacement by a proton, i.e., EAS run in reverse
vocab.microscopic reversibility: the principle that the forward and reverse of any elementary step share the same transition state and therefore the same mechanistic pathway

solution_steps.do_this: Write the first mechanistic step: a proton from sulfuric acid adds to the ipso carbon (the ring carbon bearing the tert-butyl group), forming a sigma complex.
solution_steps.why: The rule is: in EAS, an electrophile attacks the pi system of the ring to give a sigma complex (arenium ion), an intermediate in which that one carbon is sp3-hybridized and aromaticity is temporarily broken. Running EAS in reverse starts at the same intermediate — so we reach the sigma complex by protonating the ipso carbon with H+. The tert-butyl group and the newly added H are both attached to that same sp3 carbon, and the positive charge is delocalized over the remaining five ring carbons.

vocab.sigma complex (arenium ion): a carbocation intermediate formed during EAS in which one ring carbon is sp3-hybridized, breaking aromaticity, and the positive charge is spread over the remaining ring carbons
vocab.ipso carbon: the ring carbon directly attached to the substituent that is being installed or removed

solution_steps.do_this: Write the second mechanistic step: the C–C bond between the ipso carbon and the tert-butyl group breaks heterolytically, expelling a tertiary carbocation and regenerating the aromatic ring.
solution_steps.why: The rule is: in the sigma complex the sp3 ipso carbon bears two groups that can each depart as cations — either H+ (normal EAS forward direction) or the alkyl group as a carbocation (reverse direction). The tert-butyl group leaves as a stable tertiary carbocation because tertiary carbocations are far lower in energy than secondary or primary ones. Loss of the tert-butyl cation restores full aromaticity, which provides a powerful thermodynamic driving force — the resonance stabilization energy of benzene (~36 kcal/mol) is recovered.

vocab.heterolytic bond cleavage: bond breaking in which both electrons go to one atom, generating a cation and an anion (or a neutral species and a cation)
vocab.tertiary carbocation: a carbocation in which the positively charged carbon is attached to three other carbon groups, making it relatively stable through hyperconjugation

solution_steps.do_this: Write the final step: the tert-butyl carbocation loses a proton (to water or HSO4–) to give isobutylene, and identify this alkene loss as the thermodynamic sink that drives the overall reaction to completion.
solution_steps.why: The rule is: a carbocation adjacent to a carbon bearing hydrogens can be converted to an alkene by proton loss — this is an E1-type elimination. Isobutylene (2-methylpropene) is a volatile gas that escapes the reaction mixture. The rule of Le Chatelier's principle states that removing a product shifts equilibrium toward product formation; the continuous escape of isobutylene makes the overall proto-dealkylation thermodynamically favorable even though EAS itself is normally irreversible in the forward direction.

vocab.isobutylene (2-methylpropene): the volatile alkene CH2=C(CH3)2 formed when the tert-butyl carbocation loses a proton; its escape from solution drives the reaction forward
vocab.Le Chatelier's principle: when a product is continuously removed from an equilibrium mixture, the reaction shifts to produce more of that product

18.90WhenN,N-dimethylaniline is treated with bromine,orthoand
paraproducts are observed. Yet, whenN,N-dimethylaniline is treated
with a mixture of nitric acid and sulfuric acid, only themetaproduct is
observed. Explain these curious results.
NNN
Br

+
Br
N
HNO3
H2SO4

solution_text_raw:
18.90.The amino group inN,N-dimethylaniline is a strong activator, and therefore, anortho-paradirector.For this
reason, bromination occurs at theorthoandparapositions.However, in acidic conditions, the amino group is
protonated to give an ammonium ion:
Unlike the amino group, an ammonium ion is a strong deactivator and ametadirector.Under these conditions,
nitration occurs primarily at themetaposition

question_analysis.skill_tested: Predict how changing the protonation state of a nitrogen-containing substituent shifts its electronic effect from activating/ortho-para-directing to deactivating/meta-directing
question_analysis.disguise: The problem looks like a contradiction — the same molecule giving different positional selectivity under two different conditions — which hides the fact that the reactive species is chemically different in each case
question_analysis.recognition_cue: An amine-substituted benzene reacting under strongly acidic conditions (H2SO4/HNO3 mixture) — any time strong acid is present alongside an amine, suspect protonation of the nitrogen before considering directing effects

prior_knowledge_needed: Electron-donating groups (substituents that push electron density into the ring via lone-pair resonance) direct incoming electrophiles to ortho and para positions
Electron-withdrawing groups (substituents that pull electron density away from the ring) direct incoming electrophiles to the meta position
Amines (–NR2) possess a lone pair on nitrogen that donates into the aromatic ring through resonance, making them strong activators and ortho/para directors
Strong acids such as sulfuric acid can protonate an amine, converting –NR2 into –NHR2+ (an ammonium ion), which carries a full positive charge on nitrogen
A positively charged nitrogen attached to the ring withdraws electron density from the ring inductively and cannot donate lone pairs via resonance, making it a deactivator and meta director

solution_steps.do_this: Identify the electronic character of the dimethylamino group under neutral conditions (bromination reaction)
solution_steps.why: The rule is: any substituent that feeds electron density into the aromatic pi system through resonance (lone-pair donation) stabilizes the carbocation intermediates at the ortho and para positions more than at meta, so electrophilic attack is directed to those two sites. The –N(CH3)2 group has a lone pair on nitrogen that participates in resonance with the ring, satisfying this rule, so bromination lands ortho and para.

vocab.resonance donation: the sharing of a lone pair from an attached atom into the aromatic ring's pi electron system, increasing electron density at alternating (ortho/para) positions

solution_steps.do_this: Determine what happens to the dimethylamino group when the molecule is placed into the strongly acidic nitrating mixture (H2SO4 / HNO3)
solution_steps.why: The rule is: amines (basic nitrogen compounds) are protonated by strong acids before any other reaction can occur. Sulfuric acid is far stronger than the amine's conjugate acid, so the nitrogen picks up a proton and becomes a positively charged ammonium ion (–N+(CH3)2H) before any nitration begins. This means the species actually entering the nitration step is the protonated form, not the neutral amine.

vocab.ammonium ion: a nitrogen atom that has accepted a proton and therefore carries a full +1 formal charge, as in –N+(CH3)2H attached to the ring

solution_steps.do_this: Re-evaluate the directing effect of the substituent now that it is a positively charged ammonium group
solution_steps.why: The rule is: a substituent bearing a full positive charge on the atom directly attached to the ring withdraws electron density from the ring through two mechanisms — induction (the positive charge electrostatically pulls electrons toward it) and the inability to donate any lone pairs (they are now tied up in the N–H bond). Both effects lower electron density most severely at the ortho and para positions, making the meta position comparatively electron-richer and therefore the preferred site for the incoming electrophile (the nitronium ion, NO2+).

vocab.inductive withdrawal: removal of electron density through sigma bonds toward a more electronegative or positively charged atom, felt most strongly at nearby positions
vocab.nitronium ion: the electrophile NO2+ generated when nitric acid is activated by sulfuric acid; it attacks the aromatic ring during nitration

solution_steps.do_this: Reconcile the two outcomes by connecting each condition to the actual reactive species present
solution_steps.why: The rule is: directing effects are determined by the substituent's electronic state at the moment of electrophilic attack, not by the substituent's identity in isolation. Under neutral bromination conditions the substituent is –N(CH3)2 (electron-donating, ortho/para director), giving ortho and para brominated products. Under acidic nitration conditions the substituent is –N+(CH3)2H (electron-withdrawing, meta director), giving the meta nitrated product. The apparent contradiction dissolves once the protonation state is accounted for.

vocab.electrophilic aromatic substitution: a reaction class in which an electron-hungry species (electrophile) replaces a hydrogen on a benzene ring, driven by the ring's pi electron density

question_text_raw:
18.91Aromatic heterocycles are also capable of undergoing electro-
philic aromatic substitution. For example, when furan is treated with
an electrophile, an electrophilic aromatic substitution reaction occurs
in which the electrophile is installed exclusively at the C2 position.
Explain why this reaction occurs at the C2 position, rather than the
C3 position.
O+O
2E
E
3
882CHAPTER 18Aromatic Substitution Reactions
solution_text_raw:
18.91.Attack at the C2 position proceeds via an intermediate with three resonance structures:
In contrast, attack at the C3 position proceeds via an intermediate with only two resonance structures:
The intermediate for C2 attack is lower in energy than the intermediate for C3 attack. The transition state leading to the
intermediate of C2 attack will therefore be lower in energy than the transition state leading to the intermediate of C3
attack. As a result, C2 attack occurs more rapidly.
CHAPTER 18781

question_analysis.skill_tested: Compare the stability of two arenium-ion intermediates by counting and evaluating resonance structures to predict the preferred site of electrophilic attack on furan
question_analysis.disguise: The problem looks like a simple regiochemistry question, but it is actually a resonance-stability comparison problem disguised as a heterocycle substitution question
question_analysis.recognition_cue: Any time a five-membered aromatic heterocycle (a ring containing both carbon atoms and a heteroatom such as oxygen or nitrogen) is attacked by an electrophile, ask: how many resonance structures does the resulting positively charged intermediate have for each possible attack site?

prior_knowledge_needed: Electrophilic aromatic substitution (EAS) mechanism: an electrophile attacks a pi system, forming a carbocation intermediate called an arenium ion or sigma complex, followed by re-aromatization by loss of a proton

Resonance structures: alternative electron-dot representations of the same molecule that spread formal charge over multiple atoms, stabilizing the species — more resonance structures means lower energy and greater stability

Hammond's postulate: the transition state for an exothermic step resembles the reactant, but for an endothermic step it resembles the product — forming the arenium ion is endothermic, so the transition state energy closely tracks the intermediate energy
Furan ring numbering: the oxygen atom is atom 1, and the two adjacent carbons are C2; the next two carbons farther from oxygen are C3

Lone-pair donation: a heteroatom (an atom other than carbon in the ring, here oxygen) can donate electron density into the ring through resonance, and this donation stabilizes adjacent positive charges more effectively than remote ones

solution_steps.do_this: Draw the arenium-ion intermediate (the positively charged, non-aromatic ring formed after the electrophile bonds to the carbon) that results from electrophile attack at C2, then enumerate all valid resonance structures for it
solution_steps.why: The rule is: every resonance structure that spreads a formal positive charge onto an additional atom lowers the overall energy of the intermediate. Attack at C2 places the positive charge initially on C3; from there, charge can delocalize onto C4 AND onto the oxygen atom — giving three resonance contributors in total. Oxygen's lone pair is directly available to stabilize a positive charge on the adjacent C3 atom, which is adjacent to C2 where the electrophile attached.

vocab.arenium ion: the positively charged, temporarily non-aromatic carbocation intermediate formed when an electrophile bonds to one carbon of an aromatic ring during EAS
vocab.resonance contributor: one of several valid electron-dot structures for the same species, differing only in electron placement, not in atom positions

solution_steps.do_this: Draw the arenium-ion intermediate that results from electrophile attack at C3, then enumerate its resonance structures and notice what is missing
solution_steps.why: The rule is: oxygen can only donate its lone pair through resonance to stabilize a positive charge when that charge appears on an atom directly bonded to oxygen. Attack at C3 places the initial positive charge on C2 and C4; charge can delocalize between those two carbons, but oxygen is not directly bonded to C4, so it cannot donate its lone pair to stabilize charge there. Charge on C2 is adjacent to oxygen, but following the pi bonds through the ring from the C3 attack site, the oxygen lone pair cannot be incorporated into a complete resonance structure without breaking the rules of electron pushing. The result is only two resonance contributors — one fewer than the C2-attack pathway.

vocab.lone pair: a pair of electrons on an atom that is not involved in a bond and can potentially be donated into an adjacent empty orbital or positively charged center

solution_steps.do_this: Compare the two sets of resonance structures and assign relative energy levels to the two intermediates
solution_steps.why: The rule is: the more resonance structures a charged intermediate possesses, the more its charge is delocalized (spread out), and the lower its potential energy. The C2-attack intermediate has three resonance contributors including one where the oxygen atom bears the positive charge, fully exploiting oxygen's lone-pair donation. The C3-attack intermediate has only two resonance contributors and never places stabilizing charge on oxygen. Therefore the C2-attack intermediate is lower in energy — it is more stable.

vocab.charge delocalization: the spreading of a formal charge across multiple atoms through resonance, which lowers the energy of the species because the charge is not concentrated at a single point

solution_steps.do_this: Apply Hammond's postulate to connect intermediate stability to reaction rate, then state the regiochemical outcome
solution_steps.why: The rule is: when two parallel reaction pathways each pass through a high-energy (endothermic) intermediate, the pathway with the more stable intermediate also has the lower-energy transition state leading to it. Because the C2-attack intermediate is more stable, its transition state is lower in energy than the transition state for C3 attack. A lower activation energy means a faster rate. Therefore electrophilic substitution on furan occurs exclusively at C2, not C3.

vocab.Hammond's postulate: the principle that for an endothermic elementary step, the transition state closely resembles the high-energy intermediate in structure and energy, so anything that stabilizes the intermediate also stabilizes the preceding transition state
vocab.regiochemistry: the description of which specific site on a molecule undergoes reaction when multiple sites are possible

question_text_raw:
18.92The rate constants for the bromination of several disubstituted
10
stilbenes are given in the table below.Given that the double bond of
stilbene acts as the nucleophile, provide a reasonable explanation for
the trend observed among the rate constants.
YY
Br
Br2
Br
XX
XY3 
k(×10l/mol·min)
OMeOMe220
OMeMe114
OMeCl45
OMeNO25.2
solution_text_raw:
18.92.In all cases, the X substituent is a methoxy group. It is the Y substituent that varies in identity. It is difficult to
imagine that there could be a steric effect here, since the Y substituent is far away from the alkenebond. The trend is
more easily explained when we consider the electronic effect of the Y substituent.Specifically, the methoxy group (a
powerful activator) can donate electron density via resonance into the alkenebond, making it the most nucleophilic
bond among the disubstituted stilbenes. A methyl group is only weakly activating, so its effect should be smaller than
the effect of a methoxy group (giving a smaller rate constant). A chloro group is a weak deactivator, which means that
the net effect of the chloro group is to withdraw electron density from the aromatic ring and the conjugated alkene
bond.As a result, when Y = Cl, the rate constant is smaller than the rate constant when Y = Me.Finally, the nitro
group is powerfully electron-withdrawing, via resonance, and it will significantly reduce the nucleophilicity of the
alkenebond, rendering it the least reactive double bond towards molecular bromine.

question_analysis.skill_tested: Predict how electron-donating or electron-withdrawing substituents on an aromatic ring alter the nucleophilicity of a conjugated alkene, and rank reaction rates accordingly
question_analysis.disguise: The problem presents a rate-constant table for a bromination reaction, making it look like a kinetics problem, but the core task is interpreting electronic effects on pi-bond reactivity
question_analysis.recognition_cue: When a double bond is conjugated with an aromatic ring bearing substituents, and rate data vary with substituent identity, the trigger is: use donor/acceptor character of each substituent to rank pi-bond electron density and therefore nucleophilicity

prior_knowledge_needed: Electrophilic addition to alkenes proceeds when the alkene acts as a nucleophile (electron donor) toward an electrophile such as Br2, so greater electron density in the pi bond means a faster rate
Resonance donation: certain groups (e.g., -OMe) push electron density through pi bonds into conjugated systems, raising electron density at the alkene
Resonance withdrawal: certain groups (e.g., -NO2) pull electron density through pi bonds away from conjugated systems, lowering electron density at the alkene
Conjugation means the aromatic ring pi system and the alkene pi system are electronically connected, so substituent effects on the ring are transmitted to the double bond
Relative activating/deactivating power order: -OMe (strong donor) > -Me (weak donor) > -Cl (weak net withdrawer) > -NO2 (strong withdrawer)

solution_steps.do_this: Identify which variable is actually changing across the four compounds and rule out steric explanations
solution_steps.why: The rule is: when comparing a series of reactions, you must isolate the one structural difference to attribute rate changes correctly. Here the X substituent is identical in every entry (-OMe), so only Y changes. The Y group is located far from the reacting double bond, making a steric (size-based obstruction) explanation implausible; therefore the effect must be electronic

vocab.steric effect: a change in reactivity caused by the physical bulk of a group blocking approach of a reagent, not by its electron character
vocab.electronic effect: a change in reactivity caused by a group either donating or withdrawing electron density through bonds or space

solution_steps.do_this: Establish the mechanistic link between substituent electronics and alkene nucleophilicity in a conjugated system
solution_steps.why: The rule is: in electrophilic addition, the alkene is the nucleophile, so its rate depends on how electron-rich its pi bond is. Because the alkene is conjugated (directly connected through alternating single and double bonds) with the aromatic ring, any group that donates electrons into the ring also raises electron density in the alkene pi bond, making it a better nucleophile toward Br2 and increasing the rate constant

vocab.nucleophilicity: the tendency of a species to donate electrons to an electrophile; higher electron density means higher nucleophilicity
vocab.conjugated system: a series of atoms with overlapping p orbitals that allows electron density to be delocalized across the entire connected pi framework

solution_steps.do_this: Assign each Y substituent its donor/acceptor character and map it onto the observed rate constants
solution_steps.why: The rule is: resonance donors elevate pi-bond electron density while resonance acceptors deplete it, and the magnitude of the effect scales with the strength of the group. -OMe is a powerful resonance donor, so Y = OMe gives maximum alkene electron density and the highest rate (220). -Me is a weak donor by hyperconjugation only, giving a moderately high rate (114). -Cl is a net electron withdrawer despite weak pi donation, reducing alkene electron density and giving a lower rate (45). -NO2 is a powerful resonance withdrawer, strongly depleting alkene electron density and giving the lowest rate (5.2)

vocab.resonance donor: a substituent that pushes a lone pair or pi electrons into an adjacent pi system, increasing electron density there
vocab.resonance withdrawer: a substituent that pulls electron density out of an adjacent pi system toward itself through overlapping p orbitals
vocab.hyperconjugation: stabilization or electron donation arising from overlap of C-H sigma bonds with an adjacent pi or empty p orbital, a weaker effect than direct resonance

solution_steps.do_this: State the unified explanation as a single coherent principle connecting substituent identity to rate trend
solution_steps.why: The rule is: in a conjugated nucleophile-electrophile pairing, reaction rate increases monotonically with the electron density of the nucleophilic site. All four rate constants fall into perfect order once each Y group is ranked by its net electron-donating power transmitted through conjugation to the alkene. No steric or inductive argument is needed; pure resonance-based electronic tuning of alkene nucleophilicity fully accounts for the factor of ~42 difference between the fastest (OMe/OMe) and slowest (OMe/NO2) substrate

vocab.inductive effect: electron density change transmitted through sigma bonds by electronegativity differences, falling off rapidly with distance

18.93Compound1and compound2both contain tritium (T),
3
which is an isotope of hydrogen (tritium=H). Both compounds
are stable upon treatment with aqueous base. However, upon pro-
longed treatment with aqueous acid, compounds1and2both lose
tritium,togive1,3-dimethoxybenzeneand1,3,5-trimethoxyben-
11
zene, respectively:
CHALLENGE PROBLEMS
solution_text_raw:
18.93.
(a)In aqueous acidic conditions, compounds1and2lose tritium via an electrophilic aromatic substitution reaction.
First, the aromatic ring is protonated to give a resonance-stabilized sigma complex, which then undergoes loss of T+
+
(rather than H) to re-establish aromaticity.Notice that the methoxy groups render the sigma complex particularly
stable, because the positive charge is spread over the oxygen atoms as well (via resonance).
(b)Compound2has an extra methoxy group that is absent in compound1.This group further stabilizes the
intermediate sigma complex by introducing one more resonance structure (highlighted below).That is, the positive
charge is further delocalized:
When compound2undergoes an electrophilic aromatic substitution reaction, the intermediate sigma complex exhibits a
positive charge that is spread over three carbon atoms and three oxygen atoms.This intermediate is more stable than
the intermediate formed when compound1undergoes an electrophilic aromatic substitution reaction.A more stable
intermediate is associated with a lower-energy transition state, so it is therefore expected that compound2will lose
tritium at a faster rate than compound1.
