question_analysis.skill_tested: Explain why electron-donating groups on an aromatic ring accelerate electrophilic aromatic substitution by stabilizing the cationic intermediate, and predict why a ring with more such groups reacts faster
question_analysis.disguise: The problem is framed as an isotope-loss (detritiation) experiment under acidic conditions, hiding the fact that it is really an EAS mechanism question where the electrophile is simply a proton (H⁺)
question_analysis.recognition_cue: An aromatic compound bearing multiple methoxy groups loses a hydrogen isotope slowly in acid but not in base — this asymmetry (acid yes, base no) signals that the ring is acting as a nucleophile toward a proton electrophile, i.e., an EAS protonation–deprotonation sequence

prior_knowledge_needed: Electrophilic aromatic substitution (EAS) mechanism: the ring attacks an electrophile to form a carbocation intermediate called a sigma complex (also called an arenium ion or Wheland intermediate), which then loses a proton to restore aromaticity

Methoxy groups (–OCH₃ attached to a benzene ring) are electron-donating by resonance: the lone pairs on oxygen push electron density into the ring, stabilizing any positive charge that develops on the ring

The Hammond postulate: for an endothermic step the transition state resembles the high-energy intermediate, so anything that lowers the intermediate's energy also lowers the activation energy and speeds up the reaction

Isotope effects: tritium (mass-3 hydrogen) and ordinary hydrogen behave chemically the same way; if T⁺ leaves instead of H⁺ in the product-forming step, the net transformation is loss of tritium from the ring
Resonance delocalization of a positive charge: the more atoms over which a positive charge is spread, the more stable the species

solution_steps.do_this: Identify the overall transformation and rule out base-mediated pathways
solution_steps.why: The rule is: bases remove protons, so base-catalyzed loss of a ring-bound hydrogen would require the ring carbon to be unusually acidic — it is not, so base does nothing. Acid, however, supplies electrophilic protons (H⁺) that can attack the π system. Recognizing this tells us the mechanism must be EAS (the ring attacks an electrophile) rather than any kind of aromatic deprotonation.

vocab.electrophile: an electron-hungry species that accepts a pair of electrons from a nucleophile to form a new bond
vocab.pi system: the cloud of electrons above and below the benzene ring plane, formed by overlapping p orbitals, which can donate electrons to an electrophile

solution_steps.do_this: Trace the EAS mechanism step by step and locate where tritium departs
solution_steps.why: The rule is: in EAS, the ring first donates its π electrons to the electrophile (here, H⁺ from aqueous acid), forming a sigma complex — a non-aromatic carbocation intermediate in which one ring carbon is now sp³ (has four single bonds). At this point the ring carbon bearing tritium still holds T. In the second step, any proton (or T⁺) on that sp³ carbon leaves to restore the aromatic sextet. Because there are many ordinary H⁺ ions in solution but T is rare, statistically it is T⁺ that is lost most often when the reaction is driven to completion, resulting in net replacement of T by H and loss of tritium from the molecule.

vocab.sigma complex: the carbocation intermediate in EAS where the electrophile has bonded to one ring carbon, breaking aromaticity and leaving a positive charge delocalized over the remaining ring carbons
vocab.aromaticity: the special extra stability of a cyclic, planar, fully conjugated ring with 4n+2 π electrons (n = integer), which drives the ring to eject a proton and regain this stability

solution_steps.do_this: Assess how each methoxy group stabilizes the sigma complex through resonance
solution_steps.why: The rule is: any substituent that can donate electron density into a carbocation by resonance lowers that carbocation's energy. A methoxy group attached to the ring has lone pairs on oxygen; when positive charge develops on an adjacent ring carbon, oxygen donates a lone pair through the π system, spreading the charge onto oxygen itself. Each additional resonance structure that places the positive charge on a new atom further stabilizes the intermediate. The first compound (two methoxy groups) generates a sigma complex whose positive charge is delocalized over ring carbons and two oxygen atoms. The second compound (three methoxy groups, including one at the position between the other two) generates a sigma complex whose positive charge is delocalized over ring carbons and three oxygen atoms — one more resonance contributor.

vocab.resonance contributor: one of two or more valid Lewis structures for the same species that differ only in electron-pair placement; the real species is a blend of all contributors
vocab.electron-donating by resonance: the ability of a group to push its lone-pair or π electrons into the ring, stabilizing positive charge on the ring

solution_steps.do_this: Apply the Hammond postulate to rank the two compounds' reaction rates
solution_steps.why: The rule is: when the rate-determining step is endothermic (or only weakly exothermic), the transition state looks like the high-energy intermediate, so factors that stabilize the intermediate also lower the activation energy. Forming the sigma complex is the slow, endothermic step in EAS. Because the three-methoxy compound produces a more delocalized (more stable) sigma complex, its transition state is lower in energy, meaning the activation energy is smaller and the reaction is faster. Therefore the three-methoxy compound loses tritium at a greater rate than the two-methoxy compound.

vocab.Hammond postulate: the principle that the transition state of an endothermic step resembles the products (the high-energy intermediate) in structure and energy, linking intermediate stability directly to reaction rate
vocab.activation energy: the energy difference between the starting material and the transition state; the barrier that must be overcome for reaction to occur

question_text_raw:
18.94Consider the following reaction, in which the product results
12
from substitution of fluorine and not from substitution of chlorine.
Cl
NO
Cl2
NaC
NO2C
+C
C
FOMe
OMe
(a) Draw a mechanism for this reaction.
(b) Based on the observed regiochemical outcome, identify the step
of the mechanism that is rate determining. Explain your answer.
solution_text_raw:
18.94.
(a)This is a reaction between a strong nucleophile (the terminal alkynide ion) and a nitro-substituted benzene ring,
from which a halide is expelled. This satisfies the criteria for a nucleophilic aromatic substitution mechanism, as shown
below. The alkynide ion attacks the carbon atom attached to fluorine, producing a resonance-stabilized Meisenheimer
complex, which loses a fluoride leaving group to give the product.
(b)The observed regiochemical outcome (that the fluoride is substituted and not the chloride, even though chloride is a
better leaving group) indicates that the departure of the leaving group does not occur in the rate determining step. The
leaving group leaves in step 2, so step 1 must be rate determining. In other words, the poor ability of fluoride to serve
as a leaving group does not affect the rate of the reaction, since this occurs in the step that is not rate determining.
In the first step, the nucleophile prefers to attack the carbon atom attached to fluorine because of its greater
electrophilicity (due to fluorine’s greater electronegativity) relative to the carbon next to chlorine.
pairing_note: Structural diagram(s) in original not captured by text extraction

question_analysis.skill_tested: Determine the rate-determining step of a nucleophilic aromatic substitution by analyzing which leaving group is displaced despite being the poorer leaving group
question_analysis.disguise: The problem looks like a simple substitution question, but the key insight is that fluorine — normally a terrible leaving group — is the one that leaves, forcing you to reason backwards from regiochemistry to mechanism step order
question_analysis.recognition_cue: An aromatic ring bearing both a fluorine and a chlorine, a strong nucleophile, and a nitro group activator — and the product shows F was replaced rather than Cl — signals that leaving-group identity does NOT control the outcome, pointing to an addition-then-elimination mechanism where the first (addition) step is rate-determining

prior_knowledge_needed: Nucleophilic aromatic substitution (SNAr) proceeds in two stages: nucleophile addition to form a stabilized anionic intermediate, then departure of a leaving group
The Meisenheimer complex is the anionic sigma-complex intermediate formed when a nucleophile bonds to an aromatic carbon, temporarily breaking aromaticity
Electron-withdrawing groups such as nitro stabilize the Meisenheimer complex by delocalizing its negative charge, activating the ring toward SNAr

Fluorine is the worst leaving group among halogens in ordinary SN2 reactions, yet it is the best leaving group in SNAr because its high electronegativity makes the carbon it is attached to more electrophilic
In a two-step mechanism, only the step BEFORE the rate-determining step is irreversible and rate-controlling; events in later steps do not appear in the rate law

solution_steps.do_this: Recognize the reaction type by cataloguing the three diagnostic features present: a strong nucleophile, an electron-withdrawing group on the ring, and a halogen leaving group
solution_steps.why: The rule is: SNAr requires (1) a nucleophile capable of adding to an sp2 carbon, (2) a ring-activating electron-withdrawing group ortho or para to the leaving group to stabilize the incoming negative charge, and (3) a halide that can be expelled. When all three are present on an aryl halide, an addition-elimination pathway is operative rather than a direct one-step displacement.

vocab.SNAr: nucleophilic aromatic substitution — a two-step addition-then-elimination sequence on an aromatic ring, in contrast to single-step SN2 displacement
vocab.electron-withdrawing group: a substituent that pulls electron density away from the ring, stabilizing any build-up of negative charge in an intermediate

solution_steps.do_this: Draw the two-step mechanism: Step 1 — nucleophile attacks the ring carbon bearing fluorine to give a Meisenheimer complex; Step 2 — fluoride departs, restoring aromaticity
solution_steps.why: The rule is: in SNAr, the nucleophile must bond first (addition), generating a non-aromatic, negatively charged sigma-complex; only afterward does the leaving group leave (elimination) to re-establish the aromatic system. The nitro group stabilizes the Meisenheimer complex through resonance delocalization of the negative charge into its own nitrogen-oxygen bonds, making Step 1 energetically accessible.

vocab.Meisenheimer complex: the anionic, non-aromatic intermediate in SNAr formed when the nucleophile has bonded to but the leaving group has not yet left the ring carbon
vocab.sigma-complex: an intermediate where a carbon that was sp2 (part of the aromatic ring) has become sp3 by forming a new bond with the incoming nucleophile

solution_steps.do_this: Identify which carbon is attacked by asking which carbon is more electrophilic — the one bearing fluorine or the one bearing chlorine
solution_steps.why: The rule is: the electrophilicity (electron-poverty) of a carbon is proportional to the electronegativity of its directly attached substituent. Fluorine is the most electronegative element; it withdraws electron density from its attached carbon more strongly than chlorine does, making that carbon the better electrophile. Therefore the nucleophile preferentially adds to the fluorine-bearing carbon in Step 1.

vocab.electrophilicity: the tendency of an atom to attract and accept electron density from an incoming nucleophile
vocab.electronegativity: a measure of how strongly an atom pulls shared electrons toward itself in a bond

solution_steps.do_this: Use the observed regiochemical outcome — F leaves rather than the better-leaving-group Cl — to deduce which step is rate-determining
solution_steps.why: The rule is: a substituent's effect on reaction rate is only felt if it participates in or before the rate-determining step (the slowest, energy-highest step). If departure of the leaving group were rate-determining (Step 2), the better leaving group (Cl) would leave faster and the product would show Cl substituted. The experiment shows F is substituted, meaning Cl's superior leaving-group ability confers no rate advantage — so leaving-group departure is NOT rate-determining. Therefore Step 1, nucleophile addition, must be rate-determining, and the identity of the leaving group only matters for Step 2 (which is fast and not rate-controlling).

vocab.rate-determining step: the single slowest elementary step in a multi-step mechanism whose activation energy controls the overall reaction rate — analogous to the narrowest point in a pipeline controlling flow
vocab.leaving group ability: how readily a group departs with its bonding electrons; in typical reactions Cl leaves more easily than F, but this advantage is irrelevant if departure occurs after the rate-determining step

solution_steps.do_this: Consolidate the argument: connect Step 1 being rate-determining to both the regiochemistry (attack at C–F) and the kinetic invisibility of leaving-group quality
solution_steps.why: The rule is: the rate law for a multi-step reaction reflects only the species present up to and including the rate-determining step. Because Step 1 (addition to the more electrophilic C–F carbon) is rate-determining, the rate depends on nucleophile concentration and on how electrophilic the attacked carbon is — not on how well the halide can leave. This unified picture explains why a reaction with a 'bad' leaving group (F) can be faster overall than an analogous reaction with a 'good' leaving group (Cl) when the addition step controls the rate.

vocab.regiochemistry: the positional selectivity of a reaction — which of two or more possible sites on a molecule is actually transformed
vocab.rate law: a mathematical expression showing how reaction rate depends on the concentrations of reactants, determined solely by steps up to and including the rate-determining step

question_text_raw:
18.95Compare the indicated bonds (aandb) in the following com-
pound. Bondahas a bond length of 145 pm, while bondbhas a bond
13
length of 135 pm.Suggest a reason for this difference in bond length
for seemingly similar bonds.
bNO2
a
O2N
NO2
solution_text_raw:
18.95.Each of the three nitro groups is conjugated with the aromatic ring, and as a result, each of the C–N bonds has
some double-bond character. The following resonance structure illustrates this for one of the nitro groups:
There are three nitro groups.Two of them areorthoto the very large iodo group.The size of the electron cloud
associated with the iodo group offers steric strain that forces these two nitro groups out of planarity with the ring, in
order to alleviate some of the strain. This results in reduced overlap of theporbitals, and consequently, the resonance
effect is diminished.As such, the C–N bonds of these two nitro groups will have less double-bond character.In
contrast, the nitro group that isparato the iodo group is coplanar with the ring, and its C–N bond has significant
double bond character (see the resonance structure above). This additional C=N character leads to a shorter bond, as is
characteristic of double bonds.

question_analysis.skill_tested: Predict how loss of orbital overlap caused by steric strain reduces resonance delocalization and thereby lengthens a bond
question_analysis.disguise: The problem looks like a simple bond-length comparison, hiding a deeper analysis of how a bulky substituent physically twists groups out of the plane needed for pi overlap
question_analysis.recognition_cue: Two apparently identical C–N bonds in the same molecule have measurably different lengths — one shorter than a pure single bond — which signals that resonance (pi delocalization) is contributing unequally to each bond

prior_knowledge_needed: Resonance delocalization: when a pi system on one group overlaps with adjacent p orbitals, electron density is shared, giving the connecting bond partial double-bond character and shortening it
For p-orbital overlap to occur, the orbitals must be parallel — meaning the two pi systems must be coplanar (lying in the same flat plane)
Steric strain: physical crowding between large groups that forces bonds to rotate away from the ideal geometry in order to reduce repulsion
Bond length correlates with bond order: a bond with more double-bond character is shorter than a pure single bond of the same atom pair
Ortho and para relationships on a benzene ring: ortho substituents are on adjacent carbons (close together spatially), para substituents are directly across the ring (far apart)

solution_steps.do_this: Identify why any C–N bond to a nitro group attached to a benzene ring would be shorter than a pure C–N single bond under ideal conditions
solution_steps.why: The rule is: when a pi-bearing group (the nitro group's N=O system) is conjugated — meaning its p orbitals align parallel with the ring's pi system — electron density delocalizes across the C–N bond, giving it partial double-bond character. This partial double-bond character shortens the bond relative to a pure single bond (~147 pm). Bond 'b', at 135 pm, is close to a C=N double bond length, confirming strong resonance contribution.

vocab.conjugation: the continuous overlap of p orbitals across alternating single and multiple bonds, allowing electrons to spread (delocalize) over several atoms
vocab.resonance contributor: one of several valid electron-dot structures for the same molecule that together describe where electron density actually resides

solution_steps.do_this: Assess the spatial relationship between the bulky iodo substituent and each nitro group to determine which nitro groups are crowded
solution_steps.why: The rule is: substituents at ortho positions are close enough in space that large electron clouds repel each other, generating steric strain that forces rotation around the connecting bond. Two of the three nitro groups sit at ortho positions relative to the large iodine atom. Because iodine carries a very large electron cloud, these two ortho nitro groups are physically pushed out of the plane of the benzene ring to relieve the crowding.

vocab.ortho: the position on a benzene ring immediately adjacent (next-door carbon) to a reference substituent
vocab.steric strain: the energy penalty and resulting geometric distortion that arises when atoms or groups are forced closer together than their sizes comfortably allow

solution_steps.do_this: Determine how being twisted out of the ring's plane destroys the overlap needed for resonance in the ortho nitro groups
solution_steps.why: The rule is: p-orbital overlap — and therefore resonance — requires the interacting orbitals to be parallel. Once a nitro group is rotated out of the ring plane (even partially), its p orbital tilts away from parallel alignment with the ring's pi system. Reduced parallelism means reduced overlap, which means the C–N bond loses its partial double-bond character and reverts toward a longer, pure single bond. Bond 'a' at 145 pm reflects this diminished resonance.

vocab.p-orbital overlap: the sideways (lateral) interaction between two p orbitals on neighboring atoms that creates a pi bond or delocalizes electrons; maximized when the orbitals are parallel, eliminated when they are perpendicular

solution_steps.do_this: Contrast the para nitro group's geometry to explain why it retains full resonance and therefore bond 'b' is shorter
solution_steps.why: The rule is: a substituent at the para position is on the opposite side of the ring from the reference group, far enough away that steric repulsion is negligible. The para nitro group therefore remains coplanar with the benzene ring, its p orbitals stay parallel to the ring's pi system, and full conjugation is maintained. Full resonance gives the C–N bond significant double-bond character, producing the shorter 135 pm length observed for bond 'b'.

vocab.para: the position on a benzene ring directly across from a reference substituent, separated by two carbons on each side
vocab.coplanar: lying in the same flat plane, the geometry required for maximum p-orbital overlap

solution_steps.do_this: Synthesize the argument into a single causal chain: steric strain → rotation → lost overlap → lost double-bond character → longer bond
solution_steps.why: The rule is: bond length is a direct reporter of bond order, and bond order in a conjugated system depends entirely on how well the pi systems overlap. By tracing steric strain at the ortho positions through geometric distortion and diminished overlap all the way to a measurable bond-length increase (~10 pm), one connects molecular architecture to observable physical data — demonstrating that bond 'a' (ortho, twisted, less resonance) is longer and bond 'b' (para, planar, full resonance) is shorter.

vocab.bond order: the effective number of shared electron pairs between two atoms; higher bond order means a shorter, stronger bond

atom of1(pKa ~ 30) to generate anion3. This nitrogen atom will be re-protonated upon workup with water.It might

question_analysis.skill_tested: Construct a stepwise mechanistic pathway for a base-promoted benzyne formation and intramolecular trapping sequence that closes a new aromatic ring
question_analysis.disguise: The problem looks like a simple deprotonation/cyclization, but it requires recognizing that TWO equivalents of strong base serve two distinct roles, that a strained benzyne (highly reactive intermediate) is the key ring-forming species, and that aromaticity is restored through sequential proton transfers
question_analysis.recognition_cue: An aryl bromide adjacent to an acidic C–H or N–H, treated with excess strong base (NaNH2, ≥2 equiv), where the product shows a new ring formed at the position where bromine was — this pattern screams benzyne elimination-addition chemistry

prior_knowledge_needed: Benzyne (aryne) formation: strong base abstracts a proton ortho to a leaving group on a benzene ring, then the leaving group departs to give a strained triple bond in the ring
Intramolecular trapping: a nucleophilic pi system or lone pair within the same molecule can add across the benzyne triple bond to close a new ring

Resonance stabilization of anions: a carbanion or nitrogen anion adjacent to aromatic rings or other pi systems is stabilized by delocalization, which explains why seemingly non-acidic protons can be removed by strong base
pKa hierarchy: NaNH2 (conjugate acid NH3, pKa ~38) can deprotonate N–H bonds (pKa ~30) and arene C–H bonds alpha to electronegative groups
Electrophilic aromatic substitution (EAS): a pi-nucleophile adds to an electrophile attached to or embedded in a benzene ring, then a proton is lost to restore aromaticity

Two-base strategy: the first equivalent of base removes the most acidic proton (N–H) to prevent it from quenching the second base; the second equivalent then performs the elimination to generate benzyne
Rearomatization: after addition to benzyne a cyclohexadienyl anion intermediate loses a proton to regain full aromatic stabilization

solution_steps.do_this: Identify what each equivalent of NaNH2 does and in what order, assigning one mechanistic role per equivalent
solution_steps.why: The rule is: when a substrate contains multiple sites of differing acidity, the strongest acid is deprotonated first. The N–H bond (pKa ~30) is more acidic than an aryl C–H bond, so the first equivalent of base neutralizes the N–H, generating a nitrogen anion that is resonance-stabilized (electron density delocalized across the aromatic system). This step is obligatory — if it were skipped, the N–H proton would simply quench the second equivalent of base before benzyne could form. The second equivalent then abstracts the aryl C–H that is ortho (adjacent on the ring) to the C–Br bond, setting up elimination.

vocab.resonance-stabilized anion: a negatively charged species whose extra electron density is spread across multiple atoms through overlapping pi orbitals, making the anion lower in energy and easier to form
vocab.ortho: the two ring positions immediately adjacent to a reference substituent on a benzene ring

solution_steps.do_this: Draw the elimination step that converts the ortho-deprotonated aryl bromide into a benzyne intermediate, then assess why this strained species is highly reactive toward intramolecular nucleophiles
solution_steps.why: The rule is: when a carbanion and a leaving group occupy adjacent carbons on a benzene ring, 1,2-elimination occurs to give a benzyne — a six-membered ring containing a formal triple bond. Benzyne is extraordinarily reactive because incorporating a triple bond into a six-membered ring forces severe angle strain (bond angles cannot adopt the ideal 180° of a linear alkyne). This strain makes benzyne an excellent electrophile (electron-pair acceptor), eagerly reacting with any nearby nucleophile. In this intramolecular (within the same molecule) setting, the adjacent aromatic ring of the substrate is close enough in space to act as the nucleophile, making cyclization highly favorable over intermolecular side reactions.

vocab.benzyne (aryne): a benzene ring with one formal C–C triple bond embedded in it, formed by loss of H and a leaving group from adjacent carbons; extremely strained and reactive
vocab.intramolecular: a reaction where the nucleophile and electrophile are parts of the same molecule
vocab.angle strain: the extra potential energy that builds up when bond angles are forced away from their ideal values

solution_steps.do_this: Trace the addition of the nearby pi nucleophile across the benzyne triple bond to form the new ring, producing a cyclohexadienyl anion intermediate, then identify the proton-transfer step that regenerates aromaticity
solution_steps.why: The rule is: when a nucleophile adds to one carbon of a benzyne, the other carbon of the former triple bond acquires a negative charge, giving a cyclohexadienyl anion (a six-membered ring that has lost its full aromatic character because one carbon is now sp3). This carbanion is then protonated — either by solvent, by the N–H that was re-protonated in the workup, or by an intramolecular proton transfer — to give an imine (C=N double bond) intermediate. Subsequent loss of a proton from the newly formed ring restores full aromaticity (rearomatization), releasing the thermodynamic driving force that ultimately pulls the entire sequence forward. Workup with water then protonates any remaining anions.

vocab.cyclohexadienyl anion: a six-membered carbocyclic ring with two double bonds and one sp3 carbon bearing a negative charge; the key intermediate in both electrophilic aromatic substitution and benzyne addition reactions
vocab.rearomatization: the loss of a proton from a cyclohexadienyl intermediate to restore the fully conjugated, aromatic six-pi-electron system
vocab.imine: a functional group containing a C=N double bond, analogous to a carbonyl but with nitrogen in place of oxygen

solution_steps.do_this: Classify the overall ring-closure sequence using the two reaction categories it combines, confirming internal consistency of the mechanism
solution_steps.why: The rule is: a benzyne addition sequence is mechanistically equivalent to an elimination-addition pathway — the benzyne forms by elimination (loss of H and Br) and then reacts by addition (nucleophile adds across the triple bond). The subsequent protonation-deprotonation sequence that restores aromaticity in the newly formed ring is mechanistically identical to the proton-transfer steps in electrophilic aromatic substitution (EAS). Recognizing these parallels confirms that each arrow in the mechanism obeys known reaction classes, providing a logical cross-check: if every step matches a known mechanism type, the overall proposal is internally consistent.

vocab.elimination-addition: a two-stage mechanistic pathway in which atoms are first removed from adjacent carbons (elimination) to give a reactive intermediate, which then reacts with a nucleophile (addition)
vocab.electrophilic aromatic substitution (EAS: a reaction in which an electrophile replaces a hydrogen on an aromatic ring, proceeding through a cyclohexadienyl cation intermediate that loses H+ to restore aromaticity

forms between the two carbon atoms indicated below.

784CHAPTER 18
A mechanism for the formation of the minor product is presented below. The alkyne attacks ICl, resulting in the
formation of a new C–I bond, and a vinyl carbocation (see below for a justification for formation of a vinyl carbocation
in this case). This carbocation then serves as an electrophile in an intramolecular electrophilic aromatic substitution.
Theπelectrons from the methoxy-substituted ring attack this carbocation, resulting in the formation of a new C–C
bond, and a resonance-stabilized sigma complex. This intermediate is highly conjugated and has at least seven
reasonable resonance structures in addition to the four shown.Deprotonation of the sigma complex restores
aromaticity, thus yielding the minor product.
A mechanism to account for the formation of the major product is given below. Each step is analogous to the
mechanism presented above.
In Chapter 9, we noted that vinyl carbocations are not very stable, and thus we should consider whether or not our first
intermediate is a reasonable one. In this case, the vinyl carbocation is a reasonable intermediate, because it is stabilized
by the adjacent aromatic ring, as demonstrated by the following resonance structures.
CHAPTER 18785
Alternatively, it would also be plausible to suggest a mechanism involving an iodonium ion, rather than a vinyl
carbocation. In that case, the mechanism for formation of the major product would be as follows:
Formation of the minor product might also occur in this way, via an iodonium ion, rather than a vinyl carbocation.
(b)In the formation of each of the products, we should take into account both electronic and steric considerations. In
the formation of the minor product, the new C–C bond isorthoto the methoxy group. In contrast, the new C–C bond is
parato the methoxy in the formation of the major product. Both of these products are thus consistent with the methoxy
group serving as anortho-paradirector.The predominance of the major product can be explained by the increased
steric accessibility of electrophilic carbocation to the positionparato the methoxy group.

question_analysis.skill_tested: Construct a stepwise mechanism for an intramolecular electrophilic aromatic substitution triggered by electrophilic addition to an alkyne, then use directing effects and sterics to rationalize regioselectivity
question_analysis.disguise: The problem looks like a simple halogenation, but it actually requires recognizing that the electrophilic iodine activates the alkyne toward cyclization onto an electron-rich aromatic ring in the same molecule — combining two reaction types in one mechanism
question_analysis.recognition_cue: A molecule containing both an alkyne and a methoxy-substituted benzene ring reacts with an electrophilic halogen source — this surface pattern signals intramolecular electrophilic aromatic substitution after electrophilic activation of the alkyne

prior_knowledge_needed: Electrophilic addition to alkynes: an electrophile attacks a pi bond of a triple bond to generate a vinyl carbocation (a positively charged carbon that is part of a double bond) or a bridged halonium ion
Electrophilic aromatic substitution (EAS): the pi electrons of an aromatic ring attack an electrophile to form a sigma complex (arenium ion), followed by deprotonation to restore aromaticity
Vinyl carbocation stabilization by an adjacent aromatic ring through resonance delocalization
Ortho/para directors: substituents like methoxy (-OMe) that donate electron density into the ring and preferentially stabilize the sigma complex when the electrophile attacks ortho or para to them
Steric effects in EAS: bulkier electrophiles or more hindered positions disfavor attack at ortho relative to para even when both are electronically activated

solution_steps.do_this: Identify the two reactive sites in the starting material and determine which bond forms first in each pathway
solution_steps.why: The rule is: before drawing any mechanism, an expert maps which functional groups are present and which atom acts as electrophile versus nucleophile. Here the electrophilic iodine attacks the alkyne pi bond first, because triple-bond pi electrons are nucleophilic and ICl provides an electrophilic iodine. This produces either a vinyl carbocation or an iodonium ion (a three-membered ring where iodine bridges two carbons) as the initial intermediate — both are activated electrophiles that can then be attacked by the aromatic ring intramolecularly

vocab.vinyl carbocation: a carbocation in which the positive charge resides on a carbon that is also part of a double bond
vocab.iodonium ion: a bridged intermediate in which a positively charged iodine atom is bonded to two adjacent carbons, analogous to a bromonium ion

solution_steps.do_this: Trace the intramolecular EAS step: decide which carbon of the activated alkyne the aromatic ring attacks, and identify which position on the ring is attacked in each product
solution_steps.why: The rule is: in intramolecular EAS, the ring carbon that attacks the electrophile is determined by the geometry of the tether and by the directing effect of substituents already on the ring. The methoxy group is an ortho/para director, so the sigma complex (arenium ion — the non-aromatic carbocation formed when the ring attacks an electrophile) is most stable when the new bond forms ortho or para to the methoxy group. One product has the new C–C bond forming para to the methoxy group (major) and the other has it forming ortho (minor). Both are mechanistically valid; after sigma-complex formation, deprotonation restores aromaticity to give the cyclized product with iodine also incorporated

vocab.sigma complex: the non-aromatic, positively charged intermediate formed when an electrophile bonds to one carbon of an aromatic ring, breaking aromaticity temporarily
vocab.ortho/para director: a ring substituent that stabilizes the sigma complex most when the incoming electrophile is positioned ortho or para to it, via resonance donation

solution_steps.do_this: Justify why the vinyl carbocation intermediate is reasonable despite general instability of vinyl carbocations
solution_steps.why: The rule is: a carbocation is stabilized whenever adjacent pi systems can delocalize the positive charge through resonance. In this molecule the vinyl carbocation is directly conjugated with the attached aromatic ring, allowing the ring's pi electrons to spread into the empty p orbital. Drawing the resonance structures that extend the positive charge onto the ring carbon demonstrates this extra stabilization — making the intermediate viable despite the general weakness of vinyl cations

vocab.resonance delocalization: the spreading of electron density (or a charge) across multiple atoms through overlapping p orbitals, lowering the overall energy of the species

solution_steps.do_this: Apply both electronic and steric reasoning to explain why the para-attack product forms in roughly three times the amount of the ortho-attack product
solution_steps.why: The rule is: when two positions are both electronically activated by a directing group, the less sterically hindered position reacts faster. Both ortho and para positions are activated by the methoxy group's electron donation, so the electronic preference alone predicts a mixture. However, the ortho position is flanked by the existing substituent on the ring, making it more congested for the incoming electrophilic carbon of the activated alkyne to approach. The para position is more sterically accessible, so the transition state leading to the para sigma complex is lower in energy, giving the para (major) product in a roughly 3:1 ratio

vocab.steric accessibility: how unobstructed a site is to approach by a reacting species; greater bulk around a position raises the activation energy for attack at that position

solution_text_raw:
18.98.The first steps of the mechanism are two successive proton transfers to form the dication2.In each of these
steps, triflic acid is the source of the proton. The amine is protonated first, to give an ammonium ion.If we compare
the pKa values of triflic acid (–14) and a typical ammonium ion (~10), we see that this proton transfer step is effectively
irreversible, thus the irreversible reaction arrow for that step. Then, another proton transfer step generates the dication.
Notice that protonation of the carbonyl group occurs as the second step,afterprotonation of the amine, because a
protonated carbonyl group is a highly acidic species (pKa≈–7), relative to an ammonium ion.
Once the highly electrophilic dication intermediate is formed, an electrophilic aromatic substitution reaction occurs, in
which benzene functions as the nucleophile.The resulting intermediate sigma complex is deprotonated to restore
aromaticity.Under these acidic conditions, the hydroxyl group is protonated, generating an excellent leaving group
(water), which leaves to give a resonance-stabilized benzylic carbocation (resonance structures not shown).This
benzylic carbocation then functions as an electrophile in a second electrophilic aromatic substitution reaction. Finally,
the resulting sigma complex is then deprotonated to restore aromaticity, thereby producing ammonium ion3.
786CHAPTER 18
CHAPTER 18787

question_analysis.skill_tested: Construct a multi-step mechanistic sequence by ordering proton transfers according to pKa logic, then applying electrophilic aromatic substitution twice in succession
question_analysis.disguise: The problem looks like a single reaction, but it is actually a cascade: two acid-base steps to build a dication, followed by two separate electrophilic aromatic substitution events connected by a carbocation intermediate
question_analysis.recognition_cue: An extremely strong acid (pKa = −14) plus a molecule containing both an amine and a carbonyl group reacting with benzene signals: first protonate to build maximum electrophilicity, then use benzene as the pi-nucleophile in EAS

prior_knowledge_needed: pKa as a measure of acid strength: lower pKa means stronger acid and a more thermodynamically favorable proton donation
Proton transfer directionality: a proton transfer is effectively irreversible when the pKa of the donor is much lower than the pKa of the product conjugate acid
Ammonium ion pKa (~10) versus protonated carbonyl pKa (~−7): knowing these values determines the ORDER of protonation

Electrophilic aromatic substitution (EAS) mechanism: an electrophile attacks the pi electrons of benzene to form a sigma complex (arenium ion / Wheland intermediate), which then loses a proton to restore aromaticity
Leaving group activation: a hydroxyl group becomes a far better leaving group once protonated, generating water (neutral, stable molecule) instead of hydroxide
Benzylic carbocation stability: a carbocation adjacent to a benzene ring is stabilized by resonance delocalization into the ring, making it a viable electrophile for a second EAS

solution_steps.do_this: Decide which basic site on compound 1 gets protonated first by comparing the pKa values of the relevant conjugate acids
solution_steps.why: The rule is: proton transfer reaches equilibrium on the side with the weaker acid; triflic acid (pKa −14) protonates the amine nitrogen first because the resulting ammonium ion (conjugate acid pKa ~10) is a far weaker acid than triflic acid — the driving force is enormous, making this step essentially irreversible. Protonation of the carbonyl oxygen is deliberately deferred because the resulting oxocarbenium species (conjugate acid pKa ~−7) is still a strong acid, meaning that equilibrium would lie on the wrong side if attempted before the amine is already protonated.

vocab.conjugate acid: the species formed when a base accepts one proton
vocab.irreversible proton transfer: a proton transfer whose equilibrium lies so far toward products that the reverse reaction is negligible, indicated by a one-way arrow

solution_steps.do_this: Apply a second proton transfer from a second equivalent of triflic acid to the carbonyl oxygen, generating the dication intermediate
solution_steps.why: The rule is: once the amine is already protonated (and thus cannot accept another proton there), the carbonyl oxygen becomes the next available basic site. Protonation converts the carbonyl C=O into a hydroxyl-substituted carbocation center, making the carbon extremely electron-poor — a powerful electrophile. Although the protonated carbonyl is itself acidic (~pKa −7), triflic acid (pKa −14) is still seven pKa units stronger, so this transfer also favors products. The resulting species carries two positive charges (one on nitrogen, one on the former carbonyl carbon), maximizing electrophilicity at the reactive carbon.

vocab.dication: a species carrying two separate positive charges
vocab.electrophile: an electron-deficient species that seeks to accept electrons from a nucleophile

solution_steps.do_this: Execute the first electrophilic aromatic substitution: identify benzene as the nucleophile attacking the most electrophilic carbon of the dication, draw the sigma complex, then deprotonate to restore aromaticity
solution_steps.why: The rule is: in EAS, the pi electrons (delocalized electron cloud) of benzene attack an electrophile to form a sigma complex (arenium ion) in which one ring carbon becomes sp3 and aromaticity is temporarily lost; deprotonation of that sp3 carbon by any base present restores the aromatic system and releases a proton. The dication's electron-starved carbon is the site of attack because it holds both a formal positive charge and is flanked by two electron-withdrawing groups.

vocab.sigma complex: the non-aromatic carbocation intermediate in EAS, also called a Wheland intermediate or arenium ion, formed when benzene's pi electrons bond to the electrophile
vocab.aromaticity: the special stability of a cyclic, planar, fully conjugated ring with 4n+2 pi electrons

solution_steps.do_this: Activate the hydroxyl group as a leaving group by protonating it, then allow it to depart to generate a resonance-stabilized benzylic carbocation
solution_steps.why: The rule is: hydroxide (OH−) is a poor leaving group because it is strongly basic, but water (H2O) is an excellent leaving group because it is neutral and stable; protonation converts OH into OH2+, which departs as water. The resulting carbocation is benzylic — positioned at a carbon directly attached to the newly installed phenyl ring — so the positive charge delocalizes into both the ring and the nitrogen lone pair through resonance, conferring significant stability and making this intermediate viable despite harsh conditions.

vocab.leaving group: the fragment that departs with both electrons from a bond during a substitution or elimination reaction
vocab.benzylic carbocation: a carbocation whose positive charge resides on a carbon directly attached to an aromatic ring, stabilized by resonance

solution_steps.do_this: Execute the second electrophilic aromatic substitution: use the benzylic carbocation as the electrophile toward the same benzene molecule (intramolecular) or the remaining aromatic pi system, form the second sigma complex, then deprotonate to restore aromaticity and arrive at the final ammonium product
solution_steps.why: The rule is: any carbocation that is sufficiently stable and geometrically accessible can act as the electrophile in a second EAS cycle, repeating the same attack-then-deprotonation sequence. After this second ring closure and deprotonation, the nitrogen retains its protonated ammonium character (the original irreversible protonation from step 1 is preserved), yielding the observed ammonium ion product 3. Each EAS step is confirmed by the requirement to restore aromaticity through loss of a proton.

vocab.intramolecular: occurring within the same molecule rather than between two separate molecules
vocab.ammonium ion: a nitrogen atom bearing four bonds and a formal positive charge, the protonated form of an amine

18.99.
(a)A reasonable mechanism for the conversion of1to2is shown. In the first step,n-butyllithium functions as a base
and deprotonates the amine to produce a resonance-stabilized anion (resonance structures not shown).Boron
trichloride serves as a Lewis acid in the next step, accepting an electron pair from the nucleophilic nitrogen atom,
giving the Lewis acid – Lewis base complex. Subsequent loss of a chloride ion gives the compound2.
A mechanism for the final step is shown below.Overall, this step constitutes two subsequent electrophilic aromatic
substitution reactions, resulting in the formation of two new B-C bonds.Addition of aluminum trichloride, a Lewis
acid, serves to enhance the electrophilicity of the boron by forming a complex with one of the chlorine atoms. The
boron is then attacked by one of the pendant arenes, with subsequent loss of AlCl4¯ and formation of a resonance-
stabilized sigma complex (several, but not all, of the many resonance structures are shown).Deprotonation with
AlCl4¯ restores aromaticity, giving a neutral intermediate.An identical sequence of mechanistic steps (complexation
with AlCl3, attack of the arene, loss of AlCl4¯ and deprotonation) results in the formation of the second B-C bond, thus
producing compound3.
788CHAPTER 18
(b)Each of the four peripheral rings is clearly aromatic.
2
For the two central rings, the boron and nitrogen are both expected to besp-hybridized, with boron having an emptyp-
orbital and nitrogen holding its lone pair in ap-orbital.As such, each central ring exhibits a continuous system of
overlappingporbitals with a total of 6electrons, thus fulfilling the criteria for aromaticity:
CHAPTER 18789
Therefore, all six rings are aromatic, providing for one extended aromatic system that is electronically similar to the
following polycyclic aromatic compound:
(c)In part b, we determined that we expect both central rings in3to be aromatic, and as such, we would expect the
entire molecule to be planar.The observation that the central two rings are twisted (non-planar) indicates that the
central rings actually have limited aromatic character, despite the fact that they have the right number ofπelectrons to
be aromatic. There must be something causing the rings to twist out of planarity.This can be explained by the steric
repulsion between the indicated hydrogen atoms shown in the structures below. A slightly twisted structure avoids this
repulsive interaction.

conformation, as seen in the BioLinks application at the end of section 17.5 (The Development of Non-Sedating
Antihistamines).

question_analysis.skill_tested: Trace a multi-step synthesis mechanism involving lithiation, Lewis acid-base complexation, and double electrophilic aromatic substitution, then evaluate aromaticity and planarity of the product
question_analysis.disguise: The problem is wrapped in materials-science language about BN analogues and electronic devices, which obscures that the core tasks are standard mechanistic reasoning, Hückel aromaticity counting, and steric analysis
question_analysis.recognition_cue: A secondary amine treated sequentially with n-BuLi then BCl3, followed by AlCl3 with pendant arene groups — this pattern signals deprotonation-then-boron-attachment followed by Lewis-acid-catalyzed electrophilic cyclization onto arene rings

prior_knowledge_needed: n-BuLi acts as a very strong base capable of deprotonating N-H bonds to form nitrogen anions
BCl3 is a Lewis acid that accepts electron pairs at boron; nitrogen anions are Lewis bases that donate to boron

Electrophilic aromatic substitution (EAS): an electron-deficient species attacks an electron-rich aromatic ring, forming a carbocation intermediate called a sigma complex (arenium ion), which then loses a proton to restore aromaticity
Lewis acids like AlCl3 can activate electrophiles by coordinating to leaving groups, making the electrophilic center more positive
Hückel aromaticity criteria: a ring must be cyclic, planar, fully conjugated (continuous p-orbital overlap), and contain 4n+2 pi electrons (n = 0, 1, 2...)
Boron in a conjugated ring is sp2-hybridized with an empty p-orbital that can participate in pi systems; nitrogen with a lone pair in a p-orbital donates into the pi system
Steric repulsion between hydrogen atoms on adjacent rings can force a molecule out of planarity even when electronic factors favor planarity

solution_steps.do_this: Work out the mechanism for converting compound 1 into the boron-containing intermediate (compound 2) in two sub-steps: first, identify what n-BuLi does to the amine; second, identify what BCl3 does to the resulting anion
solution_steps.why: The rule is: n-BuLi is one of the strongest bases in organic chemistry and will deprotonate any N-H bond it encounters, generating a nitrogen anion (an anion formed by removing a proton from nitrogen) that is resonance-stabilized across the adjacent aromatic system. Then the rule is: a Lewis acid (an electron-pair acceptor) always coordinates to the nearest available electron-pair donor — here the nitrogen anion donates into the empty orbital on boron in BCl3, forming a Lewis acid-Lewis base complex (a pair of molecules joined by a coordinate bond where one supplies both electrons). Loss of one chloride ion then gives a neutral boron-nitrogen compound where boron still bears two remaining chlorines, which are the handles needed for the next cyclization step.

vocab.nitrogen anion: a nitrogen atom that carries a formal negative charge after losing its N-H proton
vocab.Lewis acid-base complex: a species formed when an electron-pair donor (Lewis base) bonds to an electron-pair acceptor (Lewis acid) by donating both electrons of the new bond
vocab.resonance-stabilized anion: a negatively charged species whose charge is spread over multiple atoms through overlapping p-orbitals, making it more stable than a localized anion

solution_steps.do_this: Construct the mechanism for the AlCl3-promoted double ring-closure from the intermediate to compound 3, recognizing that two sequential EAS events must occur
solution_steps.why: The rule is: AlCl3 (a Lewis acid) coordinates to a chlorine attached to an electrophilic boron, withdrawing electron density and making boron even more electron-deficient (electrophilic). This electron-deficient boron is then attacked by a nearby pendant arene ring (an aromatic ring attached to the nitrogen-boron framework but not yet cyclized), acting as a nucleophile in EAS. The attack generates a sigma complex (arenium ion — an intermediate in EAS where one carbon of the ring has become sp3-hybridized after accepting the electrophile, carrying a positive charge delocalized over the ring). Loss of the AlCl4- counterion and deprotonation restores aromaticity, closing the first new B-C bond. The exact same sequence — AlCl3 activation, arene attack, sigma complex formation, deprotonation — then repeats with the second pendant arene to close the second ring and give the fully cyclized product 3.

vocab.electrophilic aromatic substitution (EAS: a reaction where an electron-rich aromatic ring attacks an electrophile, temporarily losing aromaticity to form a sigma complex, then regains aromaticity by expelling a proton
vocab.sigma complex (arenium ion): the carbocation intermediate in EAS where one ring carbon has bonded to the incoming electrophile and become sp3, disrupting the aromatic pi system temporarily
vocab.pendant arene: an aromatic ring that is attached to the main framework but has not yet formed the bond needed to close a new ring

solution_steps.do_this: Evaluate whether all six rings in compound 3 satisfy the criteria for aromaticity by assessing the hybridization and orbital contributions of boron and nitrogen in the two central rings
solution_steps.why: The rule is: Hückel aromaticity requires every atom in the ring to contribute a p-orbital perpendicular to the ring plane, and the total pi electron count must satisfy 4n+2. For the two central rings containing boron and nitrogen: boron is sp2-hybridized (three bonds, flat geometry) with an empty p-orbital perpendicular to the ring — this empty orbital is part of the conjugated system even though it holds no electrons. Nitrogen is also sp2-hybridized and places its lone pair into a p-orbital perpendicular to the ring, contributing two electrons to the pi count. Each central ring therefore has a continuous loop of overlapping p-orbitals and six pi electrons (satisfying 4n+2 with n=1), fulfilling all aromaticity criteria. The four outer rings are straightforward benzene-like aromatic rings. Therefore all six rings are aromatic and together form one extended aromatic pi system electronically comparable to a larger all-carbon polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon.

vocab.sp2-hybridized: an atom that uses three hybrid orbitals to form three sigma bonds arranged in a flat triangle, leaving one pure p-orbital perpendicular to that plane available for pi bonding
vocab.Hückel aromaticity: the condition met when a cyclic, planar, fully conjugated ring contains 4n+2 pi electrons (where n is any non-negative integer), giving the ring special stability
vocab.extended aromatic system: a network of multiple fused aromatic rings sharing pi electrons across the entire framework

solution_steps.do_this: Rationalize why the central two rings are observed to be slightly twisted (nonplanar) despite the electronic prediction of full planarity and aromaticity
solution_steps.why: The rule is: steric repulsion (the physical crowding that occurs when two atoms are forced into close proximity) always competes against electronic factors that favor planarity. Even though aromaticity strongly prefers planarity, the hydrogen atoms on the inner edges of adjacent outer rings point toward each other across the central bond region. Forcing the molecule to be completely flat would push these hydrogens uncomfortably close together, raising the energy through steric strain. The molecule therefore adopts a slightly twisted geometry as a compromise: it sacrifices a small amount of aromatic stabilization (by reducing p-orbital overlap in the central rings) in exchange for relieving the steric clash. This is analogous to the well-known behavior of biphenyl-type systems where two rings rotate away from coplanarity due to ortho hydrogen repulsion.

vocab.steric repulsion: the increase in energy that results when two atoms that are not bonded to each other are forced closer together than the sum of their van der Waals radii allows comfortably
vocab.nonplanar conformation: a three-dimensional arrangement of a molecule in which not all atoms lie in the same plane, usually resulting from torsional rotation about a bond
vocab.torsional strain: the extra energy in a molecule caused by eclipsing or steric interactions as substituents are forced into unfavorable dihedral angle relationships
